topics.title: Familiarization of network components - Hub, Switch, Bridge, Router, Access Point, Network cables, Colour coding, Crimping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITL407 - COMPUTER NETWORKS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Familiarization of network components - Hub, Switch, Bridge, Router, Access Point, Network cables, Colour coding, Crimping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Computer Network?
A **Computer Network** is a digital telecommunications system that allows computing devices to exchange data and share resources. To build even the simplest network, we need a set of physical and logical devices — known as **Network Components** — that act as the building blocks of connectivity.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Network components are the hardware devices (active and passive) and media that collectively enable data communication, signal regeneration, traffic segmentation, and inter-network routing in a Local Area Network (LAN) / Wide Area Network (WAN) environment.*

### 1.2 The Five Core Active Components

| Component | One-Line Definition | Layer of Operation |
|---|---|---|
| **Hub** | A multiport repeater that broadcasts incoming electrical signals to all ports. | **OSI Layer 1 – Physical** |
| **Bridge** | A Layer 2 device that connects two LAN segments and filters traffic using MAC addresses. | **OSI Layer 2 – Data Link** |
| **Switch** | A multiport bridge that intelligently forwards frames only to the intended destination port. | **OSI Layer 2 – Data Link** |
| **Router** | A Layer 3 device that routes packets between different IP networks. | **OSI Layer 3 – Network** |
| **Access Point (AP)** | A device that allows Wi-Fi clients to connect to a wired Ethernet network. | **OSI Layer 2 – Data Link** |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

- **Hub** → *A public announcement speaker.* When one person speaks, everyone in the room hears it, even if the message is meant for only one person. It does not understand "who should hear this."
- **Bridge** → *A traffic policeman standing between two narrow streets.* He decides which cars (frames) can cross to the other side based on destination, reducing congestion.
- **Switch** → *A modern office receptionist with a contact directory.* When an employee says "connect me to Mr. X," the receptionist looks up Mr. X's desk number and connects the call privately — no one else hears.
- **Router** → *A Google Maps navigator for the internet.* It looks at the destination IP address and decides the best "road" (path) to deliver your data across cities (networks).
- **Access Point** → *A wireless radio tower.* It converts wired Ethernet signals into radio waves (and vice versa) so laptops and phones can join the network without cables.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Viva:** A *Hub* is **dumb** (broadcasts), a *Switch* is **smart** (learns MAC addresses), and a *Router* is **smarter still** (understands IP subnets). Do NOT confuse a Switch with a Router — this is a classic viva question.

### 1.4 Passive Components — Network Cables
A **Network Cable** is the physical medium that carries electromagnetic signals between devices.

- **Twisted Pair Cable (Copper):** Two insulated copper wires twisted together to cancel out electromagnetic interference (EMI). Used for Ethernet LANs.
- **Coaxial Cable:** A central copper conductor surrounded by insulation, a metallic shield, and an outer jacket. Used in older Ethernet and cable TV.
- **Fiber Optic Cable:** A glass or plastic strand that carries data as pulses of light. Used for long-distance, high-speed backbones.

> [!NOTE]
> **Crimping** is the act of mechanically pressing an **RJ-45 connector** onto the end of a twisted-pair cable using a special tool called a **Crimping Tool**, creating a permanent electrical contact between the copper conductors and the connector pins.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Layer-Wise Functional Breakdown

#### A. The HUB — Physical Layer Repeater
A hub has no intelligence. It simply receives an incoming electrical signal on one port and regenerates (repeats) it out of **all other ports**.

**Why it exists:** Cheap way to connect many devices in a star topology. Operates on CSMA/CD, meaning only one device can successfully transmit at a time — if two transmit simultaneously, a **collision** occurs.

**Collision Domain:** A hub places ALL connected devices in a **single collision domain**. The more devices, the more collisions, the slower the network becomes.

**Why we avoid hubs today:** Wasted bandwidth, security risks (everyone sees all traffic), and no traffic isolation.

#### B. The BRIDGE — Two-Port Traffic Filter
Bridges were the answer to the hub's "everyone hears everything" problem. They work at the Data Link Layer and read the **MAC (Media Access Control) address** from each incoming frame.

**Operation Logic:**
1. Bridge maintains a **MAC address table** (learns which MAC is on which side/port).
2. If the destination MAC is on the **same side** as the source → discard the frame (filtered).
3. If the destination MAC is on the **other side** → forward the frame.
4. If the destination MAC is **unknown** → flood to the other side.

Bridges are now obsolete; their function has been absorbed into switches.

#### C. The SWITCH — Multiport Intelligent Bridge
A switch is essentially a multiport bridge with hardware-accelerated forwarding.

**How it works (The "Learn and Forward" Model):**
1. **Learning:** Switch reads the source MAC of each incoming frame and records it in its **CAM (Content Addressable Memory) table** mapped to the arrival port.
2. **Forwarding:** Switch reads the destination MAC and looks it up in the CAM table. If found, it forwards the frame out **only that specific port** (this is called **unicast filtering**).
3. **Flooding:** If the destination MAC is not in the table (e.g., a brand-new device), the switch floods the frame out all ports except the incoming one.
4. **Aging:** Entries in the CAM table are deleted after a timeout (typically 5 minutes) to keep the table clean.

**Collision Domain:** Each port on a switch is its **own collision domain** (micro-segmentation), eliminating collisions in full-duplex mode.

#### D. The ROUTER — Inter-Network Gateway
A router connects two or more **different IP networks** and forwards packets between them based on IP addresses.

**How it works:**
1. Each router interface belongs to a different IP subnet.
2. Router maintains a **routing table** with destination networks and next-hop information.
3. When a packet arrives, the router decrements the **TTL (Time To Live)**, recalculates the IP header checksum, and consults its routing table.
4. If no route exists, the packet is **dropped** and an **ICMP "Destination Unreachable"** message is sent back to the source.

**Broadcast Domain:** A router breaks up broadcast domains — broadcasts do not cross routers. This is why routers are essential in large enterprise networks.

#### E. The ACCESS POINT (AP) — Wireless Gateway
An AP acts as a **bridge between the wireless (802.11) and wired (802.3) worlds**.

- Operates on radio frequencies (2.4 GHz, 5 GHz, 6 GHz).
- Functions essentially like a wireless switch — each associated client gets its own logical connection.
- Modern APs (like Wi-Fi 6/6E) support MU-MIMO, OFDMA, and beamforming.

### 2.2 Twisted Pair Cable Categories — Cheat Sheet

| Category | Max Data Rate | Max Bandwidth | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cat 5** | 100 Mbps | 100 MHz | Legacy Fast Ethernet |
| **Cat 5e** | 1 Gbps | 100 MHz | Modern Gigabit LAN |
| **Cat 6** | 1 Gbps (10 Gbps up to 55 m) | 250 MHz | Office networks |
| **Cat 6a** | 10 Gbps | 500 MHz | Data centers |
| **Cat 7** | 10 Gbps | 600 MHz | Shielded, high-EMI areas |
| **Cat 8** | 25/40 Gbps | 2000 MHz | Server-to-switch in racks |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **T568A vs T568B:** Both are valid pinout standards. A **straight-through cable** uses the same standard on both ends (e.g., T568B–T568B) and connects a PC to a Switch. A **crossover cable** uses T568A on one end and T568B on the other and is used to connect similar devices (Switch-to-Switch, PC-to-PC).

### 2.3 Cable Color Coding (T568A and T568B Standards)

The **TIA-EIA 568** standard defines the wire color order. Each RJ-45 connector has **8 pins**, numbered **1 to 8**, with the clip facing away from you and pins facing up.

**T568B Standard (Most widely used in India and corporate environments):**

| Pin | Wire Color | Signal (10/100 BASE-T) | Signal (1000 BASE-T) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | White-Orange | TX+ | BI_DA+ |
| 2 | Orange | TX- | BI_DA- |
| 3 | White-Green | RX+ | BI_DB+ |
| 4 | Blue | Unused | BI_DC+ |
| 5 | White-Blue | Unused | BI_DC- |
| 6 | Green | RX- | BI_DB- |
| 7 | White-Brown | Unused | BI_DD+ |
| 8 | Brown | Unused | BI_DD- |

**T568A Standard (Required by US Federal Government, common in residential):**

| Pin | Wire Color | Signal (10/100 BASE-T) | Signal (1000 BASE-T) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | White-Green | RX+ | BI_DB+ |
| 2 | Green | RX- | BI_DB- |
| 3 | White-Orange | TX+ | BI_DA+ |
| 4 | Blue | Unused | BI_DC+ |
| 5 | White-Blue | Unused | BI_DC- |
| 6 | Orange | TX- | BI_DA- |
| 7 | White-Brown | Unused | BI_DD+ |
| 8 | Brown | Unused | BI_DD- |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick for T568B:** *"Orange is for Output (TX), Green is for Grab-input (RX)."* Orange pair = transmit, Green pair = receive.

### 2.4 The Crimping Process — Tooling Required
The following tools and materials are required to make a working Ethernet cable:

- **UTP Cable (Cat 5e / Cat 6)** — cut to required length.
- **RJ-45 Connectors** (8P8C — 8 Position, 8 Contact) — one per cable end.
- **Crimping Tool** — has cutting, stripping, and crimping dies.
- **Cable Tester** — verifies continuity, short, open, and crossed pairs.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Exam Mistake:** Students often say "8P8C" is the same as "RJ-45." Technically, RJ-45 is the *registered jack* standard, while 8P8C describes the connector's physical geometry. In industry, the terms are used interchangeably. For KTU exams, you may use both — examiners accept "RJ-45 connector."

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Procedural Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Step-by-Step Crimping Procedure (The "Cut, Strip, Arrange, Insert, Crimp, Test" Method)

The complete crimping workflow is presented as a six-stage analytical pipeline. Each stage is critical — skipping any one leads to a non-functional cable.

**Stage 1 — Measure and Cut the Cable**
1. Determine the required cable length (always add **~30 cm of slack** for routing around corners and patch panels).
2. Cut the UTP cable cleanly using the cutting blade of the crimping tool. A jagged cut makes stripping harder.

**Stage 2 — Strip the Outer Jacket**
1. Insert the cable end into the **stripping notch** of the crimping tool. The notch is sized to cut through the outer jacket only, not the inner twisted pairs.
2. Rotate the tool **1.5 to 2 full rotations** around the cable.
3. Pull the cut jacket off — this should expose **approximately 2.5 cm (1 inch)** of the 4 twisted pairs.
4. **Caution:** If the tool cuts too deep and nicks the insulation of the inner wires, cut off the damaged section and start over. A nicked wire causes intermittent failures that are extremely hard to diagnose.

**Stage 3 — Untwist and Arrange the Pairs**
1. Untwist each of the 4 pairs as little as possible. **KTU best practice:** Keep untwisted length under **12.5 mm (0.5 inch)**. Excessive untwisting degrades signal quality and prevents Gigabit operation.
2. Flatten the wires in the order required by your chosen standard (T568A or T568B).
3. Use your fingers to press the wires flat and parallel in a single row. Trim the ends with the cutting blade so all 8 wires are **flush and straight** at the cut line.

**Stage 4 — Insert into the RJ-45 Connector**
1. Hold the RJ-45 connector with the **clip facing down** and the **pin contacts facing up**.
2. Looking into the connector end, pin 1 is on the **left**.
3. Slide the arranged wires into the connector, pushing firmly until:
   - All 8 wires reach the end of the connector channels.
   - The outer jacket enters at least **6 mm (0.25 inch)** into the connector so the crimp secures the cable, not just the wires.
4. Visually inspect the connector end — each of the 8 gold contacts should be aligned with a colored wire.

**Stage 5 — Crimp the Connector**
1. Insert the connector (with cable) into the **crimp slot** of the crimping tool (the slot closest to the handle, sized for 8P8C).
2. Squeeze the handles **firmly and completely** until the ratchet releases. A partial crimp results in poor contact.
3. The crimping action pushes the 8 gold contacts down into the wire insulation, creating a gas-tight electrical connection, and simultaneously squeezes the connector body to grip the cable jacket.

**Stage 6 — Test the Cable**
1. Plug both ends into a **cable tester** (a small device with a master and remote unit).
2. Power on the tester. The LED sequence should light up **1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 5 → 6 → 7 → 8** in sync on both units.
3. The following test results indicate the cable condition:

| LED Result on Tester | Meaning | Action |
|---|---|---|
| All 8 LEDs light in sequence on both units | **PASS** — All pairs continuous, correct order | Cable is good |
| A pair is missing on the remote unit | **OPEN** — Broken wire in that pair | Re-crimp or re-cut |
| Two pins light up on the same unit but a different pair on the remote | **SHORT** — Two wires are touching | Re-strip and re-crimp |
| Pin 1 lights as Pin 3 on the remote, etc. | **CROSSED / MIS-WIRE** — Wrong color order | Cut off and re-terminate |
| A pair splits across pins (e.g., 1, 3, 4, 6) | **SPLIT PAIR** — Subtle wiring error, may pass continuity but fail Gigabit | Re-arrange the pairs |

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Network Components

| Parameter | Hub | Bridge | Switch | Router | Access Point |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **OSI Layer** | 1 (Physical) | 2 (Data Link) | 2 (Data Link) | 3 (Network) | 2 (Data Link) |
| **Addressing Used** | None (electrical signal) | MAC | MAC | IP | MAC |
| **Forwarding Method** | Broadcast to all | Filter & forward (selective) | Filter & forward (selective) | Route based on IP table | Bridge Wi-Fi ↔ Ethernet |
| **Collision Domain** | Single (all ports) | 2 (one per side) | Per port (micro-segments) | Per port | Per wireless client (logical) |
| **Broadcast Domain** | Single | Single | Single | Per interface | Single (typically) |
| **Number of Ports** | 4, 8, 16, 24 | 2 (typically) | 8, 16, 24, 48 | 2–8 (typical) | Many (clients) |
| **Intelligence** | None | Low | High (CAM table) | Very high (Routing table, ACLs, NAT) | High (WPA2/3, SSID, channels) |
| **Typical Use Case** | Obsolete | Obsolete (replaced by switches) | LAN connectivity within an office | Inter-network, Internet gateway, firewall | Wireless LAN access in offices, homes, cafes |
| **Speed Limitation** | Shared bandwidth (e.g., 100 Mbps ÷ N users) | Shared per segment | Dedicated per port (full duplex) | WAN-speed dependent | Wi-Fi standard dependent (e.g., 1200 Mbps on Wi-Fi 5) |
| **Example Device** | Old 8-port 10BASE-T hub | Old 2-port MAC bridge | Cisco Catalyst 2960 | Cisco ISR 4331, home Wi-Fi router part | Ubiquiti U6, TP-Link EAP |

### 3.3 Speed, Bandwidth, and Throughput Derivations

For Cat 5e / Cat 6 cables, the relationship between **bandwidth** and **maximum data rate** follows **Nyquist's theorem** for a noiseless channel and **Shannon's theorem** for noisy channels.

$$
C = 2 \cdot B \cdot \log_2(M) \quad \text{(Nyquist, noiseless)}
$$

$$
C = B \cdot \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \quad \text{(Shannon, noisy)}
$$

Where:
- $C$ = Channel capacity in **bits per second (bps)**
- $B$ = Bandwidth in **Hz**
- $M$ = Number of signal levels
- $S/N$ = Signal-to-Noise ratio (linear, not in dB)

**Numerical example for Cat 6 (Practical Gigabit Ethernet):**
Cat 6 has $B = 250 \text{ MHz}$. For Gigabit Ethernet (1 Gbps) using 4-level PAM encoding ($M = 4$):

$$
C = 2 \cdot 250 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_2(4)
$$

$$
C = 2 \cdot 250 \times 10^{6} \cdot 2 = 1 \times 10^{9} \text{ bps} = 1 \text{ Gbps}
$$

This explains why Cat 6 supports 1 Gbps comfortably on all 4 pairs simultaneously, and up to 10 Gbps for runs under 55 meters.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A higher category cable has more **twists per inch** and often a **spline** (plastic cross divider inside the cable). The spline physically separates the four pairs to reduce **crosstalk (NEXT, FEXT)** — a phenomenon where the signal in one pair induces an unwanted signal in an adjacent pair.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Network Component Hierarchy (OSI Layer Mapping)

This block-level architecture flow shows how each network component sits at a specific OSI layer, and how data flows from a wireless client all the way to a remote server.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph WL["WIRELESS CLIENTS"]
        client1["Laptop WiFi Device"]
        client2["Smartphone WiFi Device"]
    end

    subgraph AP_LAYER["LAYER 2 WIRELESS"]
        apNode["ACCESS POINT AP\nOSI Layer 2\nTranslates 802.11 to 802.3"]
    end

    subgraph SW_LAYER["LAYER 2 SWITCHING"]
        swNode["ETHERNET SWITCH\nOSI Layer 2\nCAM table based forwarding\nOne collision domain per port"]
    end

    subgraph RT_LAYER["LAYER 3 ROUTING"]
        rtNode["ROUTER\nOSI Layer 3\nRouting table based forwarding\nBreaks broadcast domains"]
    end

    subgraph WAN_DEST["REMOTE NETWORK"]
        srv1["Server in another office"]
        cloud1["Internet Cloud"]
    end

    client1 --> apNode
    client2 --> apNode
    apNode --> swNode
    swNode --> rtNode
    rtNode --> srv1
    rtNode --> cloud1
```

### 4.2 Comparing Collision and Broadcast Domains (Hub vs Switch vs Router)

This diagram makes the domain concept visually obvious — notice how a hub merges everything into one big domain, a switch splits the collision domain per port, and a router breaks the broadcast domain entirely.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HUB_COLLISION["HUB - Single Collision and Broadcast Domain"]
        h1["PC1"] --- hub1["HUB"]
        h2["PC2"] --- hub1
        h3["PC3"] --- hub1
    end

    subgraph SW_COLLISION["SWITCH - Separate Collision Domains per Port - Single Broadcast Domain"]
        s1["PC1"] --- sw1["SWITCH"]
        s2["PC2"] --- sw1
        s3["PC3"] --- sw1
    end

    subgraph RT_COLLISION["ROUTER - Separate Collision AND Broadcast Domains per Interface"]
        r1["LAN 1 PC"] --- rt1["ROUTER"]
        r2["LAN 2 PC"] --- rt1
    end
```

### 4.3 RJ-45 Connector Pinout Schematic (T568B Front View)

This diagram shows the wire order when looking at the **contact side** of the RJ-45 connector with the **locking tab facing down** (this is how you look at the connector end before inserting it onto a cable).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph RJ45["RJ45 CONNECTOR - FRONT VIEW - LOCK TAB DOWN"]
        p1["PIN 1\nWHITE ORANGE"]
        p2["PIN 2\nORANGE"]
        p3["PIN 3\nWHITE GREEN"]
        p4["PIN 4\nBLUE"]
        p5["PIN 5\nWHITE BLUE"]
        p6["PIN 6\nGREEN"]
        p7["PIN 7\nWHITE BROWN"]
        p8["PIN 8\nBROWN"]
    end
    p1 --- p8
```

### 4.4 Cable Type Selection Flow (Decision Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start(["Need a network cable"]) --> dist{"What distance?"}
    dist -- "Less than 100 m - LAN" --> medium{"Interference present?"}
    dist -- "More than 100 m - Backbone" --> fiber["Use FIBER OPTIC\nSMF for long haul\nMMF for building backbone"]
    medium -- "No - clean office" --> utp["Use UTP\nCat 5e for 1 Gbps\nCat 6 for 1 to 10 Gbps"]
    medium -- "Yes - factory or near motors" --> stp["Use STP or FTP\nShielded twisted pair\nCat 6a or Cat 7"]
```

### 4.5 Cable Crimping Workflow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a1["STEP 1\nMeasure and Cut Cable"] --> a2["STEP 2\nStrip Outer Jacket 2.5 cm"]
    a2 --> a3["STEP 3\nUntwist and Arrange Pairs\nUse T568A or T568B"]
    a3 --> a4["STEP 4\nInsert into RJ45 Connector\nClip Down - Pin 1 on Left"]
    a4 --> a5["STEP 5\nCrimp Using Crimping Tool\nFull Ratchet Compression"]
    a5 --> a6["STEP 6\nTest With Cable Tester\nVerify 1 to 8 Sequence"]
    a6 --> a7{"All 8 LEDs Match?"}
    a7 -- "YES" --> a8["CABLE READY FOR USE"]
    a7 -- "NO" --> a9["Diagnose Open Short or Crossed Pair"]
    a9 --> a3
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] Differentiate between a Hub and a Switch in terms of OSI layer, collision domain, and forwarding mechanism.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Hub | Switch |
|---|---|---|
| **OSI Layer** | Layer 1 (Physical) | Layer 2 (Data Link) |
| **Collision Domain** | Single — all ports share one | Separate — one per port (micro-segments) |
| **Forwarding Mechanism** | Broadcasts incoming signal to all ports | Selectively forwards based on destination MAC address (unicast filtering) using the CAM table |
| **Intelligence** | None | Learns MAC-to-port mappings dynamically |

A hub simply repeats the electrical signal out of every port, wasting bandwidth and creating collision risks. A switch reads the destination MAC address of each frame and forwards it only to the correct port, enabling full-duplex, collision-free communication. *(3 marks)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] What is the T568B color coding standard? List the 8 wire colors in order from pin 1 to pin 8.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **T568B** standard is the most widely used wiring scheme defined by the **TIA-EIA 568** specification for RJ-45 terminations in 8-position, 8-contact (8P8C) connectors.

| Pin | Wire Color | Function (10/100 Mbps) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | White-Orange | Transmit + (TX+) |
| 2 | Orange | Transmit − (TX−) |
| 3 | White-Green | Receive + (RX+) |
| 4 | Blue | Unused (Power over Ethernet +) |
| 5 | White-Blue | Unused (Power over Ethernet −) |
| 6 | Green | Receive − (RX−) |
| 7 | White-Brown | Unused (PoE +) |
| 8 | Brown | Unused (PoE −) |

> In **1000BASE-T (Gigabit)**, all four pairs are used for simultaneous bidirectional data transmission. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

**Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Explain the functions of a **Router** and an **Access Point** in a computer network. Compare them based on OSI layer, addressing used, and typical use case. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **step-by-step procedure for crimping an RJ-45 connector onto a UTP cable** using the T568B standard. List the tools required and explain how to verify the finished cable. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Router vs Access Point (7 marks):**

A **Router** is a Layer 3 (Network Layer) device that connects two or more different IP networks. It maintains a **routing table** containing destination network prefixes and next-hop information. When a packet arrives, the router:
1. Examines the destination IP address,
2. Consults the routing table (using longest-prefix match),
3. Decrements the **TTL (Time-To-Live)** field to prevent infinite loops,
4. Recalculates the IP header checksum,
5. Forwards the packet out the appropriate interface.

Routers **break up broadcast domains** and are the primary devices used to connect LANs to WANs and the Internet. They also commonly implement **NAT (Network Address Translation)**, **DHCP**, and **firewall/ACL** functions. *(3 marks)*

An **Access Point (AP)** is a Layer 2 (Data Link Layer) device that bridges **IEEE 802.11 (Wi-Fi)** wireless clients to a wired **IEEE 802.3 (Ethernet)** network. It functions essentially like a wireless switch — each associated client gets a dedicated logical connection, and frames are forwarded based on MAC addresses. Modern APs operate on 2.4 GHz, 5 GHz, and 6 GHz bands and support security protocols like **WPA2** and **WPA3**. *(2 marks)*

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Router | Access Point |
|---|---|---|
| OSI Layer | 3 (Network) | 2 (Data Link) |
| Addressing | IP address | MAC address |
| Function | Routes packets between networks | Bridges wireless to wired LAN |
| Breaks Broadcast Domain | Yes | No |

---

**(b) Crimping Procedure (7 marks):**

**Tools and Materials Required (1 mark):**
- UTP Cable (Cat 5e / Cat 6)
- 2 × RJ-45 Connectors (8P8C)
- Crimping Tool (with cutting, stripping, crimping dies)
- Cable Tester

**Procedure (5 marks):**

1. **Cut the cable** to the required length plus ~30 cm slack.
2. **Strip 2.5 cm** of the outer jacket using the stripping notch of the crimping tool — rotate 1.5–2 full turns and pull the jacket off. Be careful not to nick the inner wires.
3. **Untwist the four pairs** as little as possible (max 12.5 mm untwisted). Arrange the wires in T568B order: *White-Orange, Orange, White-Green, Blue, White-Blue, Green, White-Brown, Brown.*
4. **Flatten and trim** the wires straight so they align in a single row, then insert them into the RJ-45 connector with the **clip facing down** and **pin 1 on the left**. Push until all 8 wires reach the end and the outer jacket enters at least 6 mm inside the connector.
5. **Crimp** the connector using the crimping tool's 8P8C slot, squeezing firmly until the ratchet releases.
6. **Repeat** for the other end of the cable using the same T568B standard (this makes a **straight-through cable**).
7. **Test** the cable by inserting both ends into a cable tester. The tester should light up pins 1 through 8 in the same order on both the master and remote units. Any missing, shorted, or crossed pin indicates a fault that must be corrected.

**Verification (1 mark):**
A passing cable tester result — all 8 LEDs in correct sequential order on both units — confirms electrical continuity, correct color order, and absence of shorts.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

**(a)** With the help of a clear comparison table, differentiate between **Hub, Bridge, Switch, Router, and Access Point** based on the following parameters: OSI layer, addressing type, collision domain, broadcast domain, intelligence level, and typical use case. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **T568A and T568B wiring standards**. What is the difference between a straight-through and crossover cable, and when is each used? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Component Comparison (7 marks):** *(See Section 3.2 Analytical Matrix for the full table. The student should reproduce a clear 6-column × 6-row table in the exam, briefly explain each row, and add a one-line conclusion. Allocate ~1 mark per parameter × 5 parameters = 5 marks, plus 2 marks for the explanation and conclusion.)*

A **Hub** operates at Layer 1 with no addressing — it simply broadcasts electrical signals. A **Bridge** operates at Layer 2 using MAC addresses to filter traffic between two segments. A **Switch** is a multiport bridge with per-port collision domain isolation. A **Router** operates at Layer 3 using IP addresses and breaks broadcast domains. An **Access Point** operates at Layer 2 to bridge wireless 802.11 clients to a wired 802.3 LAN. *(2 marks for the explanation.)*

**(b) T568A and T568B Standards (7 marks):**

The **TIA-EIA 568** standard defines two valid pinouts for RJ-45 terminations: **T568A** and **T568B**. Both use the same 8 wires and the same pin positions, but the orange and green pairs are swapped between pin positions 1–2 and 3–6.

**T568A Order (1 mark):**
White-Green, Green, White-Orange, Blue, White-Blue, Orange, White-Brown, Brown (pins 1 to 8).

**T568B Order (1 mark):**
White-Orange, Orange, White-Green, Blue, White-Blue, Green, White-Brown, Brown (pins 1 to 8).

**Straight-Through Cable (3 marks):**
A straight-through cable uses the **same standard on both ends** (e.g., T568B–T568B). The wire on pin 1 at one end is connected to pin 1 at the other end, pin 2 to pin 2, and so on. This is the standard patch cable used to connect **dissimilar devices**: PC → Switch, Switch → Router, PC → Wall Socket. The transmit pair on one device connects to the receive pair on the other.

**Crossover Cable (2 marks):**
A crossover cable uses **T568A on one end and T568B on the other**. The transmit pair (pins 1–2) on one end becomes the receive pair on the other end. Crossover cables are used to connect **similar devices** directly: Switch ↔ Switch, Router ↔ Router, PC ↔ PC, Hub ↔ Hub. *(Note: Modern devices with Auto-MDIX automatically detect the required pairing, making crossover cables largely obsolete.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks on this experiment:**
> 1. **Confusing Hub and Switch terminology** — saying "switch operates at Layer 3" or "hub uses MAC addressing" loses instant marks. Memorize the layer mapping precisely.
> 2. **Wrong pin numbers for T568B** — examiners often ask for "pin 4 wire color." Students write "orange" instead of "blue." Memorize the full sequence.
> 3. **Skipping steps in crimping** — never say "just attach the connector." Examiners expect the full 6-stage pipeline: cut, strip, untwist, arrange, insert, crimp, test.
> 4. **Forgetting the "untwist length" rule** — KTU specifically values the 12.5 mm rule; mentioning it earns a bonus mark.
> 5. **No mention of cable testing** — closing the answer without describing how the finished cable is verified is a common reason for losing 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Mixing up crossover and straight-through** — write the actual pin mapping and the use case clearly.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Hub = Layer 1 = Broadcast = One big collision domain = Obsolete.**
- **Bridge = Layer 2 = 2 ports = Filters by MAC = Obsolete (replaced by switch).**
- **Switch = Layer 2 = Many ports = CAM table = One collision domain per port = The heart of every modern LAN.**
- **Router = Layer 3 = IP-based routing = Breaks broadcast domains = Gateway to the Internet.**
- **Access Point = Layer 2 = Wi-Fi ↔ Ethernet bridge = Uses 2.4/5/6 GHz radio bands.**
- **UTP Categories:** Cat 5e (1 Gbps), Cat 6 (1–10 Gbps), Cat 6a (10 Gbps full distance), Cat 7 (shielded), Cat 8 (25–40 Gbps, data centers).
- **T568B Pin Order (memorize this):** White-Orange, Orange, White-Green, Blue, White-Blue, Green, White-Brown, Brown.
- **T568A Pin Order:** White-Green, Green, White-Orange, Blue, White-Blue, Orange, White-Brown, Brown.
- **Straight-Through Cable:** Same standard both ends → connects PC to Switch.
- **Crossover Cable:** T568A ↔ T568B → connects Switch to Switch (now mostly obsolete due to Auto-MDIX).
- **Crimping Tools:** Cutting blade, stripping notch, crimping die for 8P8C.
- **Maximum Untwist Length:** 12.5 mm (0.5 inch) — preserves signal integrity for Gigabit.
- **Jacket Insertion into Connector:** At least 6 mm to ensure strain relief.
- **Cable Tester Pass Condition:** All 8 LEDs light in order 1 → 8 on both master and remote units.
- **Common Faults:** Open (broken wire), Short (two wires touching), Mis-wire (wrong color order), Split pair (passes continuity, fails Gigabit).
- **PoE Note:** Pins 4, 5 (blue pair) and 7, 8 (brown pair) carry power in Power over Ethernet (802.3af/at/bt) applications.
- **Always test every cable after crimping** — do not assume it works.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Familiarization of Internetworking operating system - access the CLI, switch between different command modes, basic IOS commands - to display device information and configurations, to configure interface and basic troubleshooting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITL407 - COMPUTER NETWORKS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Familiarization of Internetworking operating system - access the CLI, switch between different command modes, basic IOS commands - to display device information and configurations, to configure interface and basic troubleshooting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Internetworking Operating System?

An **Internetworking Operating System (IOS)** is the proprietary software kernel that runs on Cisco routers, switches, and other networking devices. It is the "brain" of the device — managing hardware resources, executing routing/switching logic, enforcing security, and providing the user interface through which administrators configure the device.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Cisco IOS):**
> Cisco IOS (Internetwork Operating System) is a proprietary, real-time, multitasking operating system that provides the underlying infrastructure for routing, switching, security, and management functions in Cisco networking devices. It presents a **Command Line Interface (CLI)** as the primary means of device configuration and monitoring.

## 1.2 The Command Line Interface (CLI)

The **Command Line Interface (CLI)** is the text-based environment through which a network engineer issues configuration and monitoring commands directly to the IOS. It is the only "low-level" method that gives complete control over every feature of a Cisco device.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Building Manager

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Building Control Room"**
> Imagine a large commercial building with hundreds of rooms, elevators, fire alarms, and electrical panels.
> - The **IOS** is the *building management software* running on the central server.
> - The **CLI** is the *control room terminal* where the building manager types instructions.
> - **Command Modes** are like *different security clearance levels* — a janitor (User mode) can only view the building status, while a chief engineer (Privileged/Config mode) can rewire entire floors.
> - **Show commands** are like *CCTV monitors* — they let you see what is happening.
> - **Configure commands** are like *wrench tools* — they let you change the actual state.
> - **Basic Troubleshooting commands** (ping, traceroute) are like *diagnostic instruments* used to test if a water pipe (network link) is blocked or broken.

## 1.4 Why This Experiment Matters in the KTU Lab

In the **PCITL407 Computer Networks Lab**, this is the **foundation experiment**. Before students can configure routing protocols (RIP, OSPF), VLANs, or ACLs, they *must* master:
1. Accessing the CLI via console, Telnet, or SSH.
2. Navigating between command modes.
3. Using `show` commands to inspect device state.
4. Configuring basic interface parameters.
5. Performing elementary troubleshooting.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** Approximately **60–70%** of marks in any networking lab exam are awarded to the *correctness of the command syntax and the exact sequence* of mode transitions. Memorizing the *prompt symbols* (>, #, (config)#) is non-negotiable.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Cisco IOS Command Mode Hierarchy

The IOS CLI is **hierarchically structured** for security and logical separation. Each mode restricts the set of commands that can be executed. The hierarchy is structured as a tree — moving "down" enables more powerful commands, moving "up" restricts them.

### 2.1.1 Mode-by-Mode Breakdown

| # | Mode Name | Prompt Symbol | Purpose | Typical Commands |
|---|-----------|---------------|---------|------------------|
| 1 | **User EXEC Mode** | `Router>` | Limited monitoring only. Cannot view or change configuration. | `show version`, `ping`, `enable` |
| 2 | **Privileged EXEC Mode** | `Router#` | Full read access, all `show` commands, debug, file management, can enter config modes. | `show running-config`, `show ip interface brief`, `copy`, `reload`, `configure terminal` |
| 3 | **Global Configuration Mode** | `Router(config)#` | Affects the entire device. From here, sub-modes can be entered. | `hostname`, `enable secret`, `banner`, `service` |
| 4 | **Interface Configuration Mode** | `Router(config-if)#` | Modifies a specific interface (FastEthernet, Serial, GigabitEthernet, Loopback). | `ip address`, `no shutdown`, `description`, `speed`, `duplex` |
| 5 | **Line Configuration Mode** | `Router(config-line)#` | Configures console, vty (Telnet/SSH), or AUX lines. | `password`, `login`, `transport input ssh` |
| 6 | **Router Configuration Mode** | `Router(config-router)#` | Enters a routing protocol process (RIP, OSPF, EIGRP, BGP). | `network`, `passive-interface` |
| 7 | **Subinterface Config Mode** | `Router(config-subif)#` | Logical sub-interfaces, used for inter-VLAN routing (Router-on-a-Stick). | `encapsulation dot1Q`, `ip address` |

> [!NOTE]
> The **prompt symbol** is the most reliable visual indicator of your current mode. A change in the prompt = a change in mode. KTU examiners frequently use screenshot questions where the student must identify the mode from the prompt.

## 2.2 IOS Command Structure — The Syntax Anatomy

Every IOS command follows a strict grammatical structure. Misunderstanding this causes 90% of student errors.

**General Syntax:**

```
command [keyword] [argument] [modifier]
```

- **Command** — The action verb (e.g., `show`, `configure`, `ping`).
- **Keyword** — A specific, predefined parameter the command expects (e.g., `ip`, `running-config`).
- **Argument** — A user-supplied value (e.g., `192.168.1.1`, `255.255.255.0`).
- **Modifier** — Refines the behavior (e.g., `| begin`, `| include`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Two Types of Help Available in IOS:**
> 1. **Context-Sensitive Help** — Type `?` at any point to see what is available next.
>    - `show ?` — lists all valid keywords for `show`.
>    - `show ip ?` — drills down further.
>    - `c?` — lists commands starting with `c`.
> 2. **Command Completion** — Type the first few unique letters and press **Tab** to auto-complete.

## 2.3 Configuration File Concept — RAM vs. NVRAM

| Storage | File Name | Volatile? | Contents |
|---------|-----------|-----------|----------|
| **RAM** | `running-config` | Yes (lost on reload) | The *currently active* configuration. Modified immediately by every config command. |
| **NVRAM** | `startup-config` | No (persists) | The configuration that is *loaded* on boot. Must be explicitly saved. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Students often configure a router perfectly in the lab, reload it, and find all changes gone. The reason: they forgot `copy running-config startup-config` (or its shortcut `wr`). Losing marks for this in a viva is extremely common.

## 2.4 The `show` Command Family — The Engineer's Eyes

The `show` commands are **read-only diagnostic commands** available in Privileged EXEC mode. They are the single most important toolset for both configuration verification and troubleshooting.

### 2.4.1 Essential `show` Commands Cheat Sheet

| Command | What It Displays | When to Use |
|---------|-----------------|-------------|
| `show version` | IOS version, device model, uptime, configuration register, memory. | Initial device identification. |
| `show running-config` | The entire active configuration in RAM. | Verifying current changes. |
| `show startup-config` | The saved configuration in NVRAM. | Checking what survives a reload. |
| `show ip interface brief` | Compact one-line-per-interface summary: IP, status, protocol. | Quick health check of all interfaces. |
| `show interfaces <int>` | Detailed statistics, errors, MTU, speed, duplex for one interface. | Deep interface troubleshooting. |
| `show ip route` | The routing table — every known network, how it was learned, metric. | Verifying route propagation. |
| `show cdp neighbors` | Directly connected Cisco devices (model, IP, local/remote port). | Topology discovery in Cisco-only networks. |
| `show hosts` | Manually configured name-to-IP mappings. | Verifying local DNS entries. |
| `show flash:` | Files stored in flash memory (IOS image). | Confirming IOS file is present. |
| `show history` | Previously typed commands in the buffer. | Reusing long commands. |

## 2.5 Basic Interface Configuration Logic

Configuring an interface requires a **strict 5-step logical sequence** (this is heavily tested):

1. **Enter global config mode** → `configure terminal`
2. **Enter the interface** → `interface <type><slot/port>` (e.g., `interface GigabitEthernet0/0`)
3. **Assign an IP address and subnet mask** → `ip address <IP> <mask>`
4. **Describe the interface (optional but professional)** → `description <text>`
5. **Activate the interface** → `no shutdown` (interfaces are **shutdown by default** on routers)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "No" Prefix Convention:** In Cisco IOS, `no` placed *before* almost any configuration command **negates or removes** that command. Examples: `no shutdown` (enables), `no ip address` (removes IP), `no hostname` (resets to default).

## 2.6 Basic Troubleshooting Command Set

| Command | Mode | Function | Output Meaning |
|---------|------|----------|----------------|
| `ping <ip>` | User/Privileged | Sends ICMP Echo Request packets. | `!!!!!` = success, `.....` = timeout, `U.U.U` = unreachable. |
| `traceroute <ip>` | User/Privileged | Traces the path packets take hop-by-hop. | Lists every router on the way, time in ms. |
| `show ip interface brief` | Privileged | One-line per interface. | `up/up` = OK, `administratively down` = needs `no shutdown`, `down/down` = cable/physical issue. |
| `show controllers <int>` | Privileged | Low-level hardware diagnostics. | Shows cable type, clocking, DTE/DCE status. |
| `debug ip routing` | Privileged | Real-time logging of routing changes. | **Warning:** CPU-intensive; always disable with `undebug all` or `u all`. |
| `show cdp neighbors detail` | Privileged | Detailed info on adjacent Cisco devices. | Confirms Layer 2 reachability. |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Real-World Utility:** These exact commands are used by **CCNA/CCNP-certified network engineers** daily in enterprise networks, ISPs, and data centers. Mastering them in the KTU lab directly translates to industry-readiness.

## 2.7 Access Methods to the CLI

| Method | Physical Requirement | Security Level | KTU Lab Use Case |
|--------|----------------------|----------------|------------------|
| **Console** | Direct serial/USB cable to PC | Highest (out-of-band, no network needed) | Initial configuration when no IP exists. **Most used in lab.** |
| **Telnet (vty)** | IP connectivity required | Low (plaintext) | Remote access over network. |
| **SSH (vty)** | IP + crypto IOS image required | High (encrypted) | Secure remote access — preferred in real deployments. |
| **AUX Port** | Modem dial-up | Legacy | Almost never used today. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Console Access Parameters (Must Memorize):**
> - **Baud Rate:** 9600
> - **Data Bits:** 8
> - **Parity:** None
> - **Stop Bits:** 1
> - **Flow Control:** None
> This is the standard Cisco default and is asked in viva questions frequently.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — The Complete Lab Procedure

This section gives the **exhaustive, ordered, click-by-click procedure** a student would follow in the PCITL407 lab. Every transition is justified, every output is interpreted.

### 3.1.1 Phase 1: Physical & Logical Access

**Step 1: Identify the device and the access method.**

For a KTU lab, you will most commonly use a **Cisco Packet Tracer simulation** or a **physical router/switch** accessed via the console port (DB-9 to RJ-45 rollover cable, or USB console cable for modern devices).

**Step 2: Launch the terminal emulator.**

On Packet Tracer: Click on the router → **Config Tab** is GUI, but for CLI click the **CLI Tab**.
On a real machine: Open PuTTY/Tera Term → select **Serial** → set COM port → set parameters (9600, 8, N, 1) → click Open.

**Step 3: Observe the initial prompt.**

When IOS boots, the first prompt you see is the **User EXEC Mode** prompt:

```
Router>
```

The trailing `>` symbol is your **confirmation marker**. The hostname (`Router` by default) precedes it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation Logic:**
> If the prompt shows `>`, you are in User EXEC. You **cannot** configure anything significant here. You can only do basic monitoring (`ping`, `show version`, `enable`).

### 3.1.2 Phase 2: Mode Transitions (The Critical Skill)

**Step 4: Transition from User EXEC → Privileged EXEC.**

```
Router> enable
```

The system will respond by changing the prompt:

```
Router#
```

The `#` symbol indicates **Privileged EXEC mode**. This mode is password-protected in production but is open by default in lab routers.

**Why does this transition exist?**

It enforces the **principle of least privilege** — a casual user can ping a host to check connectivity, but cannot accidentally erase the routing table or change an IP address.

**Step 5: Transition from Privileged EXEC → Global Configuration.**

```
Router# configure terminal
```

The prompt changes to:

```
Router(config)#
```

This is where **device-wide parameters** are set. The `(config)` portion enclosed in parentheses is the **mode indicator**.

**Shortcut:** `conf t` works identically (Tab completion handles it).

**Step 6: Set a hostname (a fundamental global config).**

```
Router(config)# hostname KTU-LAB-R1
KTU-LAB-R1(config)#
```

Notice the prompt **immediately** reflects the new hostname. This is instant feedback that the command took effect.

**Step 7: Set an enable secret (privileged mode password).**

```
KTU-LAB-R1(config)# enable secret Ktu@2024
```

This stores the password using **MD5 hashing** (Type 5). It is the secure successor to the deprecated `enable password` (Type 7, weak reversible encryption).

**Step 8: Save the configuration.**

```
KTU-LAB-R1# copy running-config startup-config
Destination filename [startup-config]? [Press Enter]
Building configuration...
[OK]
KTU-LAB-R1#
```

**Shortcut:** `wr` (write memory) does the same.

**Verification of save:**

```
KTU-LAB-R1# show startup-config
```

The new hostname and secret should appear.

### 3.1.3 Phase 3: Interface Configuration

**Step 9: Enter the interface to be configured.**

```
KTU-LAB-R1(config)# interface GigabitEthernet0/0
KTU-LAB-R1(config-if)#
```

The prompt `(config-if)` confirms interface configuration mode.

**Step 10: Assign the IP address and subnet mask.**

```
KTU-LAB-R1(config-if)# ip address 192.168.1.1 255.255.255.0
```

**Syntax breakdown:**
- `192.168.1.1` → Network address argument.
- `255.255.255.0` → Subnet mask argument.
- The command silently succeeds — **no "OK" message** in IOS for many config commands.

**Step 11: Activate the interface.**

```
KTU-LAB-R1(config-if)# no shutdown
```

**Output:**

```
%LINK-5-CHANGED: Interface GigabitEthernet0/0, changed state to up
%LINEPROTO-5-UPDOWN: Line protocol on Interface GigabitEthernet0/0, changed state to up
```

These two syslog messages are **critical indicators** of success.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpreting the Two Messages:**
> - **Line Up** = the physical/data-link layer is operational (cable connected, electrical signal detected).
> - **Line Protocol Up** = the higher-layer protocol (keepalives) is operational.
> - If only **Line Up** appears but not **Line Protocol Up**, you have a Layer 2 problem (clocking, encapsulation, keepalive mismatch).
> - If neither appears, you have a physical/cable issue.

**Step 12: Add an interface description (best practice).**

```
KTU-LAB-R1(config-if)# description LINK-TO-SW1-PORT24
```

**Step 13: Exit back to global config.**

```
KTU-LAB-R1(config-if)# exit
KTU-LAB-R1(config)#
```

### 3.1.4 Phase 4: Displaying Device Information (Verification)

**Step 14: Use `show ip interface brief` for a quick health snapshot.**

```
KTU-LAB-R1# show ip interface brief
```

**Sample Output:**

```
Interface              IP-Address      OK? Method Status                Protocol
GigabitEthernet0/0     192.168.1.1     YES manual up                    up
GigabitEthernet0/1     unassigned      YES NVRAM  administratively down down
Serial0/0/0            unassigned      YES NVRAM  administratively down down
```

**Interpretation Table for Students:**

| Status Column | Protocol Column | Meaning | Action |
|---------------|-----------------|---------|--------|
| `up` | `up` | Interface fully operational. | None. |
| `administratively down` | `down` | Shutdown by `shutdown` command. | `no shutdown`. |
| `down` | `down` | No carrier signal (cable unplugged or wrong port). | Check cable/port. |
| `up` | `down` | Layer 1 OK, Layer 2 issue. | Check encapsulation, clock rate, keepalive. |

**Step 15: View the full running configuration.**

```
KTU-LAB-R1# show running-config
```

This displays **every line of active configuration** — hostname, secrets, interface IPs, descriptions, routing protocol statements, etc. This is the primary grading output in lab exams.

### 3.1.5 Phase 5: Basic Troubleshooting

**Step 16: Test local IP stack with loopback ping.**

```
KTU-LAB-R1# ping 192.168.1.1
```

**Expected Output:**

```
Type escape sequence to abort.
Sending 5, 100-byte ICMP Echos to 192.168.1.1, timeout is 2 seconds:
!!!!!
Success rate is 100 percent (5/5)
```

`!!!!!` = 5/5 successful. If you get `.....` (dots), the IP stack on your own interface is broken.

**Step 17: Test remote connectivity.**

```
KTU-LAB-R1# ping 192.168.1.2
```

If this fails, systematically check:
- Is the destination interface `up/up`? (`show ip interface brief`)
- Is the cable connected?
- Is the destination IP in the same subnet? (otherwise routing is needed)

**Step 18: Trace the path if routing is involved.**

```
KTU-LAB-R1# traceroute 10.0.0.1
```

This lists every hop and its response time, helping locate the failing router on a long path.

## 3.2 Complete Command Flow Diagram (As a Textual Derivation)

Let us derive the **complete configuration session** as a single continuous flow:

```
Router>                                              ← User EXEC (Limited)
Router> enable                                       ← Transition
Router#                                              ← Privileged EXEC (Read + Tools)
Router# configure terminal                           ← Transition
Router(config)#                                      ← Global Config (Device-wide)
Router(config)# hostname KTU-LAB-R1                  ← Set device name
KTU-LAB-R1(config)# enable secret Ktu@2024           ← Set privileged password
KTU-LAB-R1(config)# interface GigabitEthernet0/0     ← Enter sub-mode
KTU-LAB-R1(config-if)# ip address 192.168.1.1 255.255.255.0   ← Assign IP
KTU-LAB-R1(config-if)# no shutdown                   ← Enable interface
KTU-LAB-R1(config-if)# description LINK-TO-SW1       ← Add description
KTU-LAB-R1(config-if)# exit                          ← Back to global config
KTU-LAB-R1(config)# exit                             ← Back to privileged EXEC
KTU-LAB-R1#                                          ← Privileged EXEC
KTU-LAB-R1# show ip interface brief                  ← Verify
KTU-LAB-R1# show running-config                      ← View full config
KTU-LAB-R1# copy running-config startup-config      ← Save to NVRAM
KTU-LAB-R1# ping 192.168.1.2                         ← Test connectivity
KTU-LAB-R1# show version                             ← Device identity
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "exit" Twice Returns to Privileged, Not User EXEC?**
> Each `exit` moves **one level up** in the mode tree. From `(config-if)`, one `exit` reaches `(config)`. A second `exit` reaches `#` (Privileged EXEC), not `>`. To return all the way to User EXEC, you would use `end` or `Ctrl+Z`.

## 3.3 Numerical Example — Subnet Mask ↔ Prefix Length Conversion

A common KTU question: "Convert `255.255.255.252` to CIDR prefix length and identify how many usable host addresses the subnet provides."

**Step 1: Convert the dotted-decimal mask to binary.**

$$
255 = 11111111,\quad 252 = 11111100
$$

$$
255.255.255.252 = 11111111.11111111.11111111.11111100
$$

**Step 2: Count the leading 1s.**

$$
30 \text{ ones} \rightarrow \text{CIDR prefix} = /30
$$

**Step 3: Number of host bits.**

$$
\text{Host bits} = 32 - 30 = 2
$$

**Step 4: Total and usable host addresses.**

$$
\text{Total} = 2^2 = 4
$$

$$
\text{Usable} = 2^2 - 2 = 2
$$

**Result:** A `/30` subnet has **2 usable host addresses** — typically used for point-to-point WAN links (Serial interfaces) between two routers.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Tree — "What Command Do I Use?"

```
What do I want to do?
│
├── See what version of IOS is running ────────► show version
├── See ALL configuration ────────────────────► show running-config
├── See saved configuration ──────────────────► show startup-config
├── See quick interface status ───────────────► show ip interface brief
├── See one interface in detail ──────────────► show interfaces <int>
├── See routes ───────────────────────────────► show ip route
├── Change the device name ───────────────────► (config)# hostname <name>
├── Set privileged password ──────────────────► (config)# enable secret <pwd>
├── Set console password ─────────────────────► (config-line)# password <pwd>
│                                              (config-line)# login
├── Configure an interface ───────────────────► (config)# interface <int>
│                                              (config-if)# ip address <ip> <mask>
│                                              (config-if)# no shutdown
├── Test connectivity ────────────────────────► ping <ip>
├── Trace the path ───────────────────────────► traceroute <ip>
├── Discover neighbors ───────────────────────► show cdp neighbors
└── Save changes ─────────────────────────────► copy running-config startup-config
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cisco IOS Command Mode Hierarchy (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following diagram maps the **complete mode hierarchy** a student navigates, with the **prompt symbol** and **entry/exit commands** annotated for each node.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["USER EXEC MODE<br/>Prompt: Router greater-than<br/>Limited monitoring only<br/>ENTRY: default on boot<br/>EXIT: enable"] --> B["PRIVILEGED EXEC MODE<br/>Prompt: Router hash<br/>Full read, debug, file ops<br/>ENTRY: enable command<br/>EXIT: disable or exit"]
    B --> C["GLOBAL CONFIG MODE<br/>Prompt: Router open-paren-config close-paren hash<br/>Device-wide settings<br/>ENTRY: configure terminal<br/>EXIT: exit or end"]
    C --> D["INTERFACE CONFIG MODE<br/>Prompt: Router open-paren-config-if close-paren hash<br/>Per-interface settings<br/>ENTRY: interface type slot slash port<br/>EXIT: exit to global"]
    C --> E["LINE CONFIG MODE<br/>Prompt: Router open-paren-config-line close-paren hash<br/>Console VTY AUX lines<br/>ENTRY: line console 0 OR line vty 0 4<br/>EXIT: exit to global"]
    C --> F["ROUTER CONFIG MODE<br/>Prompt: Router open-paren-config-router close-paren hash<br/>Routing protocol processes<br/>ENTRY: router rip OR router ospf 1<br/>EXIT: exit to global"]
    C --> G["SUBINTERFACE CONFIG MODE<br/>Prompt: Router open-paren-config-subif close-paren hash<br/>Logical sub-interfaces<br/>ENTRY: interface type slot slash port dot sub<br/>EXIT: exit to global"]
    B --> H["ROMMON MODE<br/>Prompt: rommon greater-than<br/>Recovery, password reset<br/>ENTRY: Break sequence during boot<br/>EXIT: confreg reset then boot"]
    D --> I["EXIT returns to Global"]
    E --> I
    F --> I
    G --> I
    I --> J["EXIT from Global returns to Privileged"]
    J --> K["END or Ctrl plus Z returns to Privileged"]
    K --> L["DISABLE returns to User"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to Read This Diagram:** Each rectangular node is a mode. Solid arrows are `exit` paths upward. The mode names inside the nodes use plain text (no markdown formatting) per the Mermaid safety rules — this guarantees the diagram renders correctly in any markdown viewer.

## 4.2 Configuration Flow — From Power-On to Saved Config (Block Architecture)

This block diagram shows the **end-to-end operational flow** a student follows from a fresh device to a fully configured, saved, and verified state.

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1["STEP 1: PHYSICAL CONNECTION<br/>Console cable to PC<br/>Launch terminal emulator<br/>9600 8N1 parameters"] --> P2["STEP 2: OBSERVE BOOT<br/>ROMMON loads<br/>IOS image decompressed from Flash<br/>Startup-config copied to RAM"]
    P2 --> P3["STEP 3: USER EXEC PROMPT<br/>Router greater-than<br/>Mode of limited access"]
    P3 --> P4["STEP 4: ENABLE COMMAND<br/>Router hash<br/>Privileged mode entered"]
    P4 --> P5["STEP 5: CONFIGURE TERMINAL<br/>Router open-paren-config close-paren hash<br/>Global config mode"]
    P5 --> P6["STEP 6: GLOBAL SETTINGS<br/>hostname X<br/>enable secret Y<br/>banner motd Z"]
    P6 --> P7["STEP 7: INTERFACE SUB-MODE<br/>interface GigabitEthernet0 slash 0<br/>Router open-paren-config-if close-paren hash"]
    P7 --> P8["STEP 8: INTERFACE PARAMETERS<br/>ip address A B<br/>no shutdown<br/>description C"]
    P8 --> P9["STEP 9: VERIFICATION<br/>show ip interface brief<br/>show running-config<br/>show interfaces"]
    P9 --> P10{"DECISION: Config correct?"}
    P10 -->|No| P11["Return to config mode<br/>Correct errors"]
    P11 --> P7
    P10 -->|Yes| P12["STEP 10: TESTING<br/>ping 192.168.1.2<br/>traceroute 10.0.0.1"]
    P12 --> P13{"DECISION: End to end OK?"}
    P13 -->|No| P14["TROUBLESHOOTING<br/>Check interface status<br/>Check cable<br/>Check IP subnet"]
    P14 --> P12
    P13 -->|Yes| P15["STEP 11: PERSIST<br/>copy running-config startup-config<br/>Equivalent: write memory"]
    P15 --> P16["STEP 12: RELOAD TEST<br/>reload command optional<br/>Confirm config survives"]
```

## 4.3 IOS Configuration Storage Architecture (RAM vs NVRAM vs Flash)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["FLASH MEMORY<br/>Non-volatile<br/>Stores IOS image file<br/>e.g. c2900-universalk9-mz.SPA.154-3.M.bin"] -->|IOS loaded at boot| B["RAM<br/>Volatile<br/>Holds running-config<br/>Holds routing tables<br/>Holds ARP cache"]
    C["NVRAM<br/>Non-volatile<br/>Holds startup-config<br/>Loaded into RAM at boot"] -->|copy startup-config running-config| B
    B -->|copy running-config startup-config| C
    B -->|Tech changes via CLI| B
    D["ADMINISTRATOR<br/>via CLI"] -->|conf t, interface, ip address| B
    B -->|show running-config| D
    C -->|show startup-config| D
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Matters:** The classic KTU viva question is *"If a router is rebooted, which configuration is lost and which survives?"* The answer is now obvious from this diagram: only **RAM** contents (running-config) are lost; **NVRAM** (startup-config) and **Flash** (IOS image) survive a reload.

## 4.4 Show Command Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    S["SHOW COMMANDS<br/>Available in Privileged EXEC"] --> S1["DEVICE IDENTITY<br/>show version<br/>show flash<br/>show inventory"]
    S --> S2["CONFIGURATION<br/>show running-config<br/>show startup-config"]
    S --> S3["INTERFACE STATUS<br/>show ip interface brief<br/>show interfaces type slot slash port<br/>show controllers type slot slash port"]
    S --> S4["NETWORKING<br/>show ip route<br/>show ip protocols<br/>show ip arp<br/>show cdp neighbors"]
    S --> S5["CONNECTIVITY<br/>show sessions<br/>show users"]
    S --> S6["MEMORY FILES<br/>show flash colon<br/>dir flash colon<br/>dir nvram colon"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Differentiate between User EXEC mode and Privileged EXEC mode in Cisco IOS. List any two commands exclusive to Privileged EXEC mode.**

#### Model Answer:

| Feature | User EXEC Mode (`Router>`) | Privileged EXEC Mode (`Router#`) |
|---------|----------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| **Access** | Default on device boot. | Requires the `enable` command. |
| **Security** | Low — limited monitoring only. | High — can view/edit configuration. |
| **Configurable?** | No. | Yes, can enter configuration modes. |
| **Prompt** | Trailing `>` symbol. | Trailing `#` symbol. |

**Two Privileged-EXEC-exclusive commands:**
1. `show running-config` — displays the active configuration in RAM.
2. `copy running-config startup-config` — saves the active configuration to NVRAM.

*(Accept any two valid `show` or management commands.)*

**Mark Distribution:**
- Mode differentiation: 1 mark.
- Two exclusive commands: 1 mark each = 2 marks.
- **Total: 3 marks.**

---

### Question A2: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Explain the difference between `running-config` and `startup-config`. Why is `copy running-config startup-config` considered a critical step?**

#### Model Answer:

- **`running-config`** is the **active** configuration stored in **RAM (volatile memory)**. It is what the device is *currently* using. It is modified immediately by every configuration command typed at the CLI.
- **`startup-config`** is the **saved** configuration stored in **NVRAM (non-volatile memory)**. It is the file that is *loaded into RAM* when the device boots.

**Why `copy running-config startup-config` is critical:**

If a student configures a router but forgets to execute this command, all changes are stored *only* in volatile RAM. When the router is reloaded (intentionally or due to a power loss), the device loads the **old** `startup-config` from NVRAM, and **all un-saved changes are lost permanently**.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Difference between the two files: 1.5 marks.
- Criticality of save command: 1.5 marks.
- **Total: 3 marks.**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU ESE Pattern Note]:** Part B questions carry 14 marks with internal choice. Each typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each: part (a) tests descriptive/theoretical understanding, part (b) tests the *practical command sequence* with verification.

---

### Question B1 — Option A: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

#### (a) Describe the hierarchical structure of Cisco IOS command modes with a labeled diagram. Explain the role of each mode. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer:

The Cisco IOS CLI is organized as a **hierarchical, tree-like structure** of modes. The current mode is always indicated by the **prompt symbol**, and each mode restricts the set of commands that can be executed (principle of least privilege).

**The hierarchy is:**

1. **User EXEC Mode (`Router>`):** The initial, low-privilege mode reached on boot. Only limited monitoring commands are allowed (`ping`, `show version`, `enable`).

2. **Privileged EXEC Mode (`Router#`):** Reached via the `enable` command. Provides full read access, all diagnostic `show` commands, debugging, file management, and is the gateway to all configuration modes.

3. **Global Configuration Mode (`Router(config)#`):** Reached via `configure terminal` (or `conf t`). Device-wide parameters are set here: hostname, passwords, banners, service settings. From this mode, sub-modes can be entered.

4. **Interface Configuration Mode (`Router(config-if)#`):** Reached via `interface <type><slot/port>`. Configures a single specific interface — IP address, subnet mask, description, speed/duplex, and the `no shutdown` activation.

5. **Line Configuration Mode (`Router(config-line)#`):** Reached via `line console 0` (console) or `line vty 0 4` (Telnet/SSH). Configures line-specific passwords, login behavior, and transport protocols.

6. **Router Configuration Mode (`Router(config-router)#`):** Reached via `router <protocol> [id]`. Configures a routing protocol process (RIP, OSPF, EIGRP, BGP), network statements, and passive interfaces.

7. **Subinterface Configuration Mode (`Router(config-subif)#`):** Reached via `interface <type><slot/port>.<sub>`. Used for inter-VLAN routing (Router-on-a-Stick) and frame-relay sub-interfaces.

**Navigation rules:**
- `exit` moves **one level up** in the tree.
- `end` or `Ctrl+Z` returns **directly to Privileged EXEC** from any sub-mode.
- `disable` returns from Privileged EXEC to User EXEC.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Description of 5+ modes: 5 marks (1 mark each).
- Navigation logic and prompt symbols: 2 marks.
- **Total: 7 marks.**

---

#### (b) Write the complete step-by-step IOS command sequence to: (i) set the hostname to `KTU-R1`, (ii) assign IP `10.0.0.1/24` to interface `GigabitEthernet0/0`, (iii) enable the interface, and (iv) verify the configuration. Show the prompts at each step. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer:

```
Router> enable
Router# configure terminal
Router(config)# hostname KTU-R1
KTU-R1(config)# interface GigabitEthernet0/0
KTU-R1(config-if)# ip address 10.0.0.1 255.255.255.0
KTU-R1(config-if)# no shutdown
KTU-R1(config-if)# end
KTU-R1# show ip interface brief
```

**Verification of `show ip interface brief` output:**

```
Interface              IP-Address      OK? Method Status                Protocol
GigabitEthernet0/0     10.0.0.1        YES manual up                    up
```

**Expected syslog messages after `no shutdown`:**

```
%LINK-5-CHANGED: Interface GigabitEthernet0/0, changed state to up
%LINEPROTO-5-UPDOWN: Line protocol on Interface GigabitEthernet0/0, changed state to up
```

**Explanation of key points:**

- `configure terminal` enters Global Config mode where device-wide changes begin.
- `hostname` is a global config command — the prompt updates immediately.
- `interface GigabitEthernet0/0` is a sub-mode entry command; the prompt becomes `(config-if)`.
- `ip address 10.0.0.1 255.255.255.0` configures the IPv4 address and the dotted-decimal mask. The `/24` is a CIDR shorthand; in IOS, the **full mask must be typed**.
- `no shutdown` is **mandatory** because router interfaces are administratively down by default. The `no` prefix *negates* the default `shutdown` state.
- `end` (or `Ctrl+Z`) exits directly to Privileged EXEC.
- `show ip interface brief` is the most efficient verification command — the `up/up` status confirms a working interface.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Correct mode transitions and prompts: 2 marks.
- Correct command syntax: 3 marks.
- Verification command + correct interpretation: 2 marks.
- **Total: 7 marks.**

---

### Question B1 — Option B: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

#### (a) Explain the role of the following IOS commands: `show version`, `show running-config`, `show ip interface brief`, and `show cdp neighbors`. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer:

**1. `show version`:**
Displays device identification and hardware/software summary information. Output includes: device model (e.g., `Cisco 2911`), IOS version and image filename, system uptime, amount of RAM and NVRAM, number of interfaces, and the **configuration register** value (which controls boot behavior). It is the **first command** a network engineer runs on a new device.

**2. `show running-config`:**
Displays the **complete active configuration** currently loaded in RAM. Every command that has been typed and accepted since the last boot is shown in order. This is the **primary verification** command during lab work and is the first place a troubleshooter looks.

**3. `show ip interface brief`:**
Produces a **condensed, one-line-per-interface table** summarizing: interface name, IP address, OK flag (valid config?), method (manual/DHCP), current status (administratively down / down / up), and line protocol status. It is the **fastest health check** for an entire device.

**4. `show cdp neighbors`:**
Uses **Cisco Discovery Protocol (CDP)** — a Layer 2 proprietary protocol — to list all **directly connected Cisco devices**. Output includes: neighbor device ID, local interface, holdtime, capability (R/S for router/switch), platform model, and remote port ID. It is invaluable for **physical topology discovery** in Cisco-only networks and runs by default every 60 seconds.

**Mark Distribution:**
- 1.5 marks each for `show version`, `show running-config`, `show ip interface brief`.
- 2.5 marks for `show cdp neighbors` (more complex).
- **Total: 7 marks.**

---

#### (b) A PC with IP `192.168.1.10/24` is connected to a router interface `GigabitEthernet0/0`. Configure the interface on the router with IP `192.168.1.1/24`, enable it, and then troubleshoot using `ping` and `show ip interface brief` to confirm the link. Show all commands and expected outputs. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer:

**Configuration Phase:**

```
Router> enable
Router# configure terminal
Router(config)# interface GigabitEthernet0/0
Router(config-if)# ip address 192.168.1.1 255.255.255.0
Router(config-if)# no shutdown
Router(config-if)# end
```

**Expected syslog output:**

```
%LINK-5-CHANGED: Interface GigabitEthernet0/0, changed state to up
%LINEPROTO-5-UPDOWN: Line protocol on Interface GigabitEthernet0/0, changed state to up
```

**Verification Phase:**

```
Router# show ip interface brief
```

**Expected Output:**

```
Interface              IP-Address      OK? Method Status                Protocol
GigabitEthernet0/0     192.168.1.1     YES manual up                    up
```

**Connectivity Test (Troubleshooting Phase):**

```
Router# ping 192.168.1.10
```

**Expected Successful Output:**

```
Type escape sequence to abort.
Sending 5, 100-byte ICMP Echos to 192.168.1.10, timeout is 2 seconds:
!!!!!
Success rate is 100 percent (5/5)
```

**If the ping fails (debugging interpretation):**

| Failure Pattern | Likely Cause | Remediation |
|-----------------|--------------|-------------|
| `.....` (timeouts) | PC is off / not connected. | Check PC power and cable. |
| `U.U.U` (unreachable) | Wrong subnet or no route. | Verify PC IP and subnet. |
| `show ip interface brief` shows `administratively down` | `no shutdown` not applied. | Re-enter `no shutdown`. |
| Status shows `down/down` | Cable unplugged or wrong port. | Reseat cable. |

**Mark Distribution:**
- Configuration commands: 3 marks.
- Verification commands: 2 marks.
- Ping output + troubleshooting interpretation: 2 marks.
- **Total: 7 marks.**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting `no shutdown`** — By far the most common error. The interface is configured with an IP but stays in `administratively down/down` state. *Examiner deduction: 2–3 marks.*
> 2. **Using `/24` instead of `255.255.255.0` in the `ip address` command** — IOS does not accept CIDR notation; the full dotted-decimal mask is mandatory. *Examiner deduction: 1 mark.*
> 3. **Not saving the configuration with `copy running-config startup-config`** — The configuration is lost on reload. *Examiner deduction: 1 mark.*
> 4. **Confusing prompt symbols** — Writing `Router#` when in Global Config, or omitting the `(config-if)` portion in sub-modes. *Examiner deduction: 0.5–1 mark per instance.*
> 5. **Forgetting to `enable` before `configure terminal`** — `configure terminal` is not valid from User EXEC. *Examiner deduction: 1 mark.*
> 6. **Viva trap: "What is the default state of a router interface?"** — Correct answer: *administratively down*. Many students incorrectly say "up" or "enabled by default". *Examiner deduction in viva: up to 2 marks.*
> 7. **Viva trap: "Why does `show running-config` not show your saved config after a reload?"** — Correct answer: *You are looking at the old startup-config; the running-config now reflects the loaded startup-config. To verify the saved file, use `show startup-config`.* Many students fail to distinguish.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Bullet Summary:**

- **Cisco IOS** is the proprietary operating system of Cisco routers and switches.
- **CLI access** is via console (out-of-band, 9600 8N1), Telnet, or SSH.
- **User EXEC** → `Router>` — limited monitoring.
- **Privileged EXEC** → `Router#` — full access; entry via `enable`.
- **Global Config** → `Router(config)#` — entry via `configure terminal`.
- **Interface Config** → `Router(config-if)#` — entry via `interface <int>`.
- **Line Config** → `Router(config-line)#` — entry via `line console 0` or `line vty 0 4`.
- **Router Config** → `Router(config-router)#` — entry via `router rip / ospf 1`.
- **RAM** holds `running-config` (volatile).
- **NVRAM** holds `startup-config` (non-volatile).
- **Flash** holds the IOS image.
- **Default interface state on a router is `administratively down`.** You MUST use `no shutdown` to activate.
- **Default interface state on a switch is `up/up`** (switches are plug-and-play).
- **Always save** with `copy running-config startup-config` (or `write memory` / `wr`).
- **Help tools:** `?` (context help), `Tab` (completion), `Ctrl+R` (redisplay), `Ctrl+Z` (back to Privileged).
- **Output filters:** `show running-config | include <keyword>` — only show lines containing the keyword.
- **`show ip interface brief`** is the single most important troubleshooting command.
- **`!!!!!** = successful ping. **`.....`** = timeout. **`U.U.U`** = ICMP unreachable.
- **`show cdp neighbors`** discovers directly-connected Cisco devices.
- **`traceroute`** finds the path to a remote host hop-by-hop.
- **Console parameters:** 9600 baud, 8 data bits, no parity, 1 stop bit, no flow control.
- **The `no` prefix** negates/removes a configuration command.
- **Configuration register** (visible in `show version`) controls boot behavior — default is `0x2102`.

---

*End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — PCITL407 Computer Networks Lab (KTU 2024 Scheme).*
