It is defined in **RFC 2453 (RIPv2)** and operates over **UDP port 520**, sending the complete routing table to neighbours every **30 seconds**. - **(1 mark)** The **administrative distance (AD)** of RIP is **120**, and the **maximum hop count** is **15**. Any destination that is 16 or more hops away is treated as **unreachable** (infinity). - **(1 mark)** The value 16 is reserved as the **"infinity" marker** to indicate an unreachable route. This deliberate design choice **prevents count-to-infinity loops** — when a link fails, the metric quickly reaches 16 and the bad route is purged, stabilizing the network. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between RIPv1 and RIPv2. Which version is suitable for a network that uses VLSM and why?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **(1 mark)** RIPv1 is a **classful protocol** defined in RFC 1058. It does **not carry subnet mask information** in its updates and does not support VLSM, CIDR, or authentication. Updates are sent as broadcasts to `255.255.255.255`. - **(1 mark)** RIPv2 is a **classless protocol** defined in RFC 2453. It **carries the subnet mask** in its route entries, supports VLSM and CIDR, and sends updates via **multicast address `224.0.0.9`**. It also supports **plaintext and MD5 authentication**. - **(1 mark)** For a VLSM-based network, **RIPv2 must be used**, because the subnets have different masks (e.g., /26 for LAN and /30 for WAN). RIPv1 would auto-summarize these into a single classful boundary and **discard the mask**, making the variable-length subnets unreachable. With `no auto-summary` under RIPv2, the exact masks are preserved end-to-end. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > As per KTU 2024 ESE regulation, every Part-B question carries **internal choice**. The student must attempt **either Question A or Question B**, not both. Marks are split **7 + 7** across two sub-parts. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** **(a) Design a VLSM addressing scheme for the following requirements, and assign IP addresses to all interfaces.** (7 marks) > An organization is allocated the network **`200.10.0.0/16`**. It has **4 departments** requiring **120, 60, 30, and 12 hosts** respectively, plus **2 point-to-point WAN links** between the routers connecting them. Use **RIP** as the routing protocol. **(b) Configure RIPv2 on all routers, verify the routing table on R1, and explain the meaning of `[120/2]` in the output.** (7 marks) #### Model Solution: **Part (a) — VLSM Design (7 marks):** **Step 1 (1 mark):** Sort requirements in descending order: 120 > 60 > 30 > 12 > 2 > 2. **Step 2 (2 marks):** Calculate subnet mask for each segment using $2^{H} - 2 \geq \text{Required Hosts}$. $$ \begin{aligned} H_{120} &= 7 \;\;(\text{since } 2^{7} = 128) \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{Mask} = /25 \\ H_{60} &= 6 \;\;(\text{since } 2^{6} = 64) \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{Mask} = /26 \\ H_{30} &= 5 \;\;(\text{since } 2^{5} = 32) \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{Mask} = /27 \\ H_{12} &= 4 \;\;(\text{since } 2^{4} = 16) \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{Mask} = /28 \\ H_{2} &= 2 \;\;(\text{since } 2^{2} = 4) \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{Mask} = /30 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 (2 marks):** Allocate sequentially starting from `200.10.0.0`. | Department | Network | Mask | Usable Range | CIDR | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | Dept-1 (120) | 200.10.0.0 | 255.255.255.128 | .1 – .126 | /25 | | Dept-2 (60) | 200.10.0.128 | 255.255.255.192 | .129 – .190 | /26 | | Dept-3 (30) | 200.10.0.192 | 255.255.255.224 | .193 – .222 | /27 | | Dept-4 (12) | 200.10.0.224 | 255.255.255.240 | .225 – .238 | /28 | | WAN-1 | 200.10.0.240 | 255.255.255.252 | .241 – .242 | /30 | | WAN-2 | 200.10.0.244 | 255.255.255.252 | .245 – .246 | /30 | **Step 4 (2 marks):** Assign to router interfaces. ``` R1 Fa0/0 = 200.10.0.1 /25 (Dept-1) R1 Se0/0 = 200.10.0.241 /30 (WAN-1) R2 Fa0/0 = 200.10.0.129 /26 (Dept-2) R2 Se0/0 = 200.10.0.242 /30 (WAN-1) R2 Se0/1 = 200.10.0.245 /30 (WAN-2) R3 Fa0/0 = 200.10.0.193 /27 (Dept-3) R3 Se0/0 = 200.10.0.246 /30 (WAN-2) R4 Fa0/0 = 200.10.0.225 /28 (Dept-4) R4 Se0/0 = 200.10.0.243 /30 (WAN-1) ``` --- **Part (b) — RIPv2 Configuration on R1 (7 marks):** **Step 1 (2 marks):** Configuration commands for R1: ``` enable configure terminal router rip version 2 no auto-summary network 200.10.0.0 end ``` The `network 200.10.0.0` statement covers **all four subnets** because RIP classfully summarizes the major network, but with `no auto-summary`, the **/25, /26, /27, /28, /30 masks are preserved** in the updates (this is the RIPv2 advantage). **Step 2 (2 marks):** Verification command and expected output on R1: ``` R1# show ip route R 200.10.0.128/26 [120/1] via 200.10.0.242, 00:00:15, Serial0/0/0 R 200.10.0.192/27 [120/2] via 200.10.0.242, 00:00:15, Serial0/0/0 R 200.10.0.224/28 [120/3] via 200.10.0.242, 00:00:15, Serial0/0/0 ``` **Step 3 (3 marks):** Meaning of `[120/2]`: - **`120`** → **Administrative Distance (AD)** of RIP. AD is the **trustworthiness rating** of the routing source. Lower AD = more preferred. RIP's AD of 120 is higher than OSPF (110) and EIGRP (90/170), so if multiple protocols advertise the same route, **OSPF/EIGRP will be chosen over RIP**. - **`2`** → **Routing Metric (Hop Count)** to reach the destination. The packet must traverse **2 router hops** (R1 → R2 → R3) to reach `200.10.0.192/27`. - **`via 200.10.0.242`** → The **next-hop IP** is R2's serial interface. - **`00:00:15`** → The route was last refreshed **15 seconds ago** (i.e., the next RIP update is in 15 seconds). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — **Alternative Choice** **(a) Explain the Bellman-Ford distance-vector algorithm used by RIP. List all four RIP timers with their default values.** (7 marks) **(b) A network has three routers R1, R2, R3 connected in series. R1's LAN is `10.1.1.0/24`, R2's LAN is `10.1.2.0/24`, R3's LAN is `10.1.3.0/24`. The WAN links use `172.16.0.0/30` and `172.16.1.0/30`. Configure RIPv2 on R2 (the middle router) and show the routing table of R2 after convergence.** (7 marks) #### Model Solution: **Part (a) — Bellman-Ford Algorithm & Timers (7 marks):** **Step 1 (3 marks):** Bellman-Ford Steps: 1. Each router initializes its table with directly connected networks (metric = 0). 2. Every 30 s, each router sends a copy of its routing table to all RIP-enabled neighbours. 3. On receiving an update, the router adds **+1 to the metric** of each advertised route (because the neighbour incurred 1 hop to reach us). 4. If the new route is unknown or has a **lower metric** than the existing one, the route is updated. 5. If no update is received for 180 s, the route is marked with metric = 16 (infinity). 6. After 240 s of silence, the route is permanently removed. **Step 2 (2 marks):** RIP Timers Table: | Timer | Default Value | Function | |:---:|:---:|:---| | Update Timer | **30 s** | Periodic full-table advertisement | | Invalid Timer | **180 s** | Mark route as invalid if not refreshed | | Hold-down Timer | **180 s** | Suppress updates for a poisoned route | | Flush Timer | **240 s** | Remove the invalid route from the table | **Step 3 (2 marks):** RIP also implements **Split-Horizon with Poison Reverse** — a router advertises a route back to the neighbour that announced it, with metric = 16. This **immediately informs the neighbour that the route is unreachable** through that interface, preventing routing loops during convergence. --- **Part (b) — R2 Configuration (7 marks):** **Step 1 (2 marks):** R2 has **three interfaces** (one LAN + two serial WANs). IP assignment: ``` R2 Fa0/0 = 10.1.2.1 /24 R2 Se0/0/0 = 172.16.0.2 /30 (link to R1) R2 Se0/0/1 = 172.16.1.1 /30 (link to R3) ``` **Step 2 (3 marks):** Configuration: ``` enable configure terminal hostname R2 ! interface FastEthernet0/0 ip address 10.1.2.1 255.255.255.0 no shutdown ! interface Serial0/0/0 ip address 172.16.0.2 255.255.255.252 no shutdown ! interface Serial0/0/1 ip address 172.16.1.1 255.255.255.252 clock rate 64000 no shutdown ! router rip version 2 no auto-summary network 10.0.0.0 network 172.16.0.0 ! end write memory ``` > [!NOTE] > The `network 10.0.0.0` statement covers `10.1.1.0/24`, `10.1.2.0/24`, and `10.1.3.0/24` as a single major network command. With `no auto-summary`, RIPv2 preserves the original /24 masks in updates. **Step 3 (2 marks):** Expected R2 routing table after convergence: ``` R2# show ip route Gateway of last resort is not set 10.0.0.0/24 is subnetted, 3 subnets C 10.1.2.0 is directly connected, FastEthernet0/0 R 10.1.1.0 [120/1] via 172.16.0.1, 00:00:08, Serial0/0/0 R 10.1.3.0 [120/1] via 172.16.1.2, 00:00:14, Serial0/0/1 172.16.0.0/30 is subnetted, 1 subnets C 172.16.0.0 is directly connected, Serial0/0/0 172.16.1.0/30 is subnetted, 1 subnets C 172.16.1.0 is directly connected, Serial0/0/1 ``` --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common places where students lose marks in this experiment:** > > 1. **Forgetting `no shutdown`** — Interfaces stay administratively down. The most common reason for a failed lab. **(-2 marks)** > 2. **Missing `clock rate 64000`** on the DCE side of a serial link. **(-1 mark)** > 3. **Using RIPv1** for a VLSM network. RIPv1 silently drops the mask → routing fails → full marks lost. **(-3 marks)** > 4. **Forgetting `no auto-summary`** under RIPv2. Auto-summary causes classful boundaries → WAN /30 routes vanish. **(-2 marks)** > 5. **Wrong subnet calculations** — using the network address as the host IP, or assigning the broadcast address to a router interface. **(-2 marks)** > 6. **Not saving the configuration** with `write memory` (or `copy running-config startup-config`). **(-1 mark)** > 7. **Confusing `show ip route` codes** — not knowing that `R` = RIP, `C` = Connected, `S` = Static, `O` = OSPF. **(-1 mark)** > 8. **Skipping the `show ip protocols` verification** — the examiner specifically looks for proof that RIPv2 is active and timers are correct. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the Lab Exam (saves you the last 30 minutes of panic):** - ✅ **RIP = Distance-Vector, IGP, Hop Count, AD = 120, Max Hops = 15, Port = UDP 520.** - ✅ **Always use RIPv2 + `no auto-summary`** for any network that uses subnetting or VLSM. - ✅ **Magic Number / Subnet Increment = 256 − (last octet of subnet mask).** - ✅ **Usable Hosts = $2^{H} - 2$, Number of Subnets = $2^{S}$.** - ✅ **Use /30 for all point-to-point WAN links** (2 usable hosts, zero waste). - ✅ **Always issue `no shutdown`** on every interface immediately after `ip address`. - ✅ **Always set `clock rate 64000`** on the DCE side of every serial WAN link. - ✅ **Verification commands in this order:** `show ip interface brief` → `show ip route` → `show ip protocols` → `show ip rip database` → `ping` → `tracert`. - ✅ **Save with `write memory` or `copy running-config startup-config`** before exiting. - ✅ **`[120/x]` in routing table = AD of RIP (120) / Hop Count (x).** - ✅ **Code `R` in routing table = RIP-learned route; `C` = directly Connected; `O` = OSPF; `D` = EIGRP; `S` = Static.** - ✅ **RIP Timers to memorize: 30 / 180 / 180 / 240** (Update / Invalid / Hold-down / Flush). - ✅ **RIP convergence time ≈ 60 seconds** (two update cycles). Wait before debugging. - ✅ **Maximum RIP network diameter = 15 hops** — never design a network where any destination is 16+ hops away. - ✅ **For lab exam record: include the topology diagram, the VLSM table, the full router configs, and the verification outputs.** A record without verification outputs is considered incomplete.

topics.title: Configure and verify Enhanced Interior Gateway Routing Protocol (EIGRP) routing in a network that includes subnetting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCITL407 - COMPUTER NETWORKS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Configure and verify Enhanced Interior Gateway Routing Protocol (EIGRP) routing in a network that includes subnetting # MODULE EXPERIMENT 2 — SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of EIGRP > [!IMPORTANT] > **Enhanced Interior Gateway Routing Protocol (EIGRP)** is a Cisco-proprietary, **classless, advanced distance-vector (hybrid)** routing protocol that uses the **Diffusing Update Algorithm (DUAL)** to compute the shortest loop-free path to every destination, while maintaining **separate tables for neighbors, topology, and routing**. EIGRP was developed by Cisco Systems as an evolution of the original IGRP. Although Cisco originally classified it as an "enhanced distance-vector" protocol, internally it behaves like a link-state protocol because it builds a complete topology map of the network. KTU examiners frequently test this *hybrid* nature, so always use the phrase **"advanced distance-vector / hybrid"** rather than just "distance-vector." | EIGRP Attribute | KTU Board Value | |-----------------|-----------------| | Protocol Type | Advanced Distance-Vector (Hybrid) | | Algorithm | DUAL (Diffusing Update Algorithm) | | Administrative Distance | **90** (Internal) / **170** (External) | | Multicast Address | **224.0.0.10** | | Protocol Number | **88** (IP) | | Default Hello Timer | **5 sec** (LAN) / **60 sec** (NBMA) | | Hold Timer | **15 sec** (LAN) / **180 sec** (NBMA) | --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Google Maps with Backup Routes" Model > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** Think of EIGRP as a delivery driver using **Google Maps** with a critical feature — *cached offline alternatives*. - **Primary Route (Successor):** The fastest, currently active route — exactly like the suggested path in Google Maps. - **Backup Routes (Feasible Successors):** Pre-calculated alternate paths stored in memory — like Google's offline alternative routes. - **Topology Table:** Your mental map of the city — knowing *all possible paths*, not just the best one. - **DUAL Algorithm:** The brain that decides "If this road blocks, switch instantly to road B because I know it won't create a circular loop." When the driver hits a roadblock, EIGRP **does not wait** for new instructions; it switches immediately to the backup route. If no backup exists, the driver queries other drivers (neighbors) for help — this is the **Query/Reply** mechanism that makes EIGRP converge rapidly. --- ## 1.3 Why EIGRP + Subnetting is a KTU Lab Favorite > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to **demonstrate classless routing**, meaning subnet masks must be explicitly carried in routing updates. EIGRP is one of the few protocols that natively supports **VLSM (Variable Length Subnet Masking)** and **CIDR**, making it a perfect lab pairing. Without subnetting, an EIGRP configuration is trivial. KTU deliberately tests the **integration** because: 1. It validates student understanding of wildcard masks in `network` statements. 2. It tests EIGRP metric calculation over non-uniform subnet sizes. 3. It forces students to apply **VLSM**, an essential skill for the Networking theory paper. --- ## 1.4 EIGRP's Three Logical Tables (Must Memorize) | Table Name | Function | Key Show Command | |------------|----------|------------------| | **Neighbor Table** | Lists directly connected routers that exchange Hello packets | `show ip eigrp neighbors` | | **Topology Table** | Stores all routes learned from neighbors with FD/RD metrics | `show ip eigrp topology` | | **Routing Table** | Contains only the best (Successor) routes installed by DUAL | `show ip route eigrp` | # MODULE EXPERIMENT 2 — SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 EIGRP Operational Theory — Step-by-Step Logic Flow EIGRP operation can be decomposed into **six sequential phases**. KTU lab viva questions almost always touch on Phases 1, 3, and 6. ### Phase 1 — Neighbor Discovery - When a router's interface is enabled with EIGRP, it begins sending **multicast Hello packets** every 5 seconds to the reserved multicast group **224.0.0.10**. - A router receiving a Hello with matching **AS number, K-values, and subnet** replies with its own Hello and adds the sender to its **Neighbor Table**. ### Phase 2 — Adjacency Formation - Two-way communication is established when each router sees itself in the other's Hello packet. - The first time a neighbor is discovered, the router sends a **full update** containing its entire routing table. ### Phase 3 — Topology Exchange via RTP - EIGRP uses **Reliable Transport Protocol (RTP)** — a Cisco proprietary layer-4 protocol that guarantees ordered, reliable delivery of routing updates (using sequence numbers and acknowledgments). - Updates are **partial and bounded** — only changed routes are sent, and only to affected routers (not periodic flooding). ### Phase 4 — DUAL Algorithm Execution - The **Diffusing Update Algorithm** computes the **Feasible Distance (FD)** to every destination. - It identifies a loop-free **Successor** and pre-computes a backup **Feasible Successor**. ### Phase 5 — Query/Reply Diffusion - If the Successor fails and **no Feasible Successor exists**, the router enters the **Active state**. - It sends **Queries** to all neighbors asking for an alternate path. - Neighbors must respond with **Replies** (or their own Queries if they also lack a path). - This diffusion process guarantees loop-free convergence. ### Phase 6 — Route Installation - The best Successor route is moved from the **Topology Table** into the **Routing Table**. - Routes installed by EIGRP are marked with **`D`** in the Cisco IOS routing table (e.g., `D 192.168.10.0/26 [90/...]`). --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — EIGRP Metric Calculation > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Critical Formula:** The EIGRP composite metric is computed using five K-constants (K1 through K5). Cisco defaults are **K1 = 1, K3 = 1**, with all others = 0, which simplifies the master equation. ### Master EIGRP Metric Equation $$ \text{Metric} = \left[\frac{K_1 \cdot B + \dfrac{K_2 \cdot B}{256 - L} + K_3 \cdot D}{K_5 \cdot R + K_4}\right] \times 256 $$ ### Default Simplified Form (K2 = K4 = K5 = 0) $$ \text{Metric} = \left[\frac{B + D}{1}\right] \times 256 = (B + D) \times 256 $$ ### Component Substitutions $$ B = \frac{10^7}{\text{Minimum Bandwidth (kbps)}} $$ $$ D = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\text{delay}_i (\mu s)}{10} $$ ### Quick Reference — EIGRP K-Constant Table | K-Constant | Default Value | Controls Parameter | Engineering Meaning | |------------|---------------|--------------------|--------------------| | **K1** | **1** | Bandwidth (B) | Path throughput weight | | **K2** | **0** | Load (L) | Interface utilization weight | | **K3** | **1** | Delay (D) | Path latency weight | | **K4** | **0** | Reliability (R) | Path stability weight | | **K5** | **0** | MTU / Extra | Used only with K2 and K4 non-zero | > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall Callout:** If you change the K-values from defaults, the K-values **MUST match on every router** in the EIGRP autonomous system, or neighbor adjacencies will silently fail to form. This is a common KTU lab trap. --- ## 2.3 Feasible Distance vs. Reported Distance — The Heart of DUAL $$ \text{Feasible Distance (FD)} = \text{Lowest total metric from THIS router to destination} $$ $$ \text{Reported Distance (RD)} = \text{Metric as reported by a NEIGHBOR to the destination} $$ ### Feasibility Condition $$ \boxed{\text{A route qualifies as a Feasible Successor IF and ONLY IF } RD < FD_{\text{current successor}}} $$ This simple inequality **mathematically guarantees loop-free backup paths** — the foundation of DUAL's elegance. If a neighbor's distance to a destination is *less than your own best distance*, then that neighbor cannot be downstream of you in a loop. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | Why EIGRP is Used | |-------------|-------------------| | Enterprise Campus LANs | Fast convergence, easy configuration | | Data Center Interconnects | Supports unequal-cost load balancing | | Service Provider Edges | Hybrid nature suits mixed topologies | | Network Automation Labs | Predictable DUAL behavior, low CPU overhead | EIGRP was eventually **open-standardized in RFC 7868 (2016)** for multi-vendor interoperability, though the KTU lab context uses Cisco IOS exclusively. --- ## 2.5 Subnetting Theory Refresher (VLSM for EIGRP Lab) > [!NOTE] > **VLSM = Variable Length Subnet Masking.** Since EIGRP carries subnet masks in updates, VLSM works seamlessly. EIGRP requires that all interfaces on a subnet share the same mask — but different subnets in the same network can have different masks. The standard subnetting steps for any lab design: 1. Identify the **largest host requirement** first (greedy allocation). 2. Subtract in powers of 2 (round up to the next power of 2). 3. Assign **largest to smallest**, leaving room for WAN point-to-point links. 4. Always reserve a /30 for each WAN link. # MODULE EXPERIMENT 2 — SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Reference Network Topology Used Throughout This Module We will use this topology for all derivations, configurations, and exam questions: > **Network Block:** 192.168.10.0 /24 | Subnet Purpose | Hosts Needed | Subnet Mask | CIDR | Network Address | Broadcast Address | Usable Range | |----------------|--------------|-------------|------|-----------------|-------------------|--------------| | **LAN-3 (R3 side)** | 50 | 255.255.255.192 | **/26** | 192.168.10.0 | 192.168.10.63 | .1 – .62 | | **LAN-2 (R2 side)** | 20 | 255.255.255.224 | **/27** | 192.168.10.64 | 192.168.10.95 | .65 – .94 | | **LAN-1 (R1 side)** | 10 | 255.255.255.240 | **/28** | 192.168.10.96 | 192.168.10.111 | .97 – .110 | | **WAN-1 (R1 ↔ R2)** | 2 | 255.255.255.252 | **/30** | 192.168.10.112 | 192.168.10.115 | .113 – .114 | | **WAN-2 (R2 ↔ R3)** | 2 | 255.255.255.252 | **/30** | 192.168.10.116 | 192.168.10.119 | .117 – .118 | | **Reserved / Spare** | — | 255.255.255.252 | **/30** | 192.168.10.120 | 192.168.10.255 | (Future use) | ### IP Address Allocation Matrix | Device | Interface | IP Address | Subnet Mask | Default Gateway | |--------|-----------|------------|-------------|-----------------| | **R1** | Fa0/0 (LAN-1) | 192.168.10.97 | 255.255.255.240 | — | | **R1** | Se0/0/0 (WAN-1) | 192.168.10.113 | 255.255.255.252 | — | | **R2** | Fa0/0 (LAN-2) | 192.168.10.65 | 255.255.255.224 | — | | **R2** | Se0/0/0 (WAN-1) | 192.168.10.114 | 255.255.255.252 | — | | **R2** | Se0/0/1 (WAN-2) | 192.168.10.117 | 255.255.255.252 | — | | **R3** | Fa0/0 (LAN-3) | 192.168.10.1 | 255.255.255.192 | — | | **R3** | Se0/0/1 (WAN-2) | 192.168.10.118 | 255.255.255.252 | — | ### Wildcard Mask Calculation (Inverse of Subnet Mask) $$ \text{Wildcard Mask} = 255.255.255.255 - \text{Subnet Mask} $$ | Subnet Mask | Wildcard Mask | |-------------|---------------| | 255.255.255.252 (/30) | 0.0.0.3 | | 255.255.255.240 (/28) | 0.0.0.15 | | 255.255.255.224 (/27) | 0.0.0.31 | | 255.255.255.192 (/26) | 0.0.0.63 | --- ## 3.2 Exhaustive EIGRP Metric Calculation — Step-by-Step Derivation > [!NOTE] > **Objective:** Calculate the EIGRP metric from **R1 to LAN-3 (192.168.10.0/26)** using default K-values. ### Interface Parameters Provided (Hypothetical) | Link | Bandwidth | Delay | |------|-----------|-------| | R1 Fa0/0 (LAN-1) | 100,000 kbps | 100 µs | | R1 Se0/0/0 ↔ R2 Se0/0/0 (WAN-1) | 1,544 kbps | 20,000 µs | | R2 Se0/0/1 ↔ R3 Se0/0/1 (WAN-2) | 1,544 kbps | 20,000 µs | | R3 Fa0/0 (LAN-3) | 100,000 kbps | 100 µs | ### Step 1 — Identify the Minimum Bandwidth Along the Path The path traverses R1 → WAN-1 → R2 → WAN-2 → R3 → LAN-3. $$ \text{Min Bandwidth} = \min(100000, 1544, 1544, 100000) = 1544 \text{ kbps} $$ ### Step 2 — Compute the Throughput Component (B) $$ B = \frac{10^7}{\text{Min Bandwidth (kbps)}} = \frac{10{,}000{,}000}{1544} $$ $$ B = 6476.6839... $$ ### Step 3 — Sum All Delays Along the Path (in microseconds) $$ \text{Total Delay} = 100 + 20000 + 20000 + 100 = 40{,}200 \; \mu s $$ ### Step 4 — Convert Delay to D (tens of microseconds) $$ D = \frac{\text{Total Delay}}{10} = \frac{40200}{10} = 4020 $$ ### Step 5 — Apply the Simplified EIGRP Metric Formula $$ \text{Metric} = (B + D) \times 256 $$ $$ \text{Metric} = (6476.6839 + 4020) \times 256 $$ $$ \text{Metric} = 10496.6839 \times 256 $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Metric} \approx 2{,}687{,}151} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** Notice how the **T1 WAN links (1544 kbps)** dominate the metric. The 100 Mbps LAN segments contribute almost nothing because the formula uses the **minimum** (bottleneck) bandwidth, not the sum. This is why EIGRP prefers high-throughput paths even if they have more hops. --- ## 3.3 Configuration Derivation — Router-by-Router Commands ### Router R1 Configuration ``` Router> enable Router# configure terminal Router(config)# hostname R1 R1(config)# interface FastEthernet0/0 R1(config-if)# ip address 192.168.10.97 255.255.255.240 R1(config-if)# no shutdown R1(config-if)# exit R1(config)# interface Serial0/0/0 R1(config-if)# ip address 192.168.10.113 255.255.255.252 R1(config-if)# clock rate 64000 R1(config-if)# no shutdown R1(config-if)# exit R1(config)# router eigrp 100 R1(config-router)# network 192.168.10.96 0.0.0.15 R1(config-router)# network 192.168.10.112 0.0.0.3 R1(config-router)# no auto-summary R1(config-router)# end R1# write memory ``` ### Router R2 Configuration ``` Router> enable Router# configure terminal Router(config)# hostname R2 R2(config)# interface FastEthernet0/0 R2(config-if)# ip address 192.168.10.65 255.255.255.224 R2(config-if)# no shutdown R2(config-if)# exit R2(config)# interface Serial0/0/0 R2(config-if)# ip address 192.168.10.114 255.255.255.252 R2(config-if)# no shutdown R2(config-if)# exit R2(config)# interface Serial0/0/1 R2(config-if)# ip address 192.168.10.117 255.255.255.252 R2(config-if)# clock rate 64000 R2(config-if)# no shutdown R2(config-if)# exit R2(config)# router eigrp 100 R2(config-router)# network 192.168.10.64 0.0.0.31 R2(config-router)# network 192.168.10.112 0.0.0.3 R2(config-router)# network 192.168.10.116 0.0.0.3 R2(config-router)# no auto-summary R2(config-router)# end R2# write memory ``` ### Router R3 Configuration ``` Router> enable Router# configure terminal Router(config)# hostname R3 R3(config)# interface FastEthernet0/0 R3(config-if)# ip address 192.168.10.1 255.255.255.192 R3(config-if)# no shutdown R3(config-if)# exit R3(config)# interface Serial0/0/1 R3(config-if)# ip address 192.168.10.118 255.255.255.252 R3(config-if)# no shutdown R3(config-if)# exit R3(config)# router eigrp 100 R3(config-router)# network 192.168.10.0 0.0.0.63 R3(config-router)# network 192.168.10.116 0.0.0.3 R3(config-router)# no auto-summary R3(config-router)# end R3# write memory ``` --- ## 3.4 Verification & Troubleshooting Analytical Matrix | Verification Goal | Command | Expected Output Indicator | |-------------------|---------|---------------------------| | Confirm neighbor adjacency | `show ip eigrp neighbors` | "H" column = 0 to 2 (Hold time) | | Display topology table | `show ip eigrp topology` | Codes: `P` = Passive, `A` = Active | | View EIGRP routes only | `show ip route eigrp` | Routes prefixed with `D` | | Confirm EIGRP process details | `show ip protocols` | AS number, K-values, neighbor list | | Verify interface EIGRP state | `show ip eigrp interfaces` | Per-interface Hello/Queue stats | | Debug neighbor formation | `debug eigrp packets` | Live Hello/Update/Query packets | ### Sample Expected Verification Output (R1) ``` R1# show ip eigrp neighbors IP-EIGRP neighbors for process 100 H Address Interface Hold Uptime SRTT RTO Q Seq (sec) (ms) Cnt Num 0 192.168.10.114 Se0/0/0 12 00:05:23 40 240 0 12 ``` ``` R1# show ip route eigrp D 192.168.10.0/26 [90/2687151] via 192.168.10.114, 00:02:11, Serial0/0/0 D 192.168.10.64/27 [90/21024000] via 192.168.10.114, 00:02:11, Serial0/0/0 D 192.168.10.116/30 [90/21024000] via 192.168.10.114, 00:02:11, Serial0/0/0 ``` > [!NOTE] > **The `[90/...]` notation:** `90` = Administrative Distance of EIGRP, the second number (e.g., `2687151`) is the **composite metric** we calculated in Section 3.2. # MODULE EXPERIMENT 2 — SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Network Topology Diagram (Mermaid — Lab Reference) ```mermaid graph TD PC1A[PC1 LAN1 192.168.10.98] PC1B[PC2 LAN1 192.168.10.99] SW1A[Switch SW1] R1A[Router R1
FA0 0 192.168.10.97 28
SE0 0 0 192.168.10.113 30] R2A[Router R2
FA0 0 192.168.10.65 27
SE0 0 0 192.168.10.114 30
SE0 0 1 192.168.10.117 30] R3A[Router R3
FA0 0 192.168.10.1 26
SE0 0 1 192.168.10.118 30] SW2A[Switch SW2] SW3A[Switch SW3] PC2A[PC1 LAN2 192.168.10.66] PC2B[PC2 LAN2 192.168.10.67] PC3A[PC1 LAN3 192.168.10.2] PC3B[PC2 LAN3 192.168.10.3] PC1A --> SW1A PC1B --> SW1A SW1A --> R1A R1A -- WAN1 112 to 115 30 --> R2A R2A -- WAN2 116 to 119 30 --> R3A R2A --> SW2A SW2A --> PC2A SW2A --> PC2B R3A --> SW3A SW3A --> PC3A SW3A --> PC3B ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Solid lines represent Ethernet LANs or Serial WANs. Each label shows the subnet range and CIDR mask for that segment. Routers R1, R2, R3 form the EIGRP AS 100 backbone. --- ## 4.2 EIGRP Operational State Machine — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid graph TD State1[State 1 Neighbor Discovery
Multicast Hello to 224.0.0.10] State2[State 2 Adjacency Up
Neighbor Table Updated] State3[State 3 Full Topology Exchange
RTP Reliable Updates] State4[State 4 DUAL Computation
FD RD Calculation] State5[State 5 Successor Identified
Feasible Successor Cached] State6[State 6 Passive State
Route Installed in Routing Table] QueryPath[Query Diffusion
Active State] ReplyPath[Reply Received
Return to Passive] State1 --> State2 State2 --> State3 State3 --> State4 State4 --> State5 State5 --> State6 State6 -.Link Failure Detected.-> QueryPath QueryPath --> ReplyPath ReplyPath --> State4 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** The **Passive** state means EIGRP is satisfied — the route is stable. The **Active** state (Query path) is *only* entered when both Successor and Feasible Successor are unavailable. Stuck-in-Active (SIA) is a real-world fault scenario KTU students must recognize. --- ## 4.3 EIGRP Packet Types — Functional Block Diagram ```mermaid graph LR EIGRP[EIGRP Process] HelloPkt[Hello Packet
Neighbor Discovery 5s] UpdatePkt[Update Packet
Route Information] QueryPkt[Query Packet
Seeking Alternate Path] ReplyPkt[Reply Packet
Response to Query] AckPkt[ACK Packet
Reliable Delivery Confirm] MCast[Multicast 224.0.0.10] UCast[Unicast to Neighbor] EIGRP --> HelloPkt EIGRP --> UpdatePkt EIGRP --> QueryPkt EIGRP --> ReplyPkt EIGRP --> AckPkt HelloPkt --> MCast UpdatePkt -. Partial Bound Updates .-> MCast UpdatePkt -. Initial Full Update .-> UCast QueryPkt --> UCast ReplyPkt --> UCast AckPkt --> UCast ``` --- ## 4.4 DUAL Finite State Machine — Feasibility Decision Flow ```mermaid graph TD Start[Router Receives Topology Update] CalcFD[Calculate Feasible Distance FD] CheckFS{Feasible Successor Exists RD less than FD?} InstallRT[Install Successor in Routing Table] SendQuery[Send EIGRP Queries to All Neighbors] WaitReply[Wait for Replies in Active State] NewSuccessor[Compute New Successor from Replies] SIA[Stuck in Active Timer 180s Failure] Start --> CalcFD CalcFD --> CheckFS CheckFS -- Yes --> InstallRT CheckFS -- No --> SendQuery SendQuery --> WaitReply WaitReply -- Reply Received --> NewSuccessor WaitReply -- Timeout --> SIA NewSuccessor --> InstallRT ``` # MODULE EXPERIMENT 2 — SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **List any six key features of EIGRP that distinguish it from classical distance-vector protocols like RIP.** **Model Answer (6 points):** 1. **Hybrid nature** — Combines distance-vector simplicity with link-state efficiency through DUAL. 2. **Classless operation** — Carries subnet masks, supports VLSM and CIDR. 3. **Rapid convergence** — Pre-computed Feasible Successors eliminate query storms for most failures. 4. **Bounded, partial updates** — Sends only changed routes, only to affected routers (not periodic flooding). 5. **Multiple network layer support** — Uses Protocol Dependent Modules (PDM) for IP, IPv6, IPX, AppleTalk. 6. **Unequal-cost load balancing** — Supports up to 4 paths by default (configurable up to 16) via `variance` command. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between Feasible Distance (FD) and Reported Distance (RD) in EIGRP with a numerical example.** **Model Answer (3 points):** | Term | Definition | |------|------------| | **Feasible Distance (FD)** | The *lowest total metric* from the **local router** to the destination network. | | **Reported Distance (RD)** | The metric as **advertised by a neighbor** to reach the destination (excludes local link cost). | **Example:** R1 → R2 → LAN-X - R2 advertises LAN-X with metric 1000. So **RD from R2 = 1000**. - The link R1→R2 adds cost 500. So R1's **FD = 1000 + 500 = 1500**. - A Feasible Successor exists if any other neighbor's RD < 1500 (the local FD). --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – July 2024]** **Design an EIGRP-enabled network using the address block 192.168.20.0/24 with the following requirements:** - Three routers R1, R2, R3 connected in a linear topology. - LAN-A connected to R1 needs **60 hosts**. - LAN-B connected to R2 needs **30 hosts**. - LAN-C connected to R3 needs **12 hosts**. - Each WAN link must use the smallest possible subnet. - Use **EIGRP AS 100**, disable auto-summary. #### **(a) Perform VLSM subnetting and assign IP addresses to all router interfaces. (7 marks)** #### **(b) Write the complete EIGRP configuration for all three routers, including verification commands. (7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution — Question A** #### **Part (a) — VLSM Subnetting (7 marks)** **Step 1: Sort requirements in descending order (Greedy VLSM algorithm).** | Subnet | Hosts Needed | Block Size | CIDR | Subnet Mask | Network | Range | Broadcast | |--------|--------------|------------|------|-------------|---------|-------|-----------| | LAN-A | 60 | 64 | **/26** | 255.255.255.192 | 192.168.20.0 | .1–.62 | 192.168.20.63 | | LAN-B | 30 | 32 | **/27** | 255.255.255.224 | 192.168.20.64 | .65–.94 | 192.168.20.95 | | LAN-C | 12 | 16 | **/28** | 255.255.255.240 | 192.168.20.96 | .97–.110 | 192.168.20.111 | | WAN-1 | 2 | 4 | **/30** | 255.255.255.252 | 192.168.20.112 | .113–.114 | 192.168.20.115 | | WAN-2 | 2 | 4 | **/30** | 255.255.255.252 | 192.168.20.116 | .117–.118 | 192.168.20.119 | **Block size formula:** $2^h \geq \text{Hosts Needed}$, where $h$ = host bits, CIDR = $32 - h$. **Step 2: IP Address Assignment Matrix (3 marks for table)** | Device | Interface | IP Address | Subnet Mask | Wildcard Mask | |--------|-----------|------------|-------------|---------------| | R1 | Fa0/0 (LAN-A) | 192.168.20.1 | 255.255.255.192 | 0.0.0.63 | | R1 | Se0/0/0 (WAN-1) | 192.168.20.113 | 255.255.255.252 | 0.0.0.3 | | R2 | Fa0/0 (LAN-B) | 192.168.20.65 | 255.255.255.224 | 0.0.0.31 | | R2 | Se0/0/0 (WAN-1) | 192.168.20.114 | 255.255.255.252 | 0.0.0.3 | | R2 | Se0/0/1 (WAN-2) | 192.168.20.117 | 255.255.255.252 | 0.0.0.3 | | R3 | Fa0/0 (LAN-C) | 192.168.20.97 | 255.255.255.240 | 0.0.0.15 | | R3 | Se0/0/1 (WAN-2) | 192.168.20.118 | 255.255.255.252 | 0.0.0.3 | --- #### **Part (b) — Complete EIGRP Configuration (7 marks)** **Router R1 Configuration (2 marks):** ``` Router> enable Router# configure terminal Router(config)# hostname R1 R1(config)# interface FastEthernet0/0 R1(config-if)# ip address 192.168.20.1 255.255.255.192 R1(config-if)# no shutdown R1(config-if)# exit R1(config)# interface Serial0/0/0 R1(config-if)# ip address 192.168.20.113 255.255.255.252 R1(config-if)# clock rate 64000 R1(config-if)# no shutdown R1(config-if)# exit R1(config)# router eigrp 100 R1(config-router)# network 192.168.20.0 0.0.0.63 R1(config-router)# network 192.168.20.112 0.0.0.3 R1(config-router)# no auto-summary R1(config-router)# end R1# write memory ``` **Router R2 Configuration (2 marks):** ``` Router> enable Router# configure terminal Router(config)# hostname R2 R2(config)# interface FastEthernet0/0 R2(config-if)# ip address 192.168.20.65 255.255.255.224 R2(config-if)# no shutdown R2(config-if)# exit R2(config)# interface Serial0/0/0 R2(config-if)# ip address 192.168.20.114 255.255.255.252 R2(config-if)# no shutdown R2(config-if)# exit R2(config)# interface Serial0/0/1 R2(config-if)# ip address 192.168.20.117 255.255.255.252 R2(config-if)# clock rate 64000 R2(config-if)# no shutdown R2(config-if)# exit R2(config)# router eigrp 100 R2(config-router)# network 192.168.20.64 0.0.0.31 R2(config-router)# network 192.168.20.112 0.0.0.3 R2(config-router)# network 192.168.20.116 0.0.0.3 R2(config-router)# no auto-summary R2(config-router)# end R2# write memory ``` **Router R3 Configuration (2 marks):** ``` Router> enable Router# configure terminal Router(config)# hostname R3 R3(config)# interface FastEthernet0/0 R3(config-if)# ip address 192.168.20.97 255.255.255.240 R3(config-if)# no shutdown R3(config-if)# exit R3(config)# interface Serial0/0/1 R3(config-if)# ip address 192.168.20.118 255.255.255.252 R3(config-if)# no shutdown R3(config-if)# exit R3(config)# router eigrp 100 R3(config-router)# network 192.168.20.96 0.0.0.15 R3(config-router)# network 192.168.20.116 0.0.0.3 R3(config-router)# no auto-summary R3(config-router)# end R3# write memory ``` **Verification Commands (1 mark):** ``` R1# show ip eigrp neighbors R1# show ip eigrp topology R1# show ip route eigrp R1# show ip protocols R1# ping 192.168.20.118 ! End-to-end connectivity test R1# traceroute 192.168.20.97 ``` --- ### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – July 2024 Alternative]** **For the network topology designed in Question A:** #### **(a) Calculate the EIGRP composite metric from R1 to LAN-C (192.168.20.96/28) using default K-values. Assume LAN interfaces have bandwidth 100 Mbps and delay 100 µs; WAN links have bandwidth 1544 kbps and delay 20,000 µs. (7 marks)** #### **(b) Explain the EIGRP DUAL algorithm's response when the Successor link R1→R2 fails and no Feasible Successor exists. List the exact packet exchange that occurs and the maximum time before SIA (Stuck-in-Active) is declared. (7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution — Question B** #### **Part (a) — EIGRP Metric Calculation (7 marks)** **Step 1: Identify the path and link parameters.** Path: R1 → WAN-1 → R2 → WAN-2 → R3 → LAN-C | Link | Bandwidth (kbps) | Delay (µs) | |------|------------------|------------| | R1 Fa0/0 | 100,000 | 100 | | WAN-1 (Se0/0/0) | 1,544 | 20,000 | | WAN-2 (Se0/0/1) | 1,544 | 20,000 | | R3 Fa0/0 (LAN-C) | 100,000 | 100 | **Step 2: Find minimum bandwidth.** $$ \text{Min Bandwidth} = \min(100000, 1544, 1544, 100000) = 1544 \text{ kbps} $$ **Step 3: Compute throughput B.** $$ B = \frac{10^7}{1544} = 6476.6839 $$ **Step 4: Sum delays and convert to D.** $$ \text{Total Delay} = 100 + 20000 + 20000 + 100 = 40200 \; \mu s $$ $$ D = \frac{40200}{10} = 4020 $$ **Step 5: Apply the simplified formula.** $$ \text{Metric} = (B + D) \times 256 = (6476.6839 + 4020) \times 256 $$ $$ \text{Metric} = 10496.6839 \times 256 \approx \boxed{2{,}687{,}151} $$ --- #### **Part (b) — DUAL Active State Behaviour (7 marks)** **Scenario:** The R1↔R2 Serial link (Se0/0/0) fails. R1's Successor to all destinations beyond R2 is lost. R1 checks its topology table for any Feasible Successor. **Assume none exists.** **Step-by-Step DUAL Behaviour (5 marks):** 1. R1 marks the route as **Active** in its topology table. 2. R1 sends an **EIGRP Query packet** (multicast 224.0.0.10) to all remaining neighbors asking for an alternate path. 3. Each neighbor runs DUAL locally: - If they have a Feasible Successor, they reply immediately with an **EIGRP Reply** containing the metric. - If they also lack a Feasible Successor, they propagate Queries further (this is the "diffusion" property). 4. R1 waits for all replies. Once all are received, R1 computes the new lowest FD from the reply set. 5. R1 installs the new Successor and returns to **Passive state**. **Stuck-in-Active (SIA) Timer (2 marks):** $$ \text{SIA Timer} = 3 \times \text{Active Timer} = 3 \times 180 \text{ seconds} = 540 \text{ seconds (3 minutes)} $$ - The default **Active Timer is 180 seconds**. - If a neighbor fails to reply within this window, R1 resets the neighbor relationship (logged as `%DUAL-5-NBRCHANGE`). - This prevents indefinite query propagation — a critical reliability mechanism. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions:** > 1. **Forgetting `no auto-summary`** in the configuration → **−1 mark** (without it, EIGRP behaves classfully and breaks VLSM). > 2. **Using subnet mask instead of wildcard mask** in `network` statements → **−2 marks** (R1 will not advertise the correct range). > 3. **Mismatched AS numbers** between routers → adjacency fails silently, students often blame cables. > 4. **Forgetting `clock rate`** on the DCE serial interface → WAN link stays "down/down" → **−1 mark**. > 5. **Calculating delay in microseconds instead of tens of microseconds** → final metric is off by a factor of 10 → **−2 marks**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute KTU Revision Card — Memorize Before Exam** - [x] EIGRP = **Hybrid protocol** using **DUAL** algorithm. - [x] **Administrative Distance = 90** (internal) / **170** (external). - [x] EIGRP supports **VLSM and CIDR** (classless — subnet mask in updates). - [x] **Multicast address 224.0.0.10** for Hello and updates. - [x] **Default K-values:** K1=1, K3=1, all others=0 → simplified formula applies. - [x] Metric uses **minimum bandwidth** (bottleneck) and **sum of delays** in tens of microseconds. - [x] **Feasibility Condition:** RD < FD (strictly less than) → guarantees loop-free backup. - [x] **Three tables:** Neighbor, Topology (DUAL), Routing. - [x] **Five packet types:** Hello, Update, Query, Reply, ACK. - [x] **Two states:** Passive (stable) and Active (querying). - [x] **SIA timer = 180s active + 180s retry ≈ 3 minutes total**. - [x] Always use **`router eigrp `** with matching AS on all routers. - [x] Always disable **`no auto-summary`** for classless operation. - [x] **`network` command uses WILDCARD mask**, not subnet mask. - [x] **Verification commands:** `show ip eigrp neighbors`, `show ip eigrp topology`, `show ip route eigrp`. - [x] **Wildcard = 255.255.255.255 − Subnet Mask** (bitwise inversion). - [x] **Greedy VLSM:** Allocate largest subnet first to avoid fragmentation. - [x] **EIGRP route code in routing table = `D`** (e.g., `D 192.168.20.0/26 [90/2687151]`). - [x] **Hello Timer:** 5s (LAN), 60s (NBMA); **Hold Timer:** 15s, 180s respectively. - [x] **Unequal-cost load balancing** with `variance ` command (default = 1, i.e., equal cost). - [x] **RTP = Reliable Transport Protocol**, proprietary to EIGRP for guaranteed ordered delivery. --- **End of Module Experiment 2 — KTU 2024 Scheme Notes**

topics.title: Configure and verify Open Shortest Path First (OSPF) routing in a network that includes subnetting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCITL407 - COMPUTER NETWORKS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Configure and verify Open Shortest Path First (OSPF) routing in a network that includes subnetting # 🔷 MODULE EXPERIMENT 2 — OSPF ROUTING WITH SUBNETTING ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **OSPF (Open Shortest Path First)** is a **link-state, classless, hierarchical Interior Gateway Protocol (IGP)** defined in **RFC 2328** for IPv4. It operates within a single **Autonomous System (AS)**, uses the **Dijkstra Shortest Path First (SPF) algorithm** to compute the loop-free topology map, and assigns a **cost metric** to each interface. When combined with **VLSM (Variable Length Subnet Masking)** subnetting, OSPF carries subnet mask information in its Link-State Advertisements (LSAs), enabling **non-uniform subnet allocation** and optimal address conservation in enterprise networks. **Key Standard Metrics (KTU Board Frequently Asked):** - **Default Reference Bandwidth:** **100 Mbps** (configurable via `auto-cost reference-bandwidth`) - **OSPF Cost Range:** **1 to 65,535** (integer, minimum 1) - **OSPF Administrative Distance:** **110** - **Protocol Number (IP):** **89** - **Multicast Addresses:** **224.0.0.5** (AllSPFRouters) and **224.0.0.6** (AllDRouters) ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "GPS Navigator" of Networks > [!NOTE] > **Think of OSPF like Google Maps for a city's road network.** > > 1. **Each Router = A Smartphone** constantly broadcasting its own location (Link-State Advertisements). > 2. **The OSPF Database (LSDB) = The Shared Map** — every smartphone receives every other phone's location, building an identical, complete map of the city. > 3. **Dijkstra's Algorithm = The Routing Engine** that calculates the *shortest* path from your current location to your destination based on real-time traffic (cost). > 4. **Areas = City Zones** — to prevent the map from getting too massive, the city is divided into zones, with border routers (ABRs) summarizing traffic between zones. > 5. **VLSM Subnetting = Postal Codes of Varying Density** — a dense downtown has 6-digit codes (smaller subnets, /26), while rural outskirts use 4-digit codes (larger subnets, /24), saving addresses and routing table size. ### 1.3 What is Subnetting in the OSPF Context? Subnetting (specifically **VLSM**) divides a large network into smaller sub-subnetworks of *different* sizes to match host requirements exactly. Because OSPF is **classless**, it includes the subnet mask in its routing updates, allowing the simultaneous advertisement of `192.168.10.0/26`, `192.168.10.64/27`, and `192.168.10.128/30` without summarization issues. > [!TIP] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** OSPF's classless nature is the primary reason it is preferred over the legacy classful protocol **RIPv1** in modern VLSM networks. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 OSPF Operational Architecture — The 5 Logic Pillars ### Pillar 1: Neighbor Discovery & Adjacency Formation - Routers send **OSPF Hello packets** every **10 seconds** (Broadcast) or **30 seconds** (NBMA) on each enabled interface to multicast address `224.0.0.5`. - Hello packets contain: **Router ID, Hello/Dead intervals, Area ID, Subnet Mask, DR/BDR priority, and list of known neighbors**. - **Dead Interval:** 4 × Hello = **40 seconds** (default) before a silent neighbor is declared down. ### Pillar 2: Link-State Database (LSDB) Synchronization - After becoming neighbors, routers exchange **Database Description (DBD)** packets — essentially a "table of contents" of their LSAs. - **Link-State Request (LSR)** packets ask for missing LSAs. - **Link-State Update (LSU)** packets deliver the requested LSAs. - **Link-State Acknowledgment (LSAck)** confirms receipt. ### Pillar 3: SPF (Dijkstra) Calculation - Each router runs Dijkstra's algorithm against its synchronized LSDB. - The algorithm builds a **Shortest Path Tree (SPT)** rooted at the local router. - Result: A loop-free routing table populated via the SPT. ### Pillar 4: LSA Flooding & Aging - LSAs are flooded every **30 minutes (1800 seconds)** as a refresh. - A router detecting a topology change (link up/down) immediately floods a new LSA. - **MaxAge** = **3600 seconds (1 hour)** — if not refreshed, the LSA is purged. ### Pillar 5: Hierarchical Area Design - **Area 0 (Backbone):** The central area to which all other areas must connect. - **Area Border Router (ABR):** Router connecting Area 0 to a non-backbone area. - **Autonomous System Boundary Router (ASBR):** Router injecting external routes (e.g., from BGP or static routes) into OSPF. - **Stub Area:** Blocks external (Type 5) LSAs to reduce LSDB size. ## 2.2 OSPF Packet Types — The 5 LSA Family Members | Type Code | LSA Name | Flooding Scope | Function | |:---------:|:---------|:--------------:|:---------| | **Type 1** | Router LSA | Within Area | Generated by every router; describes router's links and state | | **Type 2** | Network LSA | Within Area | Generated by Designated Router (DR) on multi-access networks | | **Type 3** | Summary LSA | Between Areas | Generated by ABR; summarizes networks from one area to another | | **Type 4** | ASBR Summary LSA | Between Areas | Points to the ASBR | | **Type 5** | AS External LSA | Throughout AS | Generated by ASBR for routes redistributed into OSPF | ## 2.3 The 7 OSPF Adjacency States (Examiner's Favorite) | State | Meaning | |:------|:--------| | **Down** | No Hello received; initial state | | **Init** | Hello received from neighbor, but our Router ID is NOT in the neighbor's list | | **2-Way** | Bi-directional communication established; DR/BDR election occurs here | | **Exstart** | Master/Slave negotiation for DBD exchange | | **Exchange** | DBD packets sent; LSDB synchronization begins | | **Loading** | LSRs sent for missing LSAs; LSUs received | | **Full** | LSDBs are completely synchronized — adjacency complete ✅ | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — OSPF Cost & Subnet Calculations > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Golden Equations"** you MUST memorize for the KTU Board Exam. | # | Formula | Description | Units / Boundary | |:-:|:--------|:------------|:-----------------| | 1 | $\text{Cost} = \dfrac{\text{Reference Bandwidth}}{\text{Interface Bandwidth}}$ | OSPF interface cost | Mbps; min **1**, max **65,535** | | 2 | $\text{Total Path Cost} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Cost}_i$ | Sum of outgoing interface costs along path | — | | 3 | $\text{Usable Hosts} = 2^h - 2$ | Hosts per subnet (where $h$ = host bits) | Subtract 1 for network, 1 for broadcast | | 4 | $\text{Subnets Created} = 2^s$ | Number of subnets from borrowing $s$ bits | — | | 5 | $\text{Subnet Increment} = 256 - \text{Decimal Octet of Mask}$ | Block size for last octet | Used in Class C sub-netting | | 6 | $\text{New Mask} = \text{Old Mask} + s$ | After borrowing $s$ host bits | In CIDR prefix length | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility OSPF is the **de facto enterprise routing standard** in: - **University campus networks** (Kerala's IHRD, NIT Calicut deployments). - **Banking core networks** requiring sub-second convergence. - **ISP internal backbones** for scalable, hierarchical design. - Combined with VLSM, it prevents the infamous IPv4 address exhaustion that plagued the 1990s. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix & Subnetting Walkthrough ## 3.1 Worked Example — VLSM Subnetting of 192.168.20.0/24 **Requirement Table (typical KTU lab problem):** | Subnet | Location | Hosts Required | Subnet Mask | CIDR | |:------:|:---------|:--------------:|:-----------:|:----:| | LAN-A | R1 Ethernet0/0 | 60 | 255.255.255.192 | /26 | | LAN-B | R2 Ethernet0/0 | 30 | 255.255.255.224 | /27 | | LAN-C | R3 Ethernet0/0 | 12 | 255.255.255.240 | /28 | | WAN-AB | R1-R2 Serial0/0/0 | 2 | 255.255.255.252 | /30 | | WAN-BC | R2-R3 Serial0/0/1 | 2 | 255.255.255.252 | /30 | | WAN-CA | R3-R1 Serial0/0/1 | 2 | 255.255.255.252 | /30 | ### 3.2 Exhaustive VLSM Allocation Steps **Step 1: Sort requirements in DESCENDING order (largest first).** LAN-A (60) → LAN-B (30) → LAN-C (12) → WAN-AB (2) → WAN-BC (2) → WAN-CA (2) **Step 2: Allocate LAN-A.** - Need 60 hosts → borrow bits: $2^h - 2 \ge 60 \Rightarrow h = 6$ - New prefix: $24 + 2 = 26$ → mask /26 - Block size: $2^{(32-26)} = 64$ - Subnet: **192.168.20.0 / 26** (range .0 – .63, usable .1 – .62) **Step 3: Allocate LAN-B.** - Need 30 hosts → $2^h - 2 \ge 30 \Rightarrow h = 5$ - New prefix: $24 + 3 = 27$ → mask /27 - Block size: 32 - Subnet: **192.168.20.64 / 27** (range .64 – .95, usable .65 – .94) **Step 4: Allocate LAN-C.** - Need 12 hosts → $2^h - 2 \ge 12 \Rightarrow h = 4$ - New prefix: $24 + 4 = 28$ → mask /28 - Block size: 16 - Subnet: **192.168.20.96 / 28** (range .96 – .111, usable .97 – .110) **Step 5: Allocate WAN-AB (point-to-point).** - Need 2 hosts → $2^h - 2 \ge 2 \Rightarrow h = 2$ - New prefix: $24 + 6 = 30$ → mask /30 - Block size: 4 - Subnet: **192.168.20.112 / 30** (usable .113 – .114) **Step 6: Allocate WAN-BC.** - Subnet: **192.168.20.116 / 30** (usable .117 – .118) **Step 7: Allocate WAN-CA.** - Subnet: **192.168.20.120 / 30** (usable .121 – .122) ## 3.3 OSPF Cost Derivation for Each Interface Using $\text{Cost} = \dfrac{\text{Reference BW}}{\text{Interface BW}}$ with reference = **100 Mbps**: $$ \text{Cost}_{\text{FastEthernet}} = \frac{100 \text{ Mbps}}{100 \text{ Mbps}} = 1 $$ $$ \text{Cost}_{\text{GigabitEthernet}} = \frac{100}{1000} = 0.1 \longrightarrow \text{rounded to } 1 $$ $$ \text{Cost}_{\text{Serial T1 (1.544 Mbps)}} = \frac{100}{1.544} \approx 64 $$ $$ \text{Cost}_{\text{Serial 128 kbps}} = \frac{100}{0.128} = 781.25 \longrightarrow \text{rounded to } 781 $$ > [!NOTE] > **The 100 Mbps ceiling problem:** For modern Gigabit links, every interface gets cost = 1, making OSPF unable to distinguish them. KTU examiners often ask how to fix this — the answer is `auto-cost reference-bandwidth 1000` (or higher) under the OSPF process. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Path Cost Computation For the triangle topology R1–R2–R3, if all WAN links are Serial T1: | Destination | Via | Total Path Cost | |:------------|:----|:---------------:| | R2 from R1 | Direct WAN-AB | 64 | | R2 from R1 | Via R3 (WAN-CA + WAN-BC) | 64 + 64 = 128 | | **R2 from R1** | **Chosen (lowest cost) → R1 → R2 direct** | **64** ✅ | ## 3.5 Configuration Walkthrough (Cisco IOS Syntax) **Router R1 Configuration:** ``` enable configure terminal hostname R1 ! interface FastEthernet0/0 ip address 192.168.20.1 255.255.255.192 no shutdown ! interface Serial0/0/0 ip address 192.168.20.113 255.255.255.252 clock rate 64000 no shutdown ! interface Serial0/0/1 ip address 192.168.20.121 255.255.255.252 no shutdown ! router ospf 1 router-id 1.1.1.1 network 192.168.20.0 0.0.0.63 area 0 network 192.168.20.112 0.0.0.3 area 0 network 192.168.20.120 0.0.0.3 area 0 end write memory ``` **Verification Commands (Examiner's Verification Checklist):** | Command | Purpose | |:--------|:--------| | `show ip ospf neighbor` | Confirm **Full/DR** adjacency states | | `show ip ospf interface brief` | Verify **cost**, **Network Type**, **Area** on each interface | | `show ip protocols` | Confirm **OSPF 1**, **110** AD, areas, passive interfaces | | `show ip route ospf` | List O-prefixed routes in the routing table | | `show ip ospf database` | Inspect LSDB; count Type-1, Type-2 LSAs | | `ping ` | End-to-end reachability test | | `traceroute ` | Confirm path selection matches SPF cost | > [!TIP] > **The wildcard Mask trick:** $0.0.0.63$ in `network 192.168.20.0 0.0.0.63 area 0` is the inverse of `/26` ($255.255.255.192$). This is how you tell OSPF *which exact subnets* to advertise without typing the mask. # 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper] **List any THREE differences between OSPF and RIP routing protocols.** **Model Answer:** | S.No. | OSPF | RIP | |:-----:|:-----|:----| | 1 | **Link-State** protocol; maintains a complete topology map (LSDB) | **Distance-Vector** protocol; knows only its directly connected neighbors | | 2 | Uses **Dijkstra's SPF algorithm** with **Cost** as metric | Uses **Bellman-Ford algorithm** with **Hop Count** (max 15) as metric | | 3 | **Classless**; supports **VLSM** and **CIDR** natively | **RIPv1** is classful; **RIPv2** supports VLSM | | 4 | **Administrative Distance = 110**; **Convergence: seconds** | **Administrative Distance = 120**; **Convergence: minutes** | | 5 | Supports **hierarchical Area design** (Area 0 backbone) | **Flat** network design; no area concept | | 6 | Sends triggered updates + **30-min LSA refresh** | Sends full routing table every **30 seconds** | *(3 marks for any 3 correct differences with proper technical terminology)* --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper] **With the help of a suitable example, explain VLSM subnetting.** **Model Answer:** **VLSM (Variable Length Subnet Masking)** is the process of dividing a network into subnets of *unequal* sizes based on host requirements. It eliminates the address wastage inherent in classical equal-sized subnetting (FLSM). **Example:** Consider the network **200.10.20.0/24**. Suppose we need three subnets: - Subnet-A: 100 hosts - Subnet-B: 50 hosts - Subnet-C: 25 hosts - WAN link: 2 hosts **VLSM Allocation (largest first):** | Subnet | Prefix | Mask | Block | Network Address | Usable Range | Broadcast | |:------:|:------:|:----:|:-----:|:----------------|:-------------|:---------:| | Sub-A | /25 | 255.255.255.128 | 128 | 200.10.20.0 | .1 – .126 | .127 | | Sub-B | /26 | 255.255.255.192 | 64 | 200.10.20.128 | .129 – .190 | .191 | | Sub-C | /27 | 255.255.255.224 | 32 | 200.10.20.192 | .193 – .222 | .223 | | WAN | /30 | 255.255.255.252 | 4 | 200.10.20.224 | .225 – .226 | .227 | VLSM requires a **classless routing protocol** like OSPF, RIPv2, EIGRP, or BGP to advertise the variable subnet masks. --- ## 📝 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice) ### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme] **A.** Consider the network topology shown below consisting of three routers **R1, R2, R3** forming a triangle, with R1 connected to LAN-A, R2 to LAN-B, and R3 to LAN-C. The available network is **172.16.0.0/16**. **Sub-questions:** **(a) [7 Marks]** Perform **VLSM subnetting** for the following requirements: - LAN-A on R1 — 100 hosts - LAN-B on R2 — 50 hosts - LAN-C on R3 — 20 hosts - All three WAN links (R1-R2, R2-R3, R3-R1) — 2 hosts each **(b) [7 Marks]** Write the **complete OSPF configuration** for R1 and R2 (assume R3 is already configured). Show the configuration for the LAN interface, all WAN interfaces, and the OSPF process. Also list any **TWO verification commands** to confirm OSPF neighbor adjacency. --- #### 🔑 MODEL SOLUTION ##### Part (a) — VLSM Subnetting [7 Marks] **Step 1: Sort requirements descending.** LAN-A (100) → LAN-B (50) → LAN-C (20) → WAN-AB (2) → WAN-BC (2) → WAN-CA (2) **Step 2: Calculate subnet masks and block sizes.** $$ \text{For LAN-A: } 2^h - 2 \ge 100 \Rightarrow h = 7 \Rightarrow \text{prefix } /17, \text{ mask } 255.255.128.0, \text{ block } = 32768 $$ $$ \text{For LAN-B: } 2^h - 2 \ge 50 \Rightarrow h = 6 \Rightarrow \text{prefix } /18, \text{ mask } 255.255.192.0, \text{ block } = 16384 $$ $$ \text{For LAN-C: } 2^h - 2 \ge 20 \Rightarrow h = 5 \Rightarrow \text{prefix } /19, \text{ mask } 255.255.224.0, \text{ block } = 8192 $$ $$ \text{For WAN (each): } 2^h - 2 \ge 2 \Rightarrow h = 2 \Rightarrow \text{prefix } /30, \text{ mask } 255.255.255.252, \text{ block } = 4 $$ **Step 3: Final VLSM Allocation Table.** | Subnet | Prefix | Network Address | Usable Range | Broadcast | |:------:|:------:|:----------------|:-------------|:---------:| | LAN-A | /17 | 172.16.0.0 | 172.16.0.1 – 172.16.127.254 | 172.16.127.255 | | LAN-B | /18 | 172.16.128.0 | 172.16.128.1 – 172.16.191.254 | 172.16.191.255 | | LAN-C | /19 | 172.16.192.0 | 172.16.192.1 – 172.16.223.254 | 172.16.223.255 | | WAN-AB | /30 | 172.16.224.0 | 172.16.224.1 – 172.16.224.2 | 172.16.224.3 | | WAN-BC | /30 | 172.16.224.4 | 172.16.224.5 – 172.16.224.6 | 172.16.224.7 | | WAN-CA | /30 | 172.16.224.8 | 172.16.224.9 – 172.16.224.10 | 172.16.224.11 | **Sample IP Assignments:** - R1 Fa0/0: 172.16.0.1/17 (LAN-A gateway) - Host A1: 172.16.0.2/17 - R2 Fa0/0: 172.16.128.1/18 - R3 Fa0/0: 172.16.192.1/19 - R1 S0/0/0: 172.16.224.1/30; R2 S0/0/0: 172.16.224.2/30 - R2 S0/0/1: 172.16.224.5/30; R3 S0/0/0: 172.16.224.6/30 - R3 S0/0/1: 172.16.224.9/30; R1 S0/0/1: 172.16.224.10/30 *(Full marks for correct calculation, ordering, and table format)* --- ##### Part (b) — OSPF Configuration [7 Marks] **R1 Configuration (Complete):** ``` enable configure terminal hostname R1 ! ! --- LAN Interface --- interface FastEthernet0/0 ip address 172.16.0.1 255.255.128.0 no shutdown ! ! --- WAN to R2 --- interface Serial0/0/0 ip address 172.16.224.1 255.255.255.252 clock rate 64000 no shutdown ! ! --- WAN to R3 --- interface Serial0/0/1 ip address 172.16.224.10 255.255.255.252 no shutdown ! ! --- OSPF Process 1 in Area 0 --- router ospf 1 router-id 1.1.1.1 network 172.16.0.0 0.0.127.255 area 0 network 172.16.224.0 0.0.0.3 area 0 network 172.16.224.8 0.0.0.3 area 0 passive-interface FastEthernet0/0 end write memory ``` **R2 Configuration (Complete):** ``` enable configure terminal hostname R2 ! ! --- LAN Interface --- interface FastEthernet0/0 ip address 172.16.128.1 255.255.192.0 no shutdown ! ! --- WAN to R1 --- interface Serial0/0/0 ip address 172.16.224.2 255.255.255.252 no shutdown ! ! --- WAN to R3 --- interface Serial0/0/1 ip address 172.16.224.5 255.255.255.252 clock rate 64000 no shutdown ! ! --- OSPF Process 1 in Area 0 --- router ospf 1 router-id 2.2.2.2 network 172.16.128.0 0.0.63.255 area 0 network 172.16.224.0 0.0.0.3 area 0 network 172.16.224.4 0.0.0.3 area 0 passive-interface FastEthernet0/0 end write memory ``` **TWO Verification Commands [1 mark each]:** 1. `show ip ospf neighbor` → Verifies neighbor state is **FULL** and shows neighbor's RID. 2. `show ip ospf interface brief` → Confirms correct **area**, **cost**, and **network type** per interface. *(5 marks for correct commands, syntax, and router-id; 2 marks for verification commands.)* --- ### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Alternative] **B.** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working of the **Dijkstra Shortest Path First (SPF) algorithm** as used by OSPF. Apply it to a network with 4 routers (R1, R2, R3, R4) where: - R1–R2: cost 5 - R1–R3: cost 2 - R2–R3: cost 3 - R2–R4: cost 4 - R3–R4: cost 7 **Compute the shortest path from R1 to R4 and its total cost.** **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **FIVE OSPF adjacency states** starting from `Down` and ending at `Full`. For each state, state ONE packet or event that triggers the transition. --- #### 🔑 MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a) [7 Marks] **Dijkstra's Algorithm Overview:** The algorithm builds a Shortest Path Tree (SPT) rooted at the source router. It maintains two sets: **Tentative** (unfinalized) and **Permanent** (finalized) nodes. **Step-by-Step Execution (R1 as root):** **Iteration 1:** R1 is permanent (cost = 0). Its neighbors R2 (cost=5) and R3 (cost=2) become tentative. | Node | Tentative Cost | Parent | |:----:|:--------------:|:------:| | R2 | 5 | R1 | | R3 | 2 | R1 | **Iteration 2:** Smallest tentative = R3 (cost=2). Make R3 **permanent**. Examine R3's neighbors: R2 and R4. - New path to R2: $2 + 3 = 5$ → **tie** with existing, keep R1 as parent. - New path to R4: $2 + 7 = 9$ → tentative R4 cost = 9, parent R3. | Node | Tentative Cost | Parent | |:----:|:--------------:|:------:| | R2 | 5 | R1 | | R4 | 9 | R3 | **Iteration 3:** Smallest tentative = R2 (cost=5). Make R2 **permanent**. Examine R2's neighbors: R3 (already permanent), R4. - New path to R4: $5 + 4 = 9$ → **tie** with existing, keep R3 as parent. | Node | Tentative Cost | Parent | |:----:|:--------------:|:------:| | R4 | 9 | R3 | **Iteration 4:** Only R4 remains. Make it permanent (cost=9). **Result — Shortest Path Tree from R1:** $$ \boxed{\text{R1} \xrightarrow{\text{cost } 2} \text{R3} \xrightarrow{\text{cost } 7} \text{R4}, \quad \text{Total Cost} = 9} $$ --- #### 🔑 MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b) [7 Marks] **The Five Key OSPF Adjacency States:** | # | State | Triggering Event / Packet | |:-:|:------|:--------------------------| | 1 | **Down** | Initial state. No Hello received from neighbor. Interface comes up. | | 2 | **Init** | Router receives a Hello from neighbor, but neighbor's list does NOT contain its own Router ID. | | 3 | **2-Way** | Router receives a Hello that *does* contain its own RID. Bi-directional communication established. **DR/BDR election** completes here. | | 4 | **Exstart** | Routers negotiate **Master/Slave** relationship and initial DBD sequence numbers using an empty DBD packet. | | 5 | **Exchange** | Routers exchange **DBD packets** describing their LSDBs. | | 6 | **Loading** | Routers send **LSR** (Link-State Request) packets for missing LSAs; receive **LSU** (Link-State Update) packets. | | 7 | **Full** | All LSAs exchanged and acknowledged (**LSAck**). **Adjacency complete.** | *(1 mark for naming each state correctly, 1 mark for the correct triggering event/packet.)* --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU students LOSE MARKS in OSPF + VLSM questions:** > > 1. **Wildcard Mask Mistakes** — The most common error is confusing the **subnet mask** with the **wildcard mask** in the `network` command. Example: For `/26`, mask is `255.255.255.192` but wildcard is `0.0.0.63` (inverse bitwise). Examiners **deduct 1–2 marks** for this. > > 2. **Forgetting `clock rate` on the DCE side** — Serial interfaces need `clock rate 64000` ONLY on the DCE end. If both sides or neither side is configured, the line protocol stays **down**. Costs **1 mark** typically. > > 3. **VLSM Allocation Order** — Always allocate **largest subnet first**. Allocating randomly leads to overlapping or out-of-range blocks. The Board explicitly tests this. > > 4. **Area 0 Omission** — Forgetting to place the OSPF process in **Area 0 (backbone)** when designing a single-area network. OSPF will not form adjacencies across mismatched areas. > > 5. **Cost Calculation Round-off** — OSPF cost is an **integer**. Always round UP for the final result. Writing `0.1` for Gigabit Ethernet (without the `auto-cost reference-bandwidth 1000` fix) loses marks. > > 6. **No `no shutdown`** — Forgetting to enable interfaces is the #1 reason routers show `administratively down` in `show ip interface brief`. The examiner expects this in your lab record. --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Board Prep):** - ✅ OSPF is a **Link-State IGP** using **Dijkstra SPF**; **AD = 110**, **Cost = RefBW/IntBW**. - ✅ Default **Reference Bandwidth = 100 Mbps**; cost range **1 to 65,535**. - ✅ **Hello Timer = 10s** (Broadcast), **Dead Timer = 40s** (Broadcast). - ✅ OSPF is **classless** → fully supports **VLSM** and **CIDR**. - ✅ **LSA Refresh = 30 min**, **MaxAge = 1 hour**. - ✅ Multicast addresses: **224.0.0.5** (AllSPFRouters), **224.0.0.6** (AllDRouters). - ✅ **5 LSA Types** to remember: Type-1 (Router), Type-2 (Network), Type-3 (Summary), Type-4 (ASBR Summary), Type-5 (External). - ✅ **7 Adjacency States**: Down → Init → 2-Way → Exstart → Exchange → Loading → Full. - ✅ VLSM formula: $\text{Usable Hosts} = 2^h - 2$; allocate **largest first**. - ✅ OSPF process ID is **locally significant** (e.g., `router ospf 1`). - ✅ Use **wildcard mask** (inverse of subnet mask) in `network` commands. - ✅ **Area 0** is the **mandatory backbone** for inter-area communication. - ✅ DR/BDR election occurs on **multi-access** (Broadcast) networks only — not on point-to-point serial links. - ✅ `show ip ospf neighbor` must show **FULL** state for the lab to be marked complete. - ✅ **Two essential lab verification commands**: `show ip ospf neighbor` and `show ip route ospf`.

topics.title: Implement Unicast IPv6 Addresses on routers and verify static routing, RIPNG and OSPFv3
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCITL407 - COMPUTER NETWORKS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Implement Unicast IPv6 Addresses on routers and verify static routing, RIPNG and OSPFv3 # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Unicast IPv6 Addressing — Formal Definition A **Unicast IPv6 Address** is a **128-bit logical identifier** assigned to a *single* network interface in an IPv6-enabled network. Unlike IPv4 (32-bit), IPv6 expands the address space from $2^{32}$ to $2^{128}$, providing approximately **340 undecillion** ($3.4 \times 10^{38}$) unique addresses. The address is written as **eight groups of four hexadecimal digits**, separated by colons: $$\text{IPv6 Address} = X:X:X:X:X:X:X:X$$ where each $X$ is a 16-bit hexadecimal value (0000 to FFFF). > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A Unicast packet is delivered to **exactly one** network interface. This is the most common communication type in IP networks (e.g., web browsing, SSH, email). ### Three Sub-Types of IPv6 Unicast (As per KTU & RFC 4291): | Sub-Type | Scope | KTU Relevance | |----------|-------|---------------| | **Global Unicast Address (GUA)** | Public Internet routable | High — required for inter-router links in lab | | **Link-Local Address (LLA)** | Single subnet only (FE80::/10) | High — auto-generated, used by routing protocols | | **Unique Local Address (ULA)** | Private (FC00::/7) | Medium — equivalent to RFC 1918 in IPv4 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Link-Local Addresses (FE80::/10)** are auto-assigned to *every* IPv6 interface using the **EUI-64** process or randomly generated. They are used by routing protocols (RIPng, OSPFv3) for neighbour discovery and adjacencies. --- ## 1.2 Static Routing for IPv6 **Static Routing** is a manually configured path in the routing table, where a network administrator explicitly defines the next-hop address or exit interface for a destination network. **Formal Definition:** A *static route* is a permanent entry in the IPv6 routing table that does not change dynamically and is not learned via any dynamic routing protocol. ### Intuitive Analogy — The "Road Sign" Metaphor 🛣️ Imagine you are driving in a city where **there are no GPS apps, no traffic signals, and no auto-updating navigation**. You can only reach your destination by reading **hand-written signboards** placed at every junction. The city administrator must *manually* place a sign at each junction saying: *"To go to City X, take Road Y."* - **Network 1 (City A)** = Your current network - **Static Route (Signboard)** = Manually configured entry - **Next-Hop (Arrow on sign)** = IP of the next router - **Destination Network (City X)** = Where the packet must reach > If a road is closed, the signboard is **useless** until a human updates it. Similarly, a static route does **not** automatically reroute traffic on failure — it must be reconfigured manually (or supplemented with a backup **floating static route**). --- ## 1.3 RIPng (RIP Next Generation) **RIPng** is the IPv6-compatible version of the **Routing Information Protocol**, defined in **RFC 2080**. It is a **Distance-Vector** interior gateway protocol (IGP) that uses **hop count** as its sole metric. ### Key Protocol Parameters (Mandatory for KTU Lab Viva) | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Administrative Distance | **120** | | Metric | Hop Count (**max 15**, infinite = 16) | | Update Mechanism | **UDP Port 521** (multicast to FF02::9) | | Algorithm | **Bellman-Ford** (Distance Vector) | | Update Timer | 30 seconds | | Hold-Down Timer | 180 seconds | > [!NOTE] > **RIPng Multicast Group:** `FF02::9` — all RIPng routers on the local link receive routing updates. Compare with IPv4 RIPv2 which used `224.0.0.9`. ### Intuitive Analogy — "The Rumour Network" 📢 Picture a village of **15 houses** connected by a single road. Every 30 seconds, each villager stands at the gate and shouts: *"I know the way to every other house! To reach House X, it takes Y steps."* If a path is broken, the rumour *"that house is unreachable (16 hops)"* spreads and traffic stops. RIPng is simple, but it suffers from **slow convergence** and the **15-hop limit** — fine for small lab topologies, **not** for real enterprise networks. --- ## 1.4 OSPFv3 (Open Shortest Path First for IPv6) **OSPFv3** is the IPv6 implementation of OSPF, defined in **RFC 5340**. It is a **Link-State** routing protocol that uses **Dijkstra's Shortest Path First (SPF) algorithm** to compute the optimal path. ### Key Protocol Parameters | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Administrative Distance | **110** | | Algorithm | **Dijkstra's SPF** (Link State) | | Protocol Number | **89** (IP Protocol ID) | | Multicast Addresses | `FF02::5` (AllSPFRouters), `FF02::6` (AllDRouters) | | Metric | **Cost** (Reference bandwidth / Interface bandwidth) | | Reference Bandwidth | 100 Mbps (default) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Major OSPFv3 Change from OSPFv2:** In OSPFv3, the **OSPF protocol itself does NOT carry IPv4 addresses** in its headers. IPv6 addresses are advertised as opaque LSA payloads. OSPFv3 uses a **per-link** (not per-subnet) model — one OSPF instance can run over multiple IPv6 networks on a single link. ### Intuitive Analogy — "The Map Makers" 🗺️ Instead of shouting rumours (RIP), every router is a **cartographer** that: 1. Draws its own local map (LSA — Link State Advertisement). 2. Shares the map with *every* neighbour. 3. Builds a **complete map of the entire network** in its database. 4. Runs **Dijkstra's algorithm** on this map to find the absolute shortest path to every destination. This is *much* faster, *much* smarter, but *much* more CPU-intensive than RIP. --- > [!TIP] > **KTU Viva Quick Recall Card:** > - **RIPng** = Simple, hop-count, max 15 hops, UDP 521, multicast FF02::9. > - **OSPFv3** = Complex, cost-based, Dijkstra, IP Proto 89, multicast FF02::5 & FF02::6. > - **Static** = No protocol, manually configured, AD = **1**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 IPv6 Address Structure — Deep Dive An IPv6 unicast address is divided into two major parts: $$\text{IPv6 Address (128 bits)} = \underbrace{\text{Network Prefix}}_{\text{n bits}} + \underbrace{\text{Interface ID}}_{\text{128 - n bits}}$$ ### Standard Subnet Prefix Lengths (KTU Lab Standard) | Network Type | Prefix Length | Interface ID Bits | Typical Use | |--------------|---------------|-------------------|-------------| | Loopback | /128 | 128 | `::1/128` | | Point-to-Point Link | /127 | 127 | Router-to-router | | **LAN Segment** | **/64** | **64** | **KTU Lab Standard** | | Default Route | /0 | 0 | `::/0` | ### EUI-64 Process — Building the Interface ID When a router has its interface ID auto-generated from a MAC address, the **EUI-64** process is used: 1. Take the **48-bit MAC address**: `XX:XX:XX:YY:YY:YY` 2. Insert **FF:FE** in the middle → `XX:XX:XX:FF:FE:YY:YY:YY` 3. **Flip the 7th bit (Universal/Local — U/L bit)** of the first octet. **Example Calculation:** $$\text{MAC: } 00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E$$ $$\text{Step 1: Split MAC at 24th bit } \rightarrow 00:1A:2B \mid 3C:4D:5E$$ $$\text{Step 2: Insert FFFE} \rightarrow 00:1A:2B:\text{FF:FE}:3C:4D:5E$$ $$\text{Step 3: Flip U/L bit (0→2) of 00} \rightarrow 02:1A:2B:FF:FE:3C:4D:5E$$ $$\text{Resulting IPv6 Interface ID: } 021A:2BFF:FE3C:4D5E$$ > [!NOTE] > Modern OSes (Windows, Linux, Cisco IOS 15+) use **random Interface IDs** (RFC 7217) for privacy, instead of EUI-64, but Cisco routers still show the EUI-64 format in lab. --- ## 2.2 IPv6 Header Structure (40 Bytes Fixed) Unlike IPv4 (20–60 bytes variable), IPv6 has a **fixed 40-byte header**: | Field | Size | Function | |-------|------|----------| | Version | 4 bits | Always 6 | | Traffic Class | 8 bits | QoS priority | | Flow Label | 20 bits | Identifies packet flow | | Payload Length | 16 bits | Length of data after header | | Next Header | 8 bits | Identifies next protocol (TCP=6, UDP=17, ICMPv6=58) | | Hop Limit | 8 bits | Decremented each hop (like TTL) | | Source Address | 128 bits | Sender | | Destination Address | 128 bits | Receiver | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Key Point:** IPv6 has **no Header Checksum**, **no Options field** (replaced by extension headers), and **no Fragment Offset in routers** (end-to-end fragmentation only). --- ## 2.3 Routing Logic — Operational Flow ### Static Route Decision Flow 1. Router receives IPv6 packet. 2. Longest prefix match lookup in IPv6 routing table. 3. If match found → forward to next-hop (or exit interface). 4. If no match → drop packet and send **ICMPv6 Destination Unreachable** to source. ### RIPng Operation Steps 1. Router boots → sends RIPng request on all interfaces to `FF02::9`. 2. Neighbours reply with full routing table. 3. Every **30 seconds**, send unsolicited updates with full table. 4. On topology change → **triggered update** (not waiting for timer). 5. Route marked invalid if no update received for **180 seconds**. ### OSPFv3 Operation Steps 1. **Hello packets** sent on each interface to `FF02::5`. 2. Routers form **adjacencies** (full neighbour relationships). 3. Each router generates **Router LSAs (Type 1)** describing its links. 4. LSAs flooded to all OSPF routers in the area. 5. Each router builds **Link-State Database (LSDB)**. 6. **Dijkstra SPF** algorithm runs → builds shortest-path tree. 7. Best paths installed in IPv6 routing table. --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Rule | KTU Viva Significance | |---------|----------------|------------------------| | IPv6 Total Address Space | $2^{128} = 3.4 \times 10^{38}$ | Confirm it is **not** $2^{128}$ — must convert | | Number of /64 subnets in a /48 | $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ | Standard site allocation | | Hop Count Limit (RIPng) | $\text{Max} = 15, \text{ Infinite} = 16$ | 16 = unreachable | | RIPng Administrative Distance | $\text{AD} = 120$ | Lower AD = more trusted | | OSPFv3 Administrative Distance | $\text{AD} = 110$ | OSPF preferred over RIP | | Static Route Administrative Distance | $\text{AD} = 1$ | Most trusted route | | OSPFv3 Metric (Cost) | $\text{Cost} = \frac{\text{Reference Bandwidth}}{\text{Interface Bandwidth}}$ | Default ref = 100 Mbps | | IPv6 Header Size | $\text{40 bytes fixed}$ | Smaller overhead than IPv4 | | MAC → EUI-64 | $\text{Insert FFFE at 24th bit, flip U/L bit}$ | Mandatory lab exercise | | Subnet Mask Conversion | $\text{/64} \rightarrow \text{4 hex groups for network}$ | Prefix length $\rightarrow$ subnet | ### Comparison Matrix: Static vs RIPng vs OSPFv3 | Feature | Static Route | RIPng | OSPFv3 | |---------|--------------|-------|--------| | Type | Manual | Distance-Vector | Link-State | | Algorithm | None | Bellman-Ford | Dijkstra SPF | | Administrative Distance | 1 | 120 | 110 | | Metric | None | Hop Count | Cost | | Convergence | None (manual) | Slow | Fast | | Scalability | Low | Low (15 hop) | High | | CPU Usage | None | Low | High | | Multicast Address | N/A | FF02::9 | FF02::5 / FF02::6 | | Transport | N/A | UDP 521 | IP Proto 89 | | Suitable For | Stub networks | Small labs | Enterprise / ISP | > [!TIP] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In ISP backbones, **OSPFv3** or **IS-IS (with IPv6 extensions)** is preferred for fast, hierarchical routing. **RIPng** is rarely used in production — it survives mainly in small office networks and academic labs (which is why KTU uses it!). **Static routes** are universally used for *default routes* and *backup floating routes*. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — Converting an IPv6 Prefix to Binary (for Exam) **Problem:** Show the bit breakdown of the IPv6 address `2001:0DB8:ACAD:0001:0000:0000:0000:0001/64`. **Step 1:** Identify the prefix length. The first **64 bits** (4 groups) are the network portion; the last **64 bits** (4 groups) are the Interface ID. $$\text{Prefix (64 bits)} = 2001:0DB8:ACAD:0001$$ $$\text{Interface ID (64 bits)} = 0000:0000:0000:0001$$ **Step 2:** Convert each hex digit to its 4-bit binary equivalent (exhaustive).
