The hex digit conversion table is: - `0` = `0000`, `1` = `0001`, `2` = `0010`, `3` = `0011` - `4` = `0100`, `5` = `0101`, `6` = `0110`, `7` = `0111` - `8` = `1000`, `9` = `1001`, `A` = `1010`, `B` = `1011` - `C` = `1100`, `D` = `1101`, `E` = `1110`, `F` = `1111` **Step 3:** Apply conversion to the network prefix. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{2001} &= 0010\,0000\,0000\,0001 \\ \text{0DB8} &= 0000\,1101\,1011\,1000 \\ \text{ACAD} &= 1010\,1100\,1010\,1101 \\ \text{0001} &= 0000\,0000\,0000\,0001 \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Concatenate the four 16-bit groups to form the full 64-bit prefix. $$ \text{Prefix (64 bits)} = 0010\,0000\,0000\,0001\,0000\,1101\,1011\,1000\,1010\,1100\,1010\,1101\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0001 $$ **Step 5:** Apply zero-compression rules for display. The address `2001:0DB8:ACAD:0001:0000:0000:0000:0001` contains a long run of zeros in the Interface ID. The leading zeros within each group can be removed, and the longest run of all-zero 16-bit groups can be replaced with `::`. $$ \text{Final Compressed Form} = 2001:\text{DB8}:\text{ACAD}:1::1 $$ > [!NOTE] > **Compression Rule:** The `::` can be used **only ONCE** in an address. It represents one or more consecutive 16-bit groups of all zeros. --- ## 3.2 Derivation — OSPFv3 Cost Calculation **Problem:** Calculate the OSPFv3 cost of a serial interface operating at **1544 kbps** using the default reference bandwidth of **100,000 kbps (100 Mbps)**. **Step 1:** Recall the cost formula. $$ \text{Cost} = \left\lfloor \frac{\text{Reference Bandwidth}}{\text{Interface Bandwidth}} \right\rfloor $$ **Step 2:** Substitute the values. $$ \text{Cost} = \frac{100{,}000 \text{ kbps}}{1544 \text{ kbps}} $$ **Step 3:** Perform the division. $$ \text{Cost} = 64.76 $$ **Step 4:** Apply the floor function (integer truncation, since OSPF cost must be an integer in 16 bits, range 1–65535). $$ \text{Final OSPFv3 Cost} = 64 $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** If the calculated cost is less than 1, OSPFv3 assigns a minimum cost of **1**. A **/32 loopback** interface in OSPFv3 is advertised with a cost of **1** regardless of the configured cost (unless using `ip ospf cost` override). --- ## 3.3 Derivation — RIPng Hop Count Path Selection **Topology Scenario:** - **Router R1** is connected to Network `2001:DB8:1::/64`. - **Router R2** is in the middle. - **Router R3** is connected to Network `2001:DB8:3::/64`. - **R1 → R2** = 1 hop, **R2 → R3** = 1 hop. **Path to `2001:DB8:3::/64` from R1's perspective:** $$ \text{Hop Count} = \text{Hops from R1 to R2} + \text{Hops from R2 to R3} = 1 + 1 = 2 \text{ hops} $$ **Maximum Path Length (Why 15 is the limit):** RIPng uses a 4-bit hop count field in its routing update, allowing values from 0 to 15 to be valid routes. A hop count of **16** is reserved to mean *unreachable* (infinity). This is the **count-to-infinity problem mitigation** in distance-vector protocols. $$ \text{Valid Hop Range} = [0, 15], \quad \text{Unreachable Marker} = 16 $$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — CLI Command Reference for KTU Lab > [!IMPORTANT] > The following matrix maps the conceptual operation to the **Cisco IOS CLI** command used to verify it during the KTU lab exam. | Configuration Step | Command (Cisco IOS) | Purpose | |---|---|---| | Enable IPv6 routing | `ipv6 unicast-routing` | Activates IPv6 packet forwarding on the router | | Assign IPv6 address to interface | `ipv6 address 2001:DB8:1::1/64` | Configures GUA on interface | | Enable OSPFv3 on interface | `ipv6 ospf 1 area 0` | Starts OSPFv3 process 1, area 0 on interface | | Enable RIPng on interface | `ipv6 rip NG enable` | Enables RIPng process named "NG" | | Add static route | `ipv6 route 2001:DB8:3::/64 2001:DB8:12::2` | Manual route via next-hop | | View routing table | `show ipv6 route` | Display all IPv6 routes | | View OSPFv3 neighbours | `show ipv6 ospf neighbor` | List adjacencies | | View RIPng database | `show ipv6 rip database` | RIPng route table | | Ping test | `ping 2001:DB8:3::1` | Verify end-to-end connectivity | | Traceroute | `traceroute 2001:DB8:3::1` | Verify hop-by-hop path | --- ## 3.5 Full Verification Walkthrough (Expected Lab Output) When you run `show ipv6 route` after configuring **static routing**, the output must show a code **S** entry: ``` S 2001:DB8:3::/64 [1/0] via 2001:DB8:12::2 ``` Where: - `S` = Static route - `[1/0]` = Administrative Distance **1**, Metric **0** - `via` = Next-hop IPv6 address When you run `show ipv6 route` after configuring **RIPng**, you will see: ``` R 2001:DB8:3::/64 [120/2] via FE80::2, Serial0/0/0 ``` Where: - `R` = RIPng route - `[120/2]` = AD **120**, Metric **2 hops** - Next-hop is a **Link-Local Address** (FE80::) When you run `show ipv6 route` after configuring **OSPFv3**, you will see: ``` O 2001:DB8:3::/64 [110/2] via FE80::2, Serial0/0/0 ``` Where: - `O` = OSPF route - `[110/2]` = AD **110**, Cost **2** (sum of interface costs) - Next-hop is a **Link-Local Address** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Valuation Trap:** A common mistake is to use the **Global Unicast Address** as next-hop in OSPFv3. While this works, the *RFC-recommended* and *lab-expected* practice is to use the **Link-Local Address** as the next-hop, because OSPFv3 uses per-link adjacencies. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Network Topology for KTU Lab Experiment 2 The standard 3-router topology used in the KTU PCITL407 lab for verifying IPv6 routing is shown below. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph LAN1["LAN 1: 2001:DB8:1::/64"] PC1["PC1
2001:DB8:1::2/64
Default GW: R1"] end subgraph WAN12["WAN Link R1-R2: 2001:DB8:12::/64"] R1["R1
G0/0: 2001:DB8:1::1/64
S0/0/0: 2001:DB8:12::1/64"] R2["R2
S0/0/0: 2001:DB8:12::2/64
S0/0/1: 2001:DB8:23::2/64"] end subgraph WAN23["WAN Link R2-R3: 2001:DB8:23::/64"] R3["R3
S0/0/0: 2001:DB8:23::3/64
G0/0: 2001:DB8:3::1/64"] end subgraph LAN3["LAN 3: 2001:DB8:3::/64"] PC3["PC3
2001:DB8:3::2/64
Default GW: R3"] end PC1 --- R1 R1 --- R2 R2 --- R3 R3 --- PC3 style R1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style R2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style R3 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style PC1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style PC3 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style LAN1 fill:#F0FFF0,stroke:#333,stroke-dasharray: 5 5 style LAN3 fill:#F0FFF0,stroke:#333,stroke-dasharray: 5 5 style WAN12 fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#333,stroke-dasharray: 5 5 style WAN23 fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#333,stroke-dasharray: 5 5 ``` > [!NOTE] > The diagram shows the **three-router linear topology** (R1–R2–R3) with two LANs at the ends and two serial WAN links in the middle. This is the canonical KTU lab topology. --- ## 4.2 Packet Flow Diagram — PC1 to PC3 (RIPng Path Discovery) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram autonumber participant PC1 as PC1
2001:DB8:1::2 participant R1 as R1
2001:DB8:1::1 participant R2 as R2
2001:DB8:12::2 participant R3 as R3
2001:DB8:3::1 participant PC3 as PC3
2001:DB8:3::2 Note over R1,R3: Every 30 seconds — RIPng Update to FF02::9 R2->>R1: RIPng Update via UDP 521
2001:DB8:3::/64 via R3 R2->>R3: RIPng Update via UDP 521
2001:DB8:1::/64 via R1 Note over PC1,PC3: User initiates ping PC1->>R1: ICMPv6 Echo Request
Dst: 2001:DB8:3::2 R1->>R2: Forward via next-hop 2001:DB8:12::2 R2->>R3: Forward via next-hop 2001:DB8:23::3 R3->>PC3: Deliver to final destination PC3-->>PC1: ICMPv6 Echo Reply (reverse path) Note over PC1,PC3: ping 2001:DB8:3::2 should succeed ``` --- ## 4.3 Protocol Operational Comparison — Functional Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph StaticBlock["Static Routing Block"] S1[Administrator] --> S2[Manual CLI Configuration] S2 --> S3[Routing Table Entry] S3 --> S4{Route Exists?} S4 -->|Yes| S5[Forward Packet] S4 -->|No| S6[Drop + ICMPv6 Unreachable] end subgraph RIPngBlock["RIPng Block"] R1[UDP Socket 521] --> R2[Receive Neighbour Update] R2 --> R3[Validate Hop Count] R3 --> R4{Hop <= 15?} R4 -->|Yes| R5[Add to RIPng Database] R4 -->|No| R6[Mark Unreachable = 16] R5 --> R7[Install in Routing Table] R6 --> R7 end subgraph OSPFv3Block["OSPFv3 Block"] O1[IP Protocol 89] --> O2[Hello: FF02::5] O2 --> O3[Form Adjacency] O3 --> O4[Exchange LSAs] O4 --> O5[Build LSDB] O5 --> O6[Run Dijkstra SPF] O6 --> O7[Install in Routing Table] end style StaticBlock fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#8B0000 style RIPngBlock fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B style OSPFv3Block fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082 ``` --- ## 4.4 IPv6 Address Format Diagram (Block-Level) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["2001:0DB8:ACAD:0001"]:::net --> B["0000:0000:0000:0001"]:::host A --> C["Global Routing Prefix
45 bits"]:::sub A --> D["Subnet ID
16 bits"]:::sub B --> E["Interface ID
64 bits
(EUI-64 or Random)"]:::sub style A fill:#FFB6C1 style B fill:#98FB98 style C fill:#FFA07A style D fill:#FFA07A style E fill:#87CEFA classDef net fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef host fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef sub fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,stroke-dasharray: 3 3 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1: Define Unicast IPv6 Address. List any three sub-types of IPv6 unicast addresses.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** A **Unicast IPv6 Address** is a **128-bit identifier** assigned to a single network interface. A packet sent to a unicast address is delivered to exactly one host interface. The three sub-types are: 1. **Global Unicast Address (GUA)** — Publicly routable on the Internet (e.g., `2001:DB8::/32`). 2. **Link-Local Address (LLA)** — Auto-configured on every IPv6 interface; valid only on the local link (prefix `FE80::/10`). 3. **Unique Local Address (ULA)** — Private addresses for internal networks, not routable on the public Internet (prefix `FC00::/7`). --- ### **Question 2: Differentiate between RIPng and OSPFv3 in terms of algorithm, metric, and administrative distance.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** | Parameter | RIPng | OSPFv3 | |-----------|-------|--------| | **Algorithm** | Distance-Vector (Bellman-Ford) | Link-State (Dijkstra SPF) | | **Metric** | Hop Count (max 15) | Cost (Reference BW / Interface BW) | | **Administrative Distance** | 120 | 110 | | **Transport** | UDP Port 521 | IP Protocol 89 | | **Multicast** | FF02::9 | FF02::5 / FF02::6 | | **Convergence** | Slow | Fast | | **Scalability** | Low (15 hop limit) | High (hierarchical areas) | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > KTU Module Internal Choice rule: A 14-mark question typically has sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. Answer both sub-parts for full marks. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **With a suitable network diagram, explain the steps to configure and verify static IPv6 routing between two routers. Write the relevant Cisco IOS commands.** **Model Answer:** **Step 1: Topology** — Two routers R1 and R2 connected via a serial link, each with a LAN. (Use the topology from Section 4.1 as reference, with only R1 and R2 active.) **Step 2: IP Addressing Table** | Device | Interface | IPv6 Address | |--------|-----------|--------------| | R1 | G0/0 | 2001:DB8:1::1/64 | | R1 | S0/0/0 | 2001:DB8:12::1/64 | | R2 | S0/0/0 | 2001:DB8:12::2/64 | | R2 | G0/0 | 2001:DB8:2::1/64 | **Step 3: Configuration on R1** ``` enable configure terminal hostname R1 ipv6 unicast-routing interface GigabitEthernet0/0 ipv6 address 2001:DB8:1::1/64 no shutdown interface Serial0/0/0 ipv6 address 2001:DB8:12::1/64 clock rate 64000 no shutdown ipv6 route 2001:DB8:2::/64 2001:DB8:12::2 end write memory ``` **Step 4: Configuration on R2** (mirror of R1, with static route pointing to R1) ``` ipv6 route 2001:DB8:1::/64 2001:DB8:12::1 ``` **Step 5: Verification Commands** ``` show ipv6 interface brief show ipv6 route show ipv6 route static ping 2001:DB8:2::1 traceroute 2001:DB8:2::1 ``` **Expected Output in `show ipv6 route`:** ``` S 2001:DB8:2::/64 [1/0] via 2001:DB8:12::2 ``` **Conclusion:** Successful end-to-end ping confirms the static route is functioning. #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Explain the RIPng protocol in detail. List the Cisco IOS commands to enable RIPng on a router interface and verify the RIPng routing table.** **Model Answer:** **RIPng (RFC 2080)** is the IPv6-native version of RIP. It is a **distance-vector** interior gateway protocol that uses **hop count** (max 15) as its metric and **UDP port 521** for transport. Updates are sent every 30 seconds to the multicast group **FF02::9**. **Operational Steps:** 1. On boot, sends a RIPng request on all enabled interfaces. 2. Neighbours reply with their full routing table. 3. Every 30 seconds, sends unsolicited periodic updates. 4. Routes are marked invalid if no update is received in **180 seconds**. 5. Uses **split-horizon** and **poison-reverse** to prevent routing loops. **Cisco IOS Configuration Commands:** ``` enable configure terminal ipv6 unicast-routing interface Serial0/0/0 ipv6 address 2001:DB8:12::1/64 ipv6 rip NG enable exit interface GigabitEthernet0/0 ipv6 address 2001:DB8:1::1/64 ipv6 rip NG enable end write memory ``` **Verification Commands:** ``` show ipv6 rip database show ipv6 route rip show ipv6 protocols ``` **Expected Output:** ``` R 2001:DB8:2::/64 [120/2] via FE80::2, Serial0/0/0 ``` This confirms a RIPng-learned route to the remote LAN, with hop count 2 (R1 → R2 → R2's LAN). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Describe the IPv6 address format. With an example, explain the EUI-64 process of generating an Interface ID from a MAC address.** **Model Answer:** **IPv6 Address Format (128 bits):** An IPv6 unicast address is written as eight groups of 16-bit hexadecimal values: $$X:X:X:X:X:X:X:X$$ It is divided into two parts: the **Network Prefix** (typically /64) and the **Interface ID** (64 bits). **Example:** `2001:DB8:ACAD:0001:0000:0000:0000:0001/64` - **Network Prefix:** `2001:DB8:ACAD:0001` (first 64 bits) - **Interface ID:** `0000:0000:0000:0001` (last 64 bits) **EUI-64 Process — Derivation:** Given MAC address: `00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E` **Step 1:** Split the 48-bit MAC into two 24-bit halves. $$\text{OUI (24 bits)} = 00:1A:2B \quad \mid \quad \text{NIC (24 bits)} = 3C:4D:5E$$ **Step 2:** Insert `FF:FE` in the middle. $$00:1A:2B:\text{FF:FE}:3C:4D:5E$$ **Step 3:** Flip the **7th bit (U/L bit)** of the first octet. The first octet is `00` (binary `00000000`). The 7th bit (counting from the right) is the second-most-significant bit. Flipping this bit: `00000000` → `00000010` = `02` in hex. $$02:1A:2B:\text{FF:FE}:3C:4D:5E$$ **Resulting Interface ID (EUI-64):** `021A:2BFF:FE3C:4D5E` **Final Link-Local Address:** `FE80::021A:2BFF:FE3C:4D5E` #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Explain OSPFv3 and list the commands to configure and verify OSPFv3 on a router. State any three differences between OSPFv2 and OSPFv3.** **Model Answer:** **OSPFv3 (RFC 5340)** is the IPv6 implementation of OSPF, a **link-state** protocol using **Dijkstra's Shortest Path First (SPF) algorithm**. It uses **IP Protocol 89** directly (not TCP/UDP) and multicasts on `FF02::5` (AllSPFRouters) and `FF02::6` (AllDRouters). **Cisco IOS Configuration:** ``` enable configure terminal ipv6 unicast-routing interface Serial0/0/0 ipv6 address 2001:DB8:12::1/64 ipv6 ospf 1 area 0 exit interface GigabitEthernet0/0 ipv6 address 2001:DB8:1::1/64 ipv6 ospf 1 area 0 end write memory ``` **Verification Commands:** ``` show ipv6 ospf neighbor show ipv6 ospf interface show ipv6 route ospf show ipv6 protocols ``` **Expected Output in `show ipv6 ospf neighbor`:** ``` Neighbor ID Pri State Dead Time Interface 2.2.2.2 1 FULL/ - 00:00:38 Serial0/0/0 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Note for KTU Viva:** OSPFv3 still uses a **32-bit Router ID** (typically derived from an IPv4 address). If no IPv4 address is configured, the router ID is manually set using `ipv6 router-id 1.1.1.1`. **Three Key Differences: OSPFv2 vs OSPFv3:** | Aspect | OSPFv2 (IPv4) | OSPFv3 (IPv6) | |--------|---------------|---------------| | **IP Addressing** | Carries IPv4 addresses in LSU | Does **not** carry IPv6 in headers; uses LSAs as opaque payloads | | **Multicast Addresses** | 224.0.0.5 / 224.0.0.6 | FF02::5 / FF02::6 | | **Authentication** | Built-in (MD5 / SHA) | Relies on **IPsec / AH / ESP** (OSPFv3 removed built-in auth) | | **Link Model** | Per-IP-subnet | Per-link (one OSPF instance can support multiple IPv6 subnets) | | **Router ID Source** | IPv4 address | Manually set or auto from IPv4 loopback | --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > > 1. **Missing `ipv6 unicast-routing` command** — Without this, the router will NOT forward IPv6 packets even if addresses are configured. This is the **#1 reason students fail** to get ping results in the lab. **−2 marks** if missed. > 2. **Confusing OSPFv3 with OSPFv2 syntax** — OSPFv3 does **not** use the `network` command in router configuration mode. You enable OSPFv3 *directly on the interface* using `ipv6 ospf 1 area 0`. **−3 marks** if wrong syntax is used. > 3. **Forgetting `no shutdown`** on serial interfaces — Serial links stay administratively down by default. **−2 marks** if missed. > 4. **Writing IPv4 next-hop in static IPv6 routes** — The next-hop must be a valid IPv6 address, not IPv4. **−2 marks** if mixed. > 5. **Using AD-1 in floating static route incorrectly** — Floating static route is configured with a *higher* AD (e.g., `ipv6 route 2001:DB8:3::/64 2001:DB8:12::2 5`) to act as a backup. A regular static route has AD=1. **−2 marks** if concept is wrong. > 6. **Forgetting to assign Link-Local on interfaces** — OSPFv3 and RIPng adjacencies form over the **link-local address**, not the GUA. Without LLA, the adjacency will not come up. **−3 marks** if link-local is explicitly disabled. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute KTU Exam Revision — 5-Minute Recall Sheet** ### ✅ IPv6 Addressing - [ ] IPv6 address is **128 bits**, written as `XXXX:XXXX:XXXX:XXXX:XXXX:XXXX:XXXX:XXXX`. - [ ] **Link-Local** addresses use prefix `FE80::/10` and are **auto-generated** on every IPv6 interface. - [ ] **Global Unicast** addresses are publicly routable (current block: `2000::/3`). - [ ] **::1/128** = Loopback address. - [ ] **::/0** = Default route (equivalent to `0.0.0.0/0` in IPv4). - [ ] EUI-64 = Insert **FF:FE** in the middle of MAC + flip the **7th bit** of the first octet. - [ ] Zero compression (`::`) can be used **only once** per address. - [ ] Leading zeros within a 16-bit group **can be removed**, but trailing zeros **cannot**. ### ✅ Static Routing - [ ] Administrative Distance = **1** (most trusted). - [ ] Syntax: `ipv6 route `. - [ ] Floating static route uses a **higher AD** (e.g., 5 or 10) as a backup. - [ ] `show ipv6 route static` displays only static entries. ### ✅ RIPng - [ ] **Distance-Vector** protocol, **hop count** metric, **max 15 hops**, **16 = unreachable**. - [ ] Uses **UDP port 521**, multicasts to **FF02::9**. - [ ] AD = **120**, Update timer = **30 sec**, Hold-down = **180 sec**. - [ ] Enabled on interface: `ipv6 rip enable`. - [ ] Process name is local-significant (commonly "NG" or "RIPNG"). ### ✅ OSPFv3 - [ ] **Link-State** protocol using **Dijkstra SPF** algorithm. - [ ] Metric = **Cost** = (Reference BW / Interface BW); default reference = **100 Mbps**. - [ ] Uses **IP Protocol 89** directly, multicasts to **FF02::5** and **FF02::6**. - [ ] AD = **110**, Preferred over RIPng. - [ ] Enabled on interface: `ipv6 ospf area `. - [ ] OSPFv3 does **not** carry IPv6 addresses in its headers; addresses are opaque LSA payloads. - [ ] Authentication in OSPFv3 is via **IPsec**, not built-in. - [ ] Router ID is still **32-bit** (use IPv4 loopback or manual `router-id`). ### ✅ Universal Verification Commands (Always Use These!) - [ ] `show ipv6 interface brief` — Check interface status and assigned addresses. - [ ] `show ipv6 route` — Full IPv6 routing table. - [ ] `show ipv6 protocols` — Active routing protocols and their interfaces. - [ ] `show ipv6 ospf neighbor` — OSPFv3 adjacencies. - [ ] `show ipv6 rip database` — RIPng database. - [ ] `ping ` — End-to-end connectivity test. - [ ] `traceroute ` — Path verification hop-by-hop. ### ✅ Critical Lab Prerequisites (Don't Forget!) - [ ] `ipv6 unicast-routing` — **MUST** be enabled globally (most commonly missed step). - [ ] `no shutdown` on all interfaces. - [ ] Default gateway configured on PCs (IPv6: use `ip default-gateway` is for IPv4; use `ipv6 default-gateway` on router). - [ ] Clock rate set on DCE side of serial link (`clock rate 64000`).

topics.title: Create and apply Standard and Extended ACLs on routers to filter traffic based on IP addresses and protocols
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCITL407 - COMPUTER NETWORKS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Create and apply Standard and Extended ACLs on routers to filter traffic based on IP addresses and protocols # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) An **Access Control List (ACL)** is a sequential, ordered set of permit or deny rules applied to inbound or outbound traffic on a router interface. Each rule (called an **Access Control Entry – ACE**) is evaluated top-down by the router, and the **first match** determines the action. If no rule matches, an implicit **"deny all"** statement at the end blocks the packet. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition (verbatim from lab manual):** ACLs are used to provide security filtering, restrict traffic flow, and control which packets are forwarded or blocked at router interfaces. They operate at the **Network Layer (Layer 3)** and **Transport Layer (Layer 4)** of the OSI model. ## 1.2 Two Main Types of ACLs | ACL Type | ACL Number Range | Can Filter On | Cannot Filter On | |----------|------------------|---------------|------------------| | **Standard ACL** | 1–99, 1300–1999 | Source IP only | Destination IP, Protocol, Port | | **Extended ACL** | 100–199, 2000–2699 | Source IP, Destination IP, Protocol (IP/TCP/UDP/ICMP), Port numbers | — | > [!NOTE] > **Named ACLs** (a modern alternative) use descriptive alphanumeric names instead of numbers, and can be either standard or extended. The configuration syntax is `ip access-list standard/extended `. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – The Bouncer at a Club Door Imagine a **nightclub with one entrance** and a **strict bouncer (the ACL)** standing at the door. The bouncer holds a **clipboard with a list of rules** (the ACL entries) and checks every person (packet) wanting to enter: 1. The bouncer reads the rules **from top to bottom** (sequential processing). 2. The **first rule that matches** the person decides whether they enter or are turned away. 3. The bouncer **does not keep reading** after a match is found. 4. If no rule matches, the bouncer's **default instruction** is "nobody gets in" (implicit deny). 5. Some bouncers check only the **face/ID photo** (Standard ACL – source IP), while others check the **face, dress code, and invitation** (Extended ACL – source, destination, protocol, port). > [!TIP] > This analogy directly maps to the **"First Match Wins"** logic of Cisco ACLs — a critical exam point. ## 1.4 Why ACLs Matter in Real Engineering - **Enterprise Security**: Block Telnet (port 23) access to the HR server from the guest Wi-Fi VLAN. - **Bandwidth Conservation**: Prevent ICMP broadcast storms (Smurf attacks) from saturating WAN links. - **Policy Enforcement**: Allow only the Finance subnet to reach the SQL database server. - **Traffic Engineering**: In KTU labs, you will simulate these using **Cisco Packet Tracer**.
## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - The implicit deny rule at the end of every ACL is **always present**, even if you do not type it. - Maximum of **one ACL per interface per direction** (one inbound, one outbound) — total **2 ACLs per interface**. - Wildcard mask **255.255.255.255** means **"match any"** and is equivalent to the keyword `any`. - Wildcard mask **0.0.0.0** means **"match exact host"** and is equivalent to the keyword `host`. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic of an ACL (Step-by-Step Reasoning) When a packet arrives at a router interface where an ACL is applied, the following **deterministic sequence** is executed by the Cisco IOS: 1. **Packet Arrival**: The router receives a packet on an interface that has an ACL applied in the `in` or `out` direction. 2. **Header Extraction**: IOS extracts the relevant Layer 3 and Layer 4 header fields (source IP, destination IP, protocol, port numbers). 3. **Sequential Matching**: IOS compares these fields against ACE #1, then ACE #2, then ACE #3, and so on, in the exact order they were entered. 4. **First Match Action**: The very first ACE whose conditions are satisfied by the packet triggers either `permit` (forward) or `deny` (drop silently, with optional ICMP unreachable). 5. **Termination**: Processing stops immediately. No further ACEs are checked. 6. **Implicit Deny Fallback**: If the packet reaches the end of the list without a match, it is **denied by default**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Order is everything!** A `deny` rule placed above a `permit` rule can block traffic you intended to allow. Always use `show access-lists` to verify sequence numbers. ## 2.2 Wildcard Masks — The Heart of ACLs A **wildcard mask** is a 32-bit number used in ACLs to identify which bits of an IP address should be **ignored** (wildcard bit `0` = must match) and which should be **checked** (wildcard bit `1` = ignore). ### 2.2.1 The Wildcard Mask Rule $$\text{Wildcard Mask} = 255.255.255.255 - \text{Subnet Mask}$$ Where $255.255.255.255$ is the maximum 32-bit value and the subnet mask is the standard CIDR mask. ### 2.2.2 Worked Examples of Wildcard Mask Calculation | Subnet/CIDR | Subnet Mask | Wildcard Mask Calculation | Final Wildcard | Meaning | |-------------|-------------|---------------------------|----------------|---------| | 10.0.0.0/8 | 255.0.0.0 | 255 - 255 = 0, 255-0=255, ... | **0.255.255.255** | Match any 10.x.x.x | | 172.16.0.0/16 | 255.255.0.0 | 255-255=0, 255-0=255, ... | **0.0.255.255** | Match any 172.16.x.x | | 192.168.1.0/24 | 255.255.255.0 | 255-255=0, 0-0=0, 0-0=0, 255-0=255 | **0.0.0.255** | Match any 192.168.1.x | | Single host | 255.255.255.255 | 255-255=0, all zeros | **0.0.0.0** | Match exact host only | | Everything | N/A | N/A | **255.255.255.255** | Match any IP | ## 2.3 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet Table | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit | Notes | |---------|----------------|------|-------| | Wildcard from Subnet Mask | $WC = 255.255.255.255 - SM$ | 32-bit dotted | Always subtract byte-wise | | Wildcard for /n prefix | $WC = 2^{32-n} - 1$ (in last octets) | Decimal | For /24, last octet = 255 | | Subnet to Host Bits | $H = 2^{(32-n)}$ | Hosts | For /24 → 256 hosts | | Network Bits | $n$ | Bits | For /24 → 24 bits fixed | | First Match Rule | $\text{First ACE that matches} \rightarrow \text{Action}$ | — | No further checks | | Implicit Deny | $\text{End of list} \rightarrow \text{Deny}$ | — | Always present | | ACLs per Interface | $\text{Max} = 2$ (1 in + 1 out) | Count | Direction matters | ## 2.4 Standard vs Extended ACL — Detailed Comparison | Feature | Standard ACL | Extended ACL | |---------|--------------|--------------| | **ACL Number** | 1–99, 1300–1999 | 100–199, 2000–2699 | | **Filterable Criteria** | Source IP only | Source + Destination IP, Protocol, Port | | **Granularity** | Coarse (entire host match) | Fine-grained (per-service control) | | **Best Placement** | Close to **destination** (since it only knows source) | Close to **source** (filters early, saves bandwidth) | | **Cisco Exam Rule** | "Standard near destination" | "Extended near source" | | **Example Use** | Block a specific LAN from reaching internet | Block LAN from accessing server on port 80 | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Lab Exam Tip:** You will typically be asked to configure a **named extended ACL** because it allows descriptive names like `BLOCK_TELNET_HR` and supports both standard and extended syntax in one place. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Cisco IOS** routers use ACLs for: NAT (Network Address Translation) exemptions, VPN encryption policies, OSPF route filtering (`distribute-list`), and DHCP relay security. - **Modern Firewalls** (ASA, Palo Alto) use the same ACE concept but add stateful inspection. - **Linux `iptables`/`nftables`** directly map to ACL logic: chains, rules, first-match, default policy.
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Wildcard Mask for the Subnet 192.168.10.0/27 We are given a /27 prefix. We need to derive the wildcard mask step-by-step. ### Step 1: Convert /27 to dotted-decimal subnet mask The /27 means 27 network bits and $32 - 27 = 5$ host bits. The first three octets are fully network bits (24 bits), and the fourth octet has $27 - 24 = 3$ network bits. $$\text{Subnet Mask} = 11111111.11111111.11111111.11100000_2 = 255.255.255.224$$ ### Step 2: Apply the wildcard formula $WC = 255.255.255.255 - SM$ $$\begin{aligned} WC &= 255.255.255.255 - 255.255.255.224 \\ &= (255-255).(255-255).(255-255).(255-224) \\ &= 0.0.0.31 \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 3: Verify the host count The last octet wildcard 31 means the last 5 host bits are wildcards. $$H = 2^5 = 32 \text{ addresses}$$ These 32 addresses range from 192.168.10.0 to 192.168.10.31, with 192.168.10.0 as network and 192.168.10.31 as broadcast. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: ACL Configuration for a Three-Router Scenario ### Network Topology Scenario - **PC-A (192.168.1.10/24)** on LAN-A connected to **R1 (Gi0/0)** - **R1 (Se0/0/0) ↔ R2 (Se0/0/0)** via WAN link 10.0.0.0/30 - **R2 (Gi0/0)** connects to **PC-B (192.168.2.10/24)** and **Server (192.168.2.100/24)** ### Objective Block **PC-A from accessing the Server via HTTP (port 80)** but allow **all other traffic** between the LANs. ### Solution Derivation: Extended ACL on R1 (placed close to source) **Step 1: Identify what to filter** - Source: 192.168.1.10 (PC-A) - Destination: 192.168.2.100 (Server) - Protocol: TCP (HTTP uses TCP) - Destination Port: 80 **Step 2: Determine wildcard masks** - Source wildcard for 192.168.1.10/32 = 0.0.0.0 (single host) - Destination wildcard for 192.168.2.100/32 = 0.0.0.0 (single host) **Step 3: Build the Extended ACL rules** | Line | Action | Protocol | Source | Destination | Port | |------|--------|----------|--------|-------------|------| | 10 | deny | tcp | host 192.168.1.10 | host 192.168.2.100 | eq 80 | | 20 | permit | ip | any | any | — | **Step 4: Apply the ACL to the correct interface (close to source)** The ACL should be applied **inbound on R1's Gi0/0** (LAN-A facing interface) so that the unwanted traffic is dropped immediately, saving WAN bandwidth. ### 3.2.1 Complete IOS Command Sequence ```cisco enable configure terminal ! Step A: Create the named extended ACL ip access-list extended BLOCK_HTTP_PCA deny tcp host 192.168.1.10 host 192.168.2.100 eq 80 permit ip any any exit ! Step B: Apply to the inbound interface interface GigabitEthernet0/0 ip access-group BLOCK_HTTP_PCA in exit ! Step C: Verify show access-lists show ip interface GigabitEthernet0/0 ``` ### 3.2.2 Expected Verification Output ``` Router#show access-lists Extended IP access list BLOCK_HTTP_PCA 10 deny tcp host 192.168.1.10 host 192.168.2.100 eq www 20 permit ip any any ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > The `eq www` keyword is the same as `eq 80` — Cisco IOS automatically translates well-known port numbers to their service names. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Common Lab Exam Scenarios | Scenario | ACL Type | Place Near | Key Rule | |----------|----------|------------|----------| | Block one LAN from internet | Standard | Destination | `deny 192.168.1.0 0.0.0.255` | | Block Telnet to a server | Extended | Source | `deny tcp any host X eq 23` | | Block ping (ICMP) between subnets | Extended | Source | `deny icmp any any` | | Allow only FTP to one host | Extended | Source | `permit tcp any host X eq 21` then `deny ip any any` | | Block all but one IP from accessing a subnet | Standard | Destination | `permit host 1.1.1.1` then `deny any` | | Permit OSPF routing updates | Extended | Source | `permit ospf any any` | ## 3.4 Derivation 3: Numbered Standard ACL Example **Objective:** Allow only host 192.168.1.5 to reach 10.0.0.0/8 and deny everyone else. ```cisco access-list 10 permit host 192.168.1.5 access-list 10 deny any ! interface GigabitEthernet0/0 ip access-group 10 out ``` Note that we did **NOT** type the implicit deny — Cisco adds it automatically. The `deny any` is explicit so that `show access-lists` shows the hit count on that final line. ## 3.5 Derivation 4: Wildcard Mask for a Discontiguous Subnet For matching **only even-numbered hosts** in 192.168.1.0/24 (i.e., 192.168.1.0, .2, .4, ... .254): $$\text{Wildcard} = 0.0.0.254 \text{ (last bit ignored)}$$ The bit pattern of 254 in binary is `11111110` — the LSB is a wildcard bit, so it matches both 0 and 1 in the last position. > [!NOTE] > Discontiguous wildcard masks (like 0.0.0.254) are **rarely used in KTU labs** but are excellent for understanding the bitwise matching concept.
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Network Topology for Lab Experiment 2 ```mermaid graph TD subgraph "LAN-A (192.168.1.0/24)" PCA["PC-A
192.168.1.10
Web Browser"] R1G0["R1 Gi0/0
192.168.1.1"] end subgraph "WAN Link (10.0.0.0/30)" R1S0["R1 Se0/0/0
10.0.0.1"] R2S0["R2 Se0/0/0
10.0.0.2"] end subgraph "LAN-B (192.168.2.0/24)" R2G0["R2 Gi0/0
192.168.2.1"] PCB["PC-B
192.168.2.10"] SRV["Server-HTTP
192.168.2.100"] end PCA --- R1G0 R1G0 --- R1S0 R1S0 -. "Serial WAN" .- R2S0 R2S0 --- R2G0 R2G0 --- PCB R2G0 --- SRV style PCA fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc style PCB fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc style SRV fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000 style R1G0 fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#999900 style R2G0 fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#999900 style R1S0 fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#999900 style R2S0 fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#999900 ``` ## 4.2 ACL Processing Flow — First Match Logic ```mermaid graph TD A["Packet arrives at router interface"] --> B{"Is ACL applied
on this interface?"} B -- "No" --> C["Packet processed normally
(routed)"] B -- "Yes" --> D["Extract L3/L4 headers
(Source IP, Dest IP, Protocol, Port)"] D --> E["Compare with ACE #1"] E --> F{"Match with
ACE #1?"} F -- "Yes - Permit" --> G["Forward packet"] F -- "Yes - Deny" --> H["Drop packet silently"] F -- "No" --> I["Move to next ACE"] I --> J{"More ACEs
in list?"} J -- "Yes" --> E J -- "No" --> K["Implicit Deny
(deny all)"] K --> H style A fill:#e1f5ff style G fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724 style H fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24 style K fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404 ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Extended ACL Filtering Chain ```mermaid graph LR subgraph "Layer 3 Network Boundary" EXT["Extended ACL
on Router Interface"] end subgraph "Filtering Modules" M1["Module 1
Source IP Filter"] M2["Module 2
Destination IP Filter"] M3["Module 3
Protocol Filter"] M4["Module 4
Port Number Filter"] end subgraph "Action Outputs" OUT1["Permit Path
forward packet"] OUT2["Deny Path
drop packet"] end EXT --> M1 M1 --> M2 M2 --> M3 M3 --> M4 M4 --> OUT1 M4 --> OUT2 style EXT fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460 style OUT1 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724 style OUT2 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24 ``` ## 4.4 Standard ACL Placement — Architectural Rule ```mermaid graph TD SRC["Source Network
192.168.1.0/24"] --> R1["R1"] R1 -->|"Standard ACL
applied OUTBOUND
(near destination)"| R2["R2"] R2 --> DEST["Destination
192.168.3.0/24"] SRC -. "Block all 192.168.1.x
from reaching 192.168.3.x" .-> DEST style R1 fill:#fff3cd style R2 fill:#fff3cd style SRC fill:#cce5ff style DEST fill:#ffcccc ``` > [!NOTE] > **Visual Rule of Thumb:** Place **Standard ACLs close to the destination** (because they only see source IP) and **Extended ACLs close to the source** (to drop traffic early and save WAN bandwidth).
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **Differentiate between Standard and Extended ACLs with suitable examples.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Criteria | Standard ACL | Extended ACL | |----------|--------------|--------------| | **Number Range** | 1–99, 1300–1999 | 100–199, 2000–2699 | | **Filter Criteria** | Source IP address only | Source IP, Destination IP, Protocol (TCP/UDP/ICMP), Port numbers | | **Granularity** | Low | High | | **Placement** | Close to destination | Close to source | | **Example** | `access-list 10 deny 192.168.1.0 0.0.0.255` | `access-list 110 deny tcp any 10.0.0.0 0.255.255.255 eq 23` | **Example Use Case**: A Standard ACL is used to block an entire subnet from accessing a network, whereas an Extended ACL is used to block only HTTP (port 80) traffic to a specific server. **(1 mark for differentiation, 1 mark for example, 1 mark for use case)** --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **What is a wildcard mask? Compute the wildcard mask for the network 172.16.32.0/20.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A wildcard mask is a 32-bit quantity used in ACLs that tells the router which bits of the IP address to **ignore** (1 bit) and which bits to **match exactly** (0 bit). It is the bitwise inverse of the subnet mask. **(1 mark for definition)** **Computation for 172.16.32.0/20**: The /20 prefix means 20 network bits. Subnet mask in binary: `11111111.11111111.11110000.00000000` = 255.255.240.0. **(1 mark for conversion)** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Wildcard Mask} &= 255.255.255.255 - 255.255.240.0 \\ &= 0.0.15.255 \end{aligned}$$ **(1 mark for final answer with formula)** --- ## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q.A (a)** Design a network topology with two routers R1 and R2 connected via a serial WAN link. R1's LAN contains Host A (10.1.1.10/24) and Host B (10.1.1.20/24). R2's LAN contains Server S (10.2.2.100/24). Configure an **Extended ACL** to **block Telnet (port 23) access from Host A to Server S**, but allow all other traffic from Host A and all traffic from Host B. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Topology Addressing Table** | Device | Interface | IP Address | Subnet Mask | |--------|-----------|------------|-------------| | R1 | Gi0/0 | 10.1.1.1 | 255.255.255.0 | | R1 | Se0/0/0 | 20.0.0.1 | 255.255.255.252 | | R2 | Se0/0/0 | 20.0.0.2 | 255.255.255.252 | | R2 | Gi0/0 | 10.2.2.1 | 255.255.255.0 | | Host A | NIC | 10.1.1.10 | 255.255.255.0 | | Host B | NIC | 10.1.1.20 | 255.255.255.0 | | Server S | NIC | 10.2.2.100 | 255.255.255.0 | **Step 2: Identify ACL Parameters** - **Source**: Host A = `10.1.1.10`, wildcard `0.0.0.0` (single host) - **Destination**: Server S = `10.2.2.100`, wildcard `0.0.0.0` - **Protocol**: TCP (Telnet) - **Port**: 23 (`telnet`) **Step 3: IOS Configuration on R1** ```cisco enable configure terminal ! Create named extended ACL ip access-list extended BLOCK_TELNET_A_S deny tcp host 10.1.1.10 host 10.2.2.100 eq 23 permit ip any any exit ! Apply inbound on R1's Gi0/0 (close to source) interface GigabitEthernet0/0 ip access-group BLOCK_TELNET_A_S in exit ``` **Step 4: Verification** ```cisco show access-lists show ip interface GigabitEthernet0/0 ``` **Mark Split-up**: Topology diagram (1 mark), IP addressing (1 mark), ACL logic (2 marks), IOS commands (2 marks), Verification & explanation (1 mark). --- **Q.A (b)** What is the **implicit deny** rule in ACLs? Why is it important? Explain with an example what happens if you forget to add a `permit any` statement at the end of an ACL that has only `deny` statements. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution:** **Definition (2 marks):** The implicit deny is an invisible, automatic rule appended by Cisco IOS to the end of every ACL. It denies all traffic that has not matched any preceding explicit ACE. It exists by default and cannot be removed — only overridden by an explicit `permit any` at the end. **Why important (2 marks):** - Ensures the **principle of least privilege** — by default, anything not explicitly allowed is denied. - Provides a **security baseline**: if you forget to permit a service, it is blocked, alerting you to a misconfiguration. - Prevents **accidental open access** to all remaining traffic. **Example Scenario (3 marks):** Consider this ACL with only one deny entry: ```cisco access-list 100 deny tcp host 10.1.1.10 host 10.2.2.100 eq 23 ``` When applied to R1 Gi0/0 inbound: - Telnet from Host A to Server S → matches the deny → blocked. - **All other traffic** (e.g., HTTP from Host A, ICMP ping, FTP) → does not match the deny → falls through to **implicit deny** → **also blocked!** **Correction**: Add `permit ip any any` as the final line. ```cisco access-list 100 deny tcp host 10.1.1.10 host 10.2.2.100 eq 23 access-list 100 permit ip any any ``` > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write only `deny` rules in the ACL and forget the final `permit ip any any`. This results in the network going completely dark, and the student loses **2 full marks** in configuration. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice]` **Q.B (a)** Write the complete Cisco IOS commands to create and apply a **Standard ACL numbered 20** that performs the following: - **Permit** host 192.168.5.10 - **Permit** the entire subnet 192.168.6.0/24 - **Deny** all other traffic Apply this ACL **outbound on interface Gi0/1** of router R1. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Compute Wildcard Masks** - Host 192.168.5.10 → wildcard `0.0.0.0` - Subnet 192.168.6.0/24 → wildcard `0.0.0.255` **Step 2: IOS Commands** ```cisco enable configure terminal ! Create numbered standard ACL access-list 20 permit host 192.168.5.10 access-list 20 permit 192.168.6.0 0.0.0.255 access-list 20 deny any exit ! Apply outbound on Gi0/1 interface GigabitEthernet0/1 ip access-group 20 out exit ! Verification show access-lists 20 show ip interface GigabitEthernet0/1 ``` **Step 3: Expected Output** ``` Standard IP access list 20 10 permit 192.168.5.10 20 permit 192.168.6.0, wildcard bits 0.0.0.255 30 deny any ``` **Mark Split-up**: Wildcard mask calculation (2 marks), ACL syntax (2 marks), Interface application (1 mark), Verification (2 marks). --- **Q.B (b)** A company has three departments: **HR (10.10.1.0/24)**, **Sales (10.10.2.0/24)**, and **IT (10.10.3.0/24)**. They all connect to a router R1. Configure a **Named Extended ACL** to: 1. Block HR from accessing the IT server (10.10.3.100) on **HTTPS (port 443)**. 2. Block Sales from pinging (ICMP) the IT subnet. 3. Permit all other traffic. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Identify ACL entries** | Line | Action | Protocol | Source | Destination | Port | |------|--------|----------|--------|-------------|------| | 10 | deny | tcp | 10.10.1.0 0.0.0.255 | host 10.10.3.100 | eq 443 | | 20 | deny | icmp | 10.10.2.0 0.0.0.255 | 10.10.3.0 0.0.0.255 | — | | 30 | permit | ip | any | any | — | **Step 2: IOS Commands on R1** ```cisco enable configure terminal ip access-list extended DEPT_POLICY deny tcp 10.10.1.0 0.0.0.255 host 10.10.3.100 eq 443 deny icmp 10.10.2.0 0.0.0.255 10.10.3.0 0.0.0.255 permit ip any any exit interface GigabitEthernet0/0 ip access-group DEPT_POLICY in exit ``` **Step 3: Verification Commands** ```cisco show access-lists DEPT_POLICY show running-config | include access-list ``` **Mark Split-up**: ACE table (2 marks), IOS syntax (2 marks), Interface placement reasoning (1 mark), Verification (2 marks). --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Wrong wildcard mask** — confusing subnet mask with wildcard mask. Wildcard = 255.255.255.255 − Subnet Mask. (–2 marks) > 2. **Missing final `permit ip any any`** — leaves network completely blocked. (–1 to –2 marks) > 3. **Wrong direction (`in` vs `out`)** — applying inbound instead of outbound, or vice versa. Extended ACLs should be `in` near source. (–1 mark) > 4. **ACL applied to wrong interface** — Standard ACL near source causes over-blocking. (–2 marks) > 5. **Forgetting `no shutdown`** or `ip address` on interface — even perfect ACL fails if interface is not up. (–1 mark) > 6. **Verifying with `ping` but forgetting to allow ICMP** — gets confused thinking ACL is wrong. (–1 mark) > 7. **Spelling mistakes in named ACL** — `BLOCK-HTTP` works, but `BLOCK HTTP` with space is invalid. (–1 mark) --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Standard ACL**: Filters **source IP only** (numbers 1–99, 1300–1999); place **near destination**. - ✅ **Extended ACL**: Filters **source + dest + protocol + port** (numbers 100–199, 2000–2699); place **near source**. - ✅ **Named ACL**: Use `ip access-list {standard|extended} NAME`; allows descriptive labels and editing with sequence numbers. - ✅ **Wildcard Mask Formula**: $WC = 255.255.255.255 - SM$. Special: `0.0.0.0` = exact host; `255.255.255.255` = any. - ✅ **First Match Wins** — IOS stops checking after the first matching ACE. - ✅ **Implicit Deny** — every ACL has a hidden `deny any` at the end. Always add explicit `permit any` if you want to allow all other traffic. - ✅ **Max 2 ACLs per interface** — 1 inbound + 1 outbound. Cannot apply two inbound ACLs on the same interface. - ✅ **Apply with `ip access-group {in|out}`** on the interface. - ✅ **Verify with**: `show access-lists`, `show ip interface `, `show running-config`. - ✅ **Common well-known ports**: 20/21 (FTP), 22 (SSH), 23 (Telnet), 25 (SMTP), 53 (DNS), 80 (HTTP), 443 (HTTPS), 110 (POP3). - ✅ **Standard ACL Cisco Rule**: "Standard near destination, Extended near source." - ✅ **Lab Tools**: Cisco Packet Tracer 8.x or GNS3 for simulation. - ✅ **Save config**: Always end with `copy running-config startup-config` or `write memory` after successful testing.

topics.title: Configure VLANs and implement Inter-VLAN routing using router on a stick technique
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCITL407 - COMPUTER NETWORKS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Configure VLANs and implement Inter-VLAN routing using router on a stick technique ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition of Key Terms > [!NOTE] > **VLAN (Virtual Local Area Network):** A logical grouping of switch ports (or MAC addresses) into separate, isolated broadcast domains, defined under the **IEEE 802.1Q** standard. Devices in one VLAN cannot directly communicate with devices in another VLAN without the assistance of a Layer 3 routing device. > [!NOTE] > **Inter-VLAN Routing:** The process of forwarding network traffic between different VLANs by using a router (or Layer 3 switch). Each VLAN is a separate IP subnet, and the router acts as the default gateway for hosts in each VLAN. > [!NOTE] > **Router-on-a-Stick (ROAS):** A specific inter-VLAN routing topology where a single physical router interface is logically divided into multiple **sub-interfaces**, each representing a different VLAN. The router connects to a switch via a single **802.1Q trunk link**, and inter-VLAN traffic is routed through this single physical pipe. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Office Building Analogy:** > Imagine a large corporate office building with many floors. > - **VLANs** are like different departments (HR, Finance, Engineering) on the same floor. People in HR talk only to HR unless they need to go to another department. > - **The Switch** is the corridor on each floor — it connects offices (ports) within the department. > - **The Router** is the central elevator connecting all floors. > - **Router-on-a-Stick** is a **single elevator shaft (one physical cable)** that has multiple **buttons (sub-interfaces)** for each floor. The elevator knows which floor to deliver the packet to based on a tag (802.1Q) attached to it. > - **802.1Q Tag** is the floor number written on every envelope entering the elevator. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants - **MTU Overhead (802.1Q):** **4 bytes** added to every Ethernet frame on a trunk link. - **Native VLAN:** Default VLAN is **VLAN 1** (untagged traffic on trunk). - **VLAN ID Range:** **1 – 4094** (usable: 1–4094; 0 and 4095 reserved). - **Sub-interface Limit (Cisco):** Practically up to ~**1000+ per physical interface**. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference ### 2.1 Why VLANs? — Operational Reasoning - **Broadcast Containment:** Broadcasts (ARP, DHCP) are confined to a VLAN, reducing network-wide flooding. - **Security:** Sensitive traffic (Finance) is isolated from general traffic (Students). - **Logical Segmentation:** Departments can be grouped across physical switches, not bound by physical location. - **Cost Reduction:** Fewer routers and firewalls required for segmentation. ### 2.2 Why Inter-VLAN Routing? By default, switches operate at **Layer 2** and forward frames only within the same VLAN/broadcast domain. To allow **HR ↔ Finance** communication, a **Layer 3 device (router)** is mandatory because: > [!NOTE] > Different VLANs = Different IP Subnets = Requires Routing for inter-subnet communication. ### 2.3 Router-on-a-Stick — Operational Logic 1. Switch has ports assigned to multiple VLANs (e.g., VLAN 10, VLAN 20). 2. One port on the switch is configured as a **trunk port** (carries traffic for multiple VLANs, tagged with 802.1Q). 3. This trunk port is connected to a single physical interface on the router. 4. The single physical interface is split into **logical sub-interfaces** (e.g., `fa0/0.10`, `fa0/0.20`). 5. Each sub-interface is bound to a specific VLAN via the `encapsulation dot1Q ` command. 6. Each sub-interface gets the **default gateway IP** for its VLAN's subnet. 7. Routing between sub-interfaces is **automatically enabled** on a router (IP routing is on by default). ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Reference Table | Concept | Command / Value | Description | |---|---|---| | Create VLAN | `vlan ` | Enters VLAN configuration mode | | Name VLAN | `name ` | Assigns name to VLAN | | Assign port | `switchport access vlan ` | Static port-to-VLAN assignment | | Set trunk mode | `switchport mode trunk` | Enables 802.1Q tagging on port | | Trunk encapsulation (older) | `switchport trunk encapsulation dot1q` | Mandatory on older Cisco switches | | Sub-interface | `interface fa0/0.` | Creates logical sub-interface | | Bind to VLAN | `encapsulation dot1Q ` | Ties sub-interface to a VLAN | | Gateway IP | `ip address ` | Default gateway for VLAN hosts | | Native VLAN | `switchport trunk native vlan ` | Untagged traffic VLAN (default = 1) | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world Engineering Utility:** ROAS is widely used in **small-to-medium enterprise branch offices**, **lab environments**, and **CCNA/CCNP training simulations** because it eliminates the need for multiple physical router interfaces — a major cost and cabling saver. ### 2.5 The 802.1Q Frame Format A normal Ethernet frame is modified by inserting a **4-byte 802.1Q tag** between the Source MAC and EtherType fields. $$ \text{Normal Frame} = \text{Dest MAC} \vert \text{Src MAC} \vert \text{EtherType} \vert \text{Data} \vert \text{FCS} $$ $$ \text{802.1Q Frame} = \text{Dest MAC} \vert \text{Src MAC} \vert \mathbf{802.1Q\ Tag} \vert \text{EtherType} \vert \text{Data} \vert \text{FCS} $$ Where the 4-byte tag contains: - **TPID (2 bytes):** Fixed value `0x8100` indicating an 802.1Q frame. - **PCP (3 bits):** Priority Code Point for QoS. - **DEI (1 bit):** Drop Eligible Indicator. - **VID (12 bits):** VLAN Identifier (range 0–4095). ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedure & Analytical Walkthrough ### 3.1 Sample Lab Scenario > **Scenario:** Design a network with two VLANs — **VLAN 10 (Students)** and **VLAN 20 (Faculty)**. Configure ROAS so a student PC can ping the faculty PC. **IP Addressing Plan:** | Device | VLAN | IP Address | Subnet Mask | Default Gateway | |---|---|---|---|---| | Student PC | 10 | 192.168.10.10 | 255.255.255.0 | 192.168.10.1 | | Faculty PC | 20 | 192.168.20.10 | 255.255.255.0 | 192.168.20.1 | | Router Sub-if | 10 | 192.168.10.1 | 255.255.255.0 | — | | Router Sub-if | 20 | 192.168.20.1 | 255.255.255.0 | — | ### 3.2 Switch Configuration (Step-by-Step) ```cisco Switch> enable Switch# configure terminal Switch(config)# hostname SW1 SW1(config)# vlan 10 SW1(config-vlan)# name STUDENTS SW1(config-vlan)# exit SW1(config)# vlan 20 SW1(config-vlan)# name FACULTY SW1(config-vlan)# exit ! Assign Student PC port to VLAN 10 SW1(config)# interface fa0/1 SW1(config-if)# switchport mode access SW1(config-if)# switchport access vlan 10 SW1(config-if)# exit ! Assign Faculty PC port to VLAN 20 SW1(config)# interface fa0/2 SW1(config-if)# switchport mode access SW1(config-if)# switchport access vlan 20 SW1(config-if)# exit ! Configure trunk port to Router SW1(config)# interface fa0/3 SW1(config-if)# switchport mode trunk SW1(config-if)# switchport trunk encapsulation dot1q SW1(config-if)# exit ``` **Logic:** The `switchport mode access` command hard-binds the port to one VLAN (untagged). The `switchport mode trunk` command on `fa0/3` allows the port to carry **tagged** traffic for **all VLANs** to the router. ### 3.3 Router Configuration (Step-by-Step) ```cisco Router> enable Router# configure terminal Router(config)# hostname R1 R1(config)# interface fa0/0 R1(config-if)# no shutdown R1(config-if)# exit ! Sub-interface for VLAN 10 R1(config)# interface fa0/0.10 R1(config-subif)# encapsulation dot1Q 10 R1(config-subif)# ip address 192.168.10.1 255.255.255.0 R1(config-subif)# exit ! Sub-interface for VLAN 20 R1(config)# interface fa0/0.20 R1(config-subif)# encapsulation dot1Q 20 R1(config-subif)# ip address 192.168.20.1 255.255.255.0 R1(config-subif)# exit ``` **Logic:** The parent interface `fa0/0` is activated with `no shutdown` to bring the physical link UP. Sub-interfaces `fa0/0.10` and `fa0/0.20` are **logical children** of `fa0/0`. The `encapsulation dot1Q 10` tells the router to **strip the 802.1Q tag with VLAN ID 10** and forward the frame to that sub-interface. ### 3.4 Host Configuration **On Student PC:** - IP: `192.168.10.10` - Mask: `255.255.255.0` - Gateway: `192.168.10.1` **On Faculty PC:** - IP: `192.168.20.10` - Mask: `255.255.255.0` - Gateway: `192.168.20.1` ### 3.5 Verification Commands ```cisco ! On Switch SW1# show vlan brief SW1# show interfaces trunk ! On Router R1# show ip interface brief R1# show interfaces fa0/0.10 R1# show ip route ``` ### 3.6 Packet Flow Derivation (Analytical Walkthrough) **Step 1:** Student PC wants to ping Faculty PC (`192.168.20.10`). **Step 2:** Student compares `192.168.20.10` with its own subnet `192.168.10.0/24`. Destination is **remote**, so packet is sent to default gateway `192.168.10.1`. **Step 3:** Student ARPs for `192.168.10.1` (the router). Switch forwards ARP within VLAN 10. Router responds with its `fa0/0.10` MAC. **Step 4:** Student builds an Ethernet frame: - **Dest MAC:** Router's `fa0/0.10` MAC - **Src MAC:** Student's MAC - **Payload:** ICMP Echo Request destined to `192.168.20.10` **Step 5:** Switch receives frame on `fa0/1` (VLAN 10 access port). Since egress port is `fa0/3` (trunk), switch **inserts 802.1Q tag with VLAN ID 10** and sends out trunk. **Step 6:** Router receives tagged frame on `fa0/0`. It matches tag `10` to sub-interface `fa0/0.10`. It strips the tag, looks at the destination IP. **Step 7:** Router routing table: `192.168.20.0/24` is connected via `fa0/0.20`. Router ARPs for `192.168.20.10` (on sub-interface `fa0/0.20`). **Step 8:** Router rebuilds the frame: - **Dest MAC:** Faculty PC's MAC - **Src MAC:** Router's `fa0/0.20` MAC - **Tag:** `encapsulation dot1Q 20` adds **VLAN 20 tag** **Step 9:** Switch receives tagged frame on trunk `fa0/3`, sees VLAN 20 tag, strips tag (for access egress), and forwards out `fa0/2` to Faculty PC. **Step 10:** Faculty PC replies → process reverses. ### 3.7 Mathematical Summary — Subnet Validation Subnet check confirms hosts are in different broadcast domains: $$ 192.168.10.0/24 \rightarrow \text{Network} = 192.168.10.0,\ \text{Range} = 192.168.10.1 - 192.168.10.254 $$ $$ 192.168.20.0/24 \rightarrow \text{Network} = 192.168.20.0,\ \text{Range} = 192.168.20.1 - 192.168.20.254 $$ Since `192.168.20.10` is **not** in the `192.168.10.0/24` range, the host correctly routes to the gateway. ✅ ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Network Topology — Router-on-a-Stick ```mermaid graph TD PC1["PC STUDENT
192.168.10.10
GW 192.168.10.1"]:::pc PC2["PC FACULTY
192.168.20.10
GW 192.168.20.1"]:::pc SW1["SWITCH SW1
Fa0-1 VLAN 10 Access
Fa0-2 VLAN 20 Access
Fa0-3 TRUNK to Router"]:::switch RTR["ROUTER R1
Fa0-0 Physical UP
Fa0-0.10 VLAN 10 Sub-if
Fa0-0.20 VLAN 20 Sub-if"]:::router PC1 ---|Fa0-1 VLAN 10| SW1 PC2 ---|Fa0-2 VLAN 20| SW1 SW1 ---|Fa0-3 802.1Q Trunk| RTR classDef pc fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000 classDef switch fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000 classDef router fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Packet Flow Diagram — Student to Faculty Ping ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant S as Student PC 192.168.10.10 participant SW as Switch SW1 participant R as Router R1 participant F as Faculty PC 192.168.20.10 S->>SW: ARP Request for 192.168.10.1 SW->>R: ARP Request forwarded (VLAN 10) R->>SW: ARP Reply (fa0/0.10 MAC) SW->>S: ARP Reply delivered S->>SW: ICMP Echo to 192.168.20.10 (Untagged) SW->>R: ICMP Echo with 802.1Q Tag VLAN 10 Note over R: Strip tag, route to fa0/0.20 R->>SW: ICMP Echo with 802.1Q Tag VLAN 20 SW->>F: ICMP Echo Untagged on fa0/2 F->>SW: ICMP Reply Untagged SW->>R: ICMP Reply with Tag VLAN 20 Note over R: Route back to fa0/0.10 R->>SW: ICMP Reply with Tag VLAN 10 SW->>S: ICMP Reply Untagged on fa0/1 ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid graph LR VLAN10["VLAN 10 BROADCAST DOMAIN
Subnet 192.168.10.0/24"]:::vlanA VLAN20["VLAN 20 BROADCAST DOMAIN
Subnet 192.168.20.0/24"]:::vlanB TRUNK["802.1Q TRUNK LINK
Single Physical Cable
Tagged Frames Only"]:::trunk SUBIF10["SUB-INTERFACE fa0/0.10
Encapsulation dot1Q 10
IP 192.168.10.1/24"]:::subif SUBIF20["SUB-INTERFACE fa0/0.20
Encapsulation dot1Q 20
IP 192.168.20.1/24"]:::subif ROUTER["ROUTER INTERNAL ROUTING TABLE
Static or Connected Routes
Routes between sub-interfaces"]:::rt VLAN10 -->|Tagged Tag 10| TRUNK VLAN20 -->|Tagged Tag 20| TRUNK TRUNK --> SUBIF10 TRUNK --> SUBIF20 SUBIF10 <--> ROUTER SUBIF20 <--> ROUTER classDef vlanA fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000 classDef vlanB fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000 classDef trunk fill:#cff4fc,stroke:#0dcaf0,color:#000 classDef subif fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000 classDef rt fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#6c757d,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** Notice that the trunk link carries **both VLANs simultaneously** through **tag identification**. The router's internal routing table treats each sub-interface as a separate logical network, enabling Layer 3 forwarding between VLANs. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question]** **Define VLAN. List any three advantages of VLANs.** **Model Answer:** > A **Virtual Local Area Network (VLAN)** is a logical grouping of switch ports into separate broadcast domains, defined by the IEEE 802.1Q standard. Devices in one VLAN cannot directly communicate with devices in another VLAN without a Layer 3 device. **Advantages of VLANs:** 1. **Broadcast Domain Segmentation** — Reduces unnecessary broadcast traffic and improves performance. 2. **Enhanced Security** — Sensitive data traffic (e.g., Finance) is isolated from other departments. 3. **Cost Reduction** — Eliminates the need for additional physical routers/switches for segmentation. *(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark for 3 advantages)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question]** **What is the "Router-on-a-Stick" technique? Why is it preferred over traditional inter-VLAN routing?** **Model Answer:** > The **Router-on-a-Stick (ROAS)** technique uses a **single physical router interface** divided into multiple **logical sub-interfaces**, each associated with a different VLAN via IEEE 802.1Q trunking. It allows inter-VLAN communication through one physical link between the router and switch. **Why preferred:** 1. **Single physical interface** is sufficient for multiple VLANs → reduces hardware cost. 2. **Reduced cabling complexity** between router and switch. 3. **Scalable and easy to expand** by adding new sub-interfaces. *(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks for justification)* --- ### Part B Question A (14 Marks) **Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Model Question]** **(a)** Design a network topology with **3 VLANs** (VLAN 10 – HR, VLAN 20 – Finance, VLAN 30 – IT). Assign IP subnets and configure Router-on-a-Stick. Draw the topology diagram. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the step-by-step **Switch and Router configuration** for the above design. Show all relevant IOS commands. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(a): **IP Addressing Plan:** | VLAN | Name | Subnet | Gateway | |---|---|---|---| | 10 | HR | 192.168.10.0/24 | 192.168.10.1 | | 20 | Finance | 192.168.20.0/24 | 192.168.20.1 | | 30 | IT | 192.168.30.0/24 | 192.168.30.1 | **Topology:** ```mermaid graph TD HR["HR PC
192.168.10.10
VLAN 10"]:::a FIN["Finance PC
192.168.20.10
VLAN 20"]:::b IT["IT PC
192.168.30.10
VLAN 30"]:::c SW["SWITCH
Fa0-1 V10
Fa0-2 V20
Fa0-3 V30
Fa0-4 TRUNK"]:::d RT["ROUTER
Fa0-0
.10 .20 .30"]:::e HR --- SW FIN --- SW IT --- SW SW ---|802.1Q Trunk| RT classDef a fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd classDef b fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754 classDef c fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545 classDef d fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107 classDef e fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#6c757d ``` *(7 marks: 3 marks IP plan + 2 marks diagram + 2 marks justification)* #### Model Solution for Q.A(b): **Switch Configuration:** ```cisco Switch> enable Switch# configure terminal Switch(config)# vlan 10 Switch(config-vlan)# name HR Switch(config-vlan)# exit Switch(config)# vlan 20 Switch(config-vlan)# name FINANCE Switch(config-vlan)# exit Switch(config)# vlan 30 Switch(config-vlan)# name IT Switch(config-vlan)# exit ! Access ports Switch(config)# interface fa0/1 Switch(config-if)# switchport mode access Switch(config-if)# switchport access vlan 10 Switch(config-if)# exit Switch(config)# interface fa0/2 Switch(config-if)# switchport mode access Switch(config-if)# switchport access vlan 20 Switch(config-if)# exit Switch(config)# interface fa0/3 Switch(config-if)# switchport mode access Switch(config-if)# switchport access vlan 30 Switch(config-if)# exit ! Trunk to router Switch(config)# interface fa0/4 Switch(config-if)# switchport mode trunk Switch(config-if)# switchport trunk encapsulation dot1q Switch(config-if)# exit ``` **Router Configuration:** ```cisco Router> enable Router# configure terminal Router(config)# interface fa0/0 Router(config-if)# no shutdown Router(config-if)# exit Router(config)# interface fa0/0.10 Router(config-subif)# encapsulation dot1Q 10 Router(config-subif)# ip address 192.168.10.1 255.255.255.0 Router(config-subif)# exit Router(config)# interface fa0/0.20 Router(config-subif)# encapsulation dot1Q 20 Router(config-subif)# ip address 192.168.20.1 255.255.255.0 Router(config-subif)# exit Router(config)# interface fa0/0.30 Router(config-subif)# encapsulation dot1Q 30 Router(config-subif)# ip address 192.168.30.1 255.255.255.0 Router(config-subif)# exit ``` **Verification:** - `show vlan brief` on switch → confirm VLANs 10, 20, 30 created. - `show interfaces trunk` → confirm fa0/4 is trunking. - `show ip interface brief` on router → confirm sub-interfaces UP. - Ping test: HR PC → IT PC should succeed. *(7 marks: 3 marks switch config + 3 marks router config + 1 mark verification)* --- ### Part B Question B (14 Marks) **Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Model Question]** **(a)** Compare **Traditional Inter-VLAN Routing** and **Router-on-a-Stick** with a neat comparison table. Mention at least **five** comparison points. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **802.1Q frame format** in detail. How is a tagged frame different from an untagged Ethernet frame? Illustrate with diagrams. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(a): | S.No. | Parameter | Traditional Inter-VLAN Routing | Router-on-a-Stick (ROAS) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Physical interfaces used | One physical interface per VLAN | One physical interface for all VLANs (sub-interfaces) | | 2 | Cabling | One cable per VLAN between switch and router | Single trunk cable | | 3 | Cost | High (more interfaces/cables) | Low (single interface/cable) | | 4 | Scalability | Limited by physical ports | Highly scalable (sub-interfaces) | | 5 | Configuration complexity | Simple (one interface per VLAN) | Moderate (sub-interfaces + encapsulation) | | 6 | Bandwidth utilization | Dedicated per VLAN | Shared trunk bandwidth | | 7 | Use case | Small networks, legacy | Medium networks, modern designs | *(7 marks: 1 mark per row + 1 mark for overall conclusion)* #### Model Solution for Q.B(b): **802.1Q Frame Format:** **Untagged (Normal) Ethernet II Frame:** $$ \text{Dest MAC (6B)} \vert \text{Src MAC (6B)} \vert \text{EtherType (2B)} \vert \text{Payload (46-1500B)} \vert \text{FCS (4B)} $$ **802.1Q Tagged Frame:** $$ \text{Dest MAC (6B)} \vert \text{Src MAC (6B)} \vert \textbf{TPID (2B)} \vert \textbf{TCI (2B)} \vert \text{EtherType (2B)} \vert \text{Payload} \vert \text{FCS (4B)} $$ **Detailed TCI (Tag Control Information) – 2 bytes:** | Field | Size | Purpose | |---|---|---| | PCP (Priority Code Point) | 3 bits | QoS priority (0–7) | | DEI (Drop Eligible Indicator) | 1 bit | Marks frames eligible to drop in congestion | | VID (VLAN Identifier) | 12 bits | VLAN number (0–4095) | **Diagram:** ```mermaid graph LR subgraph UNTAGGED ["UNTAGGED FRAME 1518 bytes max"] A1["Dest MAC
6 bytes"]:::h A2["Src MAC
6 bytes"]:::h A3["EtherType
2 bytes"]:::h A4["Payload
46-1500 bytes"]:::h A5["FCS
4 bytes"]:::h A1 --- A2 --- A3 --- A4 --- A5 end subgraph TAGGED ["802.1Q TAGGED FRAME 1522 bytes max"] B1["Dest MAC
6 bytes"]:::i B2["Src MAC
6 bytes"]:::i B3["TPID 0x8100
2 bytes"]:::j B4["PCP 3b DEI 1b VID 12b
2 bytes total"]:::j B5["EtherType
2 bytes"]:::i B6["Payload
46-1500 bytes"]:::i B7["FCS
4 bytes"]:::i B1 --- B2 --- B3 --- B4 --- B5 --- B6 --- B7 end classDef h fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#6c757d,color:#000 classDef i fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000 classDef j fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000 ``` **Key Differences:** - 802.1Q inserts a **4-byte tag** after Source MAC. - Maximum frame size increases from **1518 bytes** to **1522 bytes**. - Tag identifies **VLAN membership** of the frame. - Switches strip tags before sending to access (untagged) ports. *(7 marks: 3 marks frame format + 2 marks TCI breakdown + 2 marks diagram/difference)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting `no shutdown` on the parent router interface** — Sub-interfaces will not come UP. *Lose 1 mark.* > 2. **Mismatching VLAN IDs** between switch `access vlan` and router `encapsulation dot1Q`. *Lose 2 marks.* > 3. **Not setting default gateway on PCs** — Ping will fail even with correct router config. *Lose 1 mark.* > 4. **Confusing `switchport mode access` vs `trunk`** — Access ports do NOT tag frames. *Lose 2 marks.* > 5. **Wrong IP/subnet assignment** (e.g., assigning VLAN 10 hosts in `192.168.20.0` subnet) — routing table will be inconsistent. *Lose 2 marks.* > 6. **Forgetting `ip routing`** (only required if using a Layer 3 switch, NOT for ROAS router — IP routing is on by default in routers, but examiners may test this). *Lose 1 mark.* --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **VLAN = Broadcast Domain Segmentation at Layer 2.** - **Inter-VLAN Routing = Layer 3 communication between VLANs.** - **ROAS = Single physical interface, multiple sub-interfaces, one per VLAN.** - **802.1Q = Standard tagging protocol; 4-byte tag added to frame.** - **Native VLAN = VLAN 1 by default; frames on native VLAN are untagged on trunk.** - **Always do `no shutdown` on parent router interface before sub-interfaces.** - **Sub-interface number does NOT have to match VLAN ID** (convention only, not required). - **Maximum 802.1Q frame size = 1522 bytes** (1518 + 4-byte tag). - **Default gateway on every host = IP of the corresponding sub-interface.** - **Verification commands: `show vlan brief`, `show interfaces trunk`, `show ip interface brief`, `show ip route`.** - **Trunk port must be explicitly configured — it is NOT automatic.** - **Modern switches auto-negotiate trunk** (using DTP), but for lab purposes, **manually set trunk mode** for deterministic behavior. - **VLANs 1 and 1002–1005 are reserved** — do not use for user data. - **Private VLANs, Voice VLANs, Management VLAN** are advanced extensions built on the base VLAN concept.

topics.title: Configure TELNET, login to a remote machine and view the files on the remote machine; Configure FTP and transfer files between two machines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITL407 - COMPUTER NETWORKS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Configure TELNET, login to a remote machine and view the files on the remote machine; Configure FTP and transfer files between two machines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is TELNET?

**TELNET (Telecommunication Network Protocol)** is an application layer protocol standardized under **RFC 854** that provides a **bidirectional, interactive, text-oriented communication facility** between two networked devices using a virtual terminal connection. It operates by default on **TCP port 23** and allows a user on one machine (the client) to **log in remotely** to another machine (the server) and execute commands as if sitting in front of it.

TELNET uses a client–server architecture and follows the principle of a **Network Virtual Terminal (NVT)** — an abstract device that acts as a common language between heterogeneous systems.

## 1.2 What is FTP?

**File Transfer Protocol (FTP)** is an application layer protocol standardized under **RFC 959** that is used to **transfer files between a client and a server** over a TCP/IP network. It is built on a **client-server model** and uses **two separate channels**:

- **Control Channel** on **TCP port 21** — used for sending commands and receiving replies.
- **Data Channel** on **TCP port 20** — used for the actual file/data transfer.

FTP supports authentication via **username and password** and allows operations such as uploading, downloading, renaming, deleting, and listing files.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCITL407 – Module 2):**
> - **TELNET** is studied under *Remote Login Protocols* — focus is on **remote command execution**, **NVT translation**, and **port 23**.
> - **FTP** is studied under *File Transfer Protocols* — focus is on **dual-channel architecture**, **active vs. passive modes**, and **commands like `get`/`put`**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

### Analogy for TELNET — *The "Universal Translator" Phone Call*

Imagine you speak **Malayalam** at home and your friend speaks **Tamil**. To talk over the phone, you hire a translator in the middle. Both of you speak your own language; the translator converts it to a **common neutral language (English)** before transmitting. The other translator then converts it back.

TELNET works exactly the same way:
- Your local terminal uses your OS-specific character set (e.g., ASCII variants).
- TELNET converts everything into a **common language called NVT (Network Virtual Terminal)**.
- The remote server receives it and converts back to its own format.
- Result: You can remotely log in to *any* machine, regardless of operating system or terminal type.

### Analogy for FTP — *The Two-Receptionist Office*

Think of FTP as a government office:
- **Receptionist 1** (Control Channel, Port 21): Stays on the phone the **entire time** to take your commands — *"Get me file A", "Show me the directory"*. This line never hangs up.
- **Receptionist 2** (Data Channel, Port 20): Only connects **temporarily** whenever a real file or folder listing needs to be physically handed over.

This is why FTP uses **two separate connections** — one for *conversation* (commands) and one for *delivery* (data).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why TELNET is rarely used today:** TELNET transmits everything in **plain text**, including passwords. Modern equivalents like **SSH (Secure Shell)** encrypt traffic. However, TELNET is still taught in KTU because it forms the **conceptual foundation** for all remote-access protocols.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 TELNET — Deep Theoretical Analysis

### 2.1.1 Three Core Services Provided by TELNET

1. **Network Virtual Terminal (NVT)** — A standard representation that allows interoperability between different terminal types.
2. **Negotiation of Options** — Client and server agree on terminal characteristics (echo mode, character set, window size) using TELNET commands like `WILL`, `WONT`, `DO`, `DONT`.
3. **Symmetric Connection** — Either side can negotiate options; the protocol is peer-to-peer at the application layer even though it looks client-server.

### 2.1.2 TELNET Protocol Stack

TELNET sits at the top of the TCP/IP stack and uses TCP for reliability:

$$\text{Application Layer (TELNET)} \rightarrow \text{Transport Layer (TCP)} \rightarrow \text{Network Layer (IP)} \rightarrow \text{Data Link + Physical}$$

### 2.1.3 Operation Flow

1. **Connection Establishment:** Client initiates a TCP three-way handshake with the server on **port 23**.
2. **Option Negotiation:** Both sides exchange WILL/WONT/DO/DONT commands to agree on terminal features.
3. **Data Transfer:** User keystrokes and server responses flow as NVT-ASCII bytes over the same TCP connection.
4. **Connection Termination:** Either side closes the TCP connection.

### 2.1.4 NVT — The Heart of TELNET

NVT defines a very simple virtual device:
- Uses **7-bit ASCII** characters for data.
- Uses **8-bit bytes** for control commands.
- End-of-line is represented as **`CR (Carriage Return) + LF (Line Feed)`** — i.e., `\r\n`.

### 2.1.5 TELNET Command Codes (Important for Exams)

| Code | Decimal | Meaning |
|:----:|:-------:|:--------|
| IAC  | 255     | Interpret As Command |
| WILL | 251     | Sender wants to enable an option |
| WONT | 252     | Sender refuses to enable an option |
| DO   | 253     | Sender requests receiver to enable option |
| DONT | 254     | Sender requests receiver to disable option |

## 2.2 FTP — Deep Theoretical Analysis

### 2.2.1 FTP Architecture

FTP uses a **two-channel, two-port model**:

| Channel | Default Port | Purpose |
|:-------:|:------------:|:--------|
| Control Connection | **TCP 21** | Carries commands (USER, PASS, LIST, RETR, STOR) and replies (codes like 220, 331, 226) |
| Data Connection | **TCP 20** | Carries actual file data or directory listings |

### 2.2.2 Active vs. Passive Mode

| Feature | **Active Mode (PORT)** | **Passive Mode (PASV)** |
|:-------:|:----------------------:|:-----------------------:|
| Who initiates data connection? | **Server** connects back to client | **Client** connects to server |
| Data port | Server port **20** | Server opens a **random high port** (>1023) |
| Client firewall issue | Problematic (server's incoming connection blocked) | Solves client-side firewall issues |
| Command used | `PORT` | `PASV` |
| Default in browsers? | No | Yes |

### 2.2.3 FTP Reply Codes (Must-Know for KTU)

| Code | Meaning |
|:----:|:--------|
| 220  | Service ready |
| 331  | User name okay, need password |
| 230  | User logged in, proceed |
| 530  | Not logged in |
| 226  | Closing data connection, transfer successful |
| 425  | Can't open data connection |
| 550  | Requested action not taken (file unavailable) |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Formula & Fact Table)

| Parameter | TELNET | FTP |
|:----------|:------:|:---:|
| **Default Port** | **TCP 23** | **TCP 21 (control), TCP 20 (data)** |
| **Underlying Transport** | TCP | TCP |
| **RFC Standard** | RFC 854 | RFC 959 |
| **Encryption** | ❌ Plain text | ❌ Plain text |
| **Authentication** | OS-level login | Username + Password |
| **Data Format** | NVT-ASCII | Binary or ASCII mode |
| **Channels Used** | 1 (combined) | 2 (control + data) |
| **Security Successor** | SSH (Port 22) | SFTP / FTPS |
| **Typical Commands** | `open`, `close`, `quit`, `status` | `get`, `put`, `mget`, `mput`, `ls`, `cd`, `bye` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** TELNET and FTP form the bedrock of **remote administration** and **file exchange** in enterprise networks. Even though SSH/SFTP have replaced them in production, **every router, switch, and IoT device** still supports TELNET/FTP as a fallback management plane — making these protocols essential knowledge for network engineers.

### Key Engineering Calculation — File Transfer Time

When a file of size $S$ bits is transferred over a link of bandwidth $B$ bits per second, the minimum transfer time is:

$$T_{transfer} = \frac{S}{B} \quad \text{(seconds)}$$

**Example:** A **4 MB** file over a **10 Mbps** link:

$$S = 4 \times 1024 \times 1024 \times 8 = 33{,}554{,}432 \text{ bits}$$

$$T = \frac{33{,}554{,}432}{10 \times 10^6} \approx 3.36 \text{ seconds}$$

In practice, this is higher due to **TCP overhead**, **control channel latency**, and **FTP command-response overhead** between control and data channels.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Procedure & Analytical Matrix

This section provides the **complete, lab-executable procedure** for both experiments — modeled exactly on KTU 2024 Scheme lab manual expectations.

## 3.1 EXPERIMENT 2A — Configuring TELNET

### 3.1.1 Aim
To configure the TELNET service on a Linux (Ubuntu) server, log in from a remote client, and view files on the remote machine.

### 3.1.2 Prerequisites
- Two machines on the same LAN (or one server + one client using VMs/loopback).
- **Server IP** (e.g., `192.168.1.10`) and **Client IP** (e.g., `192.168.1.20`).
- Sudo/root access on the server.
- Packages: `xinetd`, `telnetd` (or `inetutils-telnetd`).

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Procedure on the **Server**

**Step 1 — Install TELNET Server Components**

Open a terminal on the server and run:

```bash
sudo apt update
sudo apt install -y xinetd telnetd
```

**Explanation:** `xinetd` is the *super-server* that listens on behalf of TELNET and spawns the daemon when a connection arrives. `telnetd` is the actual TELNET daemon.

**Step 2 — Verify TELNET Configuration File**

TELNET under `xinetd` is configured via `/etc/xinetd.d/telnet`:

```bash
sudo cat /etc/xinetd.d/telnet
```

The file should contain:

```bash
service telnet
{
    disable         = no
    socket_type     = stream
    protocol        = tcp
    wait            = no
    user            = root
    server          = /usr/sbin/in.telnetd
    server_args     = -h
}
```

**Explanation:** `disable = no` **enables** the TELNET service. `socket_type = stream` indicates TCP. `server` points to the actual TELNET daemon binary.

**Step 3 — Restart xinetd Service**

```bash
sudo systemctl restart xinetd
sudo systemctl status xinetd
```

**Step 4 — Allow TELNET Through the Firewall**

```bash
sudo ufw allow 23/tcp
sudo ufw reload
```

**Step 5 — Verify the Server is Listening on Port 23**

