```bash
sudo ss -tlnp | grep :23
```

Expected output:

```text
LISTEN  0  5  0.0.0.0:23  0.0.0.0:*  users:(("in.telnetd",pid=1234,fd=4))
```

**Step 6 — Create a Test User (Optional but Recommended)**

```bash
sudo useradd -m telnetuser
sudo passwd telnetuser
```

### 3.1.4 Step-by-Step Procedure on the **Client**

**Step 7 — Install TELNET Client (if not present)**

```bash
sudo apt install -y telnet
```

**Step 8 — Connect to the Remote Server**

```bash
telnet 192.168.1.10
```

**Step 9 — Login Using Server Credentials**

```text
Ubuntu 22.04 LTS
login: telnetuser
Password: ********
Welcome to Ubuntu
$
```

**Step 10 — View Files on the Remote Machine**

```bash
ls          # list files in current directory
ls -la      # list files with permissions
pwd         # print working directory
cd /home    # change directory
cat file.txt
```

**Step 11 — Exit the Remote Session**

```bash
exit
```

### 3.1.5 Expected Output Snapshot

```text
$ telnet 192.168.1.10
Trying 192.168.1.10...
Connected to 192.168.1.10.
Escape character is '^]'.

Ubuntu 22.04 LTS
server login: telnetuser
Password:
Last login: Mon Oct  7 10:30:22 from 192.168.1.20
$ ls
file1.txt  file2.txt  Documents  Downloads
$ pwd
/home/telnetuser
$ exit
Connection closed by foreign host.
```

### 3.1.6 Result
The TELNET service was successfully configured on the server, and the client was able to log in remotely and view files on the server.

---

## 3.2 EXPERIMENT 2B — Configuring FTP

### 3.2.1 Aim
To configure an FTP server using **vsftpd**, connect from a remote client, and transfer files between the two machines using `get` and `put`.

### 3.2.2 Prerequisites
- Two machines on the same LAN.
- **Server IP** (e.g., `192.168.1.10`), **Client IP** (e.g., `192.168.1.20`).
- Package: `vsftpd` (Very Secure FTP Daemon — the standard on Ubuntu).

### 3.2.3 Step-by-Step Procedure on the **FTP Server**

**Step 1 — Install vsftpd**

```bash
sudo apt update
sudo apt install -y vsftpd
```

**Step 2 — Back Up the Original Configuration**

```bash
sudo cp /etc/vsftpd.conf /etc/vsftpd.conf.bak
```

**Step 3 — Edit the Configuration File**

```bash
sudo nano /etc/vsftpd.conf
```

Make sure the following directives are set (uncomment or add):

```bash
listen=YES
anonymous_enable=NO          # Disable anonymous login
local_enable=YES             # Allow local users
write_enable=YES             # Allow upload (PUT)
chroot_local_user=YES        # Restrict users to their home dir
allow_writeable_chroot=YES
pam_service_name=vsftpd
pasv_enable=YES
pasv_min_port=40000
pasv_max_port=50000
```

**Explanation:**
- `listen=YES` makes vsftpd run as a standalone daemon (not under xinetd).
- `local_enable=YES` allows real OS users to log in via FTP.
- `write_enable=YES` is required for `put` (upload) operations.
- `chroot_local_user=YES` locks users to their home directory — a critical security feature.

**Step 4 — Restart and Enable vsftpd**

```bash
sudo systemctl restart vsftpd
sudo systemctl enable vsftpd
sudo systemctl status vsftpd
```

**Step 5 — Open FTP Ports in the Firewall**

```bash
sudo ufw allow 20/tcp
sudo ufw allow 21/tcp
sudo ufw allow 40000:50000/tcp
sudo ufw reload
```

**Step 6 — Create an FTP User**

```bash
sudo useradd -m ftpuser
sudo passwd ftpuser
```

**Step 7 — Create a Sample File on the Server**

```bash
echo "This is a test file from the FTP server." | sudo tee /home/ftpuser/server_file.txt
sudo chown ftpuser:ftpuser /home/ftpuser/server_file.txt
```

### 3.2.4 Step-by-Step Procedure on the **FTP Client**

**Step 8 — Install FTP Client**

```bash
sudo apt install -y ftp
```

**Step 9 — Connect to the FTP Server**

```bash
ftp 192.168.1.10
```

**Step 10 — Login**

```text
Name (192.168.1.10:clientuser): ftpuser
331 Please specify the password.
Password: ********
230 Login successful.
Remote system type is UNIX.
Using binary mode to transfer files.
ftp>
```

**Step 11 — Navigate and List Files on the Server**

```bash
ftp> ls              # list files
ftp> pwd             # show current server directory
ftp> cd Documents    # change directory
ftp> ls -la          # detailed listing
```

**Step 12 — Download a File from Server (GET)**

```bash
ftp> get server_file.txt
```

Expected output:

```text
local: server_file.txt remote: server_file.txt
229 Entering Extended Passive Mode (|||40001|).
150 Opening BINARY mode data connection for server_file.txt (45 bytes).
100% |*******|    45      1.21 KiB/s    00:00s
226 Transfer complete.
45 bytes received in 0.03 secs (1.50 KiB/s)
```

**Step 13 — Create a File Locally and Upload It (PUT)**

```bash
ftp> !echo "Hello from client" > client_upload.txt
ftp> put client_upload.txt
```

Expected output:

```text
local: client_upload.txt remote: client_upload.txt
229 Entering Extended Passive Mode (|||40002|).
150 Ok to send data.
226 Transfer complete.
18 bytes sent in 0.01 secs (1.80 KiB/s)
```

**Step 14 — Verify the Transfer on the Server**

On the server terminal:

```bash
ls -la /home/ftpuser/
cat /home/ftpuser/client_upload.txt
```

**Step 15 — Exit FTP**

```bash
ftp> bye
```

### 3.2.5 Analytical Matrix — FTP Command Reference

| Command | Purpose | Example |
|:-------:|:--------|:--------|
| `open`  | Connect to FTP server | `ftp> open 192.168.1.10` |
| `user`  | Send username | `ftp> user ftpuser` |
| `ls`    | List server files | `ftp> ls` |
| `cd`    | Change server directory | `ftp> cd Documents` |
| `get`   | Download single file | `ftp> get file.txt` |
| `mget`  | Download multiple files | `ftp> mget *.txt` |
| `put`   | Upload single file | `ftp> put file.txt` |
| `mput`  | Upload multiple files | `ftp> mput *.txt` |
| `delete`| Delete server file | `ftp> delete old.txt` |
| `mkdir` | Create directory | `ftp> mkdir backup` |
| `binary`| Switch to binary mode | `ftp> binary` |
| `ascii` | Switch to ASCII mode | `ftp> ascii` |
| `!`     | Run local shell command | `ftp> !ls` |
| `bye` / `quit` | Exit FTP | `ftp> bye` |

### 3.2.6 Result
The FTP server was successfully configured using vsftpd. Files were transferred **bidirectionally** between the server and the client using `get` (download) and `put` (upload).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — TELNET Client-Server Architecture with NVT Translation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ClientSide["Client Machine 192.168.1.20"]
        cliUser["User Terminal"]
        cliApp["TELNET Client"]
        cliNVT["NVT Translator"]
        cliOS["Local OS Character Set"]
    end

    subgraph Network["TCP IP Network LAN"]
        tcpLink["Reliable TCP Connection Port 23"]
    end

    subgraph ServerSide["Server Machine 192.168.1.10"]
        srvApp["TELNET Daemon in.telnetd"]
        srvNVT["NVT Translator"]
        srvShell["Remote Shell Bash"]
        srvFS["File System"]
    end

    cliUser -->|"Types ls -la"| cliOS
    cliOS -->|"Local encoding"| cliNVT
    cliNVT -->|"Convert to NVT ASCII"| cliApp
    cliApp -->|"IAC WILL DO Option Negotiation"| tcpLink
    tcpLink -->|"NVT Data Stream"| srvApp
    srvApp -->|"Reverse NVT conversion"| srvNVT
    srvNVT -->|"Server encoding"| srvShell
    srvShell -->|"ls -la output"| srvNVT
    srvNVT -->|"NVT ASCII"| srvApp
    srvApp -->|"TCP Port 23"| tcpLink
    tcpLink -->|"Reply stream"| cliApp
    cliApp -->|"NVT to local"| cliNVT
    cliNVT -->|"Local encoding"| cliOS
    cliOS -->|"Visible output"| cliUser

    srvShell -.->|"Reads/Writes"| srvFS
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — FTP Dual-Channel Architecture (Active Mode)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ClientMachine["FTP Client 192.168.1.20"]
        ftpCli["FTP Client Process"]
        cliCtrlSock["Control Socket Port X greater than 1023"]
        cliDataSock["Data Socket Port Y greater than 1023"]
    end

    subgraph ServerMachine["FTP Server 192.168.1.10"]
        ftpSrv["vsftpd Daemon"]
        srvCtrlSock["Control Socket Port 21"]
        srvDataSock["Data Socket Port 20"]
        srvDisk["Server File System"]
    end

    ftpCli -->|"Step1 USER PASS"| cliCtrlSock
    cliCtrlSock -->|"Persistent Control Channel"| srvCtrlSock
    srvCtrlSock -->|"Authentication Replies 220 230"| ftpCli

    ftpCli -->|"Step2 PORT 192.168.1.20 Y Y"| cliCtrlSock
    cliCtrlSock -->|"Command via Port 21"| srvCtrlSock
    srvCtrlSock -->|"Server notes client data port"| ftpSrv

    ftpCli -->|"Step3 RETR file.txt"| cliCtrlSock
    cliCtrlSock -->|"Command via Port 21"| srvCtrlSock
    srvCtrlSock -->|"150 Ok to send"| ftpSrv

    ftpSrv -->|"Step4 Server INITIATES data connection"| srvDataSock
    srvDataSock -->|"Connects to client port Y"| cliDataSock
    cliDataSock -->|"File bytes flow"| ftpCli
    ftpCli -->|"Saves to local disk"| ftpCli

    srvDataSock -->|"226 Transfer complete"| ftpCli
    ftpCli -->|"Control channel stays open for next command"| cliCtrlSock

    ftpSrv -.->|"Reads/Writes"| srvDisk
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Combined Lab Topology for TELNET + FTP Experiment

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LabLAN["KTU Lab LAN 192.168.1.0/24"]
        server["Server Machine Ubuntu 22.04\nIP 192.168.1.10\nServices TELNET 23 and FTP 21 20"]
        client["Client Machine\nIP 192.168.1.20\nTELNET client and FTP client"]
        switch["Network Switch"]
    end

    client -->|"telnet 192.168.1.10 Remote login"| switch
    client -->|"ftp 192.168.1.10 File transfer"| switch
    switch --> server

    server -->|"Files served via FTP control channel Port 21"| switch
    server -->|"Files transferred via FTP data channel Port 20"| switch
    server -->|"Remote shell via TELNET Port 23"| switch
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural Insight:** Notice how TELNET uses a **single TCP connection (port 23)** for both commands and data, whereas FTP **splits** this into a persistent control channel and an ephemeral data channel. This dual-channel design of FTP is the reason it can transfer files in parallel while still accepting new commands — a feature TELNET lacks.
