## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**Q1. Define a network simulator. List any two popular network simulators used in academic research.**

**Model Answer:**

A **network simulator** is a software tool that models the behavior of a computer network by simulating the interaction of nodes, links, and protocols in a virtual environment, without the need for physical hardware. It uses **discrete-event simulation** to advance time and log packet-level events for analysis.

**Two popular network simulators:**
1. **NS-2 / NS-3** — Discrete-event simulator widely used in academic research.
2. **Cisco Packet Tracer** — Graphical simulator for CCNA-level networking labs.

*(Acceptable alternatives: OMNeT++, GNS3, Mininet, OPNET, NetSim)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q2. Differentiate between a network simulator and a network emulator with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Network Simulator | Network Emulator |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Models network behavior using mathematical/algorithmic representations | Runs actual device firmware/OS in a virtualized environment |
| **Realism** | Lower — synthetic packet flow | Higher — real packet processing |
| **Examples** | NS-2, NS-3, OMNeT++ | GNS3, QEMU-based emulators |
| **Use Case** | Protocol research, scalability studies | Real firmware testing, hybrid labs |
| **Resource Usage** | Low | High |
| **Cost** | Mostly open-source (free) | Often requires licensed images |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse **Packet Tracer** as a pure simulator. In reality, it is a **hybrid tool** because it provides a functional Cisco IOS-like CLI. Mentioning this nuance can earn a **bonus mark**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Module 2, Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 — Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **architecture of NS-2** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of OTcl and C++ in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The NS-2 (Network Simulator Version 2) architecture is a **split-language design** combining C++ and OTcl:

**1. C++ Backend (Compiled Layer):**
- Implements the **core event scheduler** that manages discrete-event execution.
- Defines **network components**: nodes, links, queues, agents, and applications.
- Encodes **protocol logic** (TCP, UDP, AODV, DSR, etc.) for performance.
- Handles **per-packet processing** efficiently using compiled code.

**2. OTcl Frontend (Interpreted Layer):**
- **Object-oriented Tool Command Language** used to *configure simulation scenarios*.
- Allows users to define **topology, traffic, parameters** without recompiling.
- Provides a **rapid prototyping** environment — modify and re-run quickly.

**3. Event Scheduler:**
- Maintains a **priority queue of events** sorted by timestamp.
- At each step, the scheduler picks the event with the smallest timestamp:
$$t_{next} = \min(t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n)$$
- Dispatches the event to the appropriate handler (e.g., a TCP agent processes a packet-arrival event).

**4. Trace Files (.tr):**
- Log every packet event in a structured ASCII format.
- Used for **post-simulation analysis** (throughput, delay, PDR).

**5. NAM (Network Animator):**
- Visualization tool that reads `.nam` files.
- Animates packet flow, queue occupancy, and node movement in real-time playback.

> **Block Diagram:** Refer to the architecture diagram in **Section 4.1** of these notes for the KTU board presentation.

---

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain the **seven steps of performing a network simulation** using NS-2. Write a sample OTcl script to create a 2-node TCP connection and explain each line. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The seven steps are:
1. **Define Objective** — e.g., measure TCP throughput over a 1 Mbps link.
2. **Create Topology** — use `$ns node` to instantiate nodes.
3. **Configure Links** — `$ns duplex-link` to set bandwidth, delay, queue type.
4. **Attach Agents** — `Agent/TCP` and `Agent/TCPSink` to enable transport-layer communication.
5. **Attach Application** — `Application/FTP` to generate traffic.
6. **Schedule Events** — `$ns at <time> "<event>"` to control start/stop.
7. **Run and Analyze** — `$ns run` starts the scheduler; trace file is post-processed.

**Sample OTcl Script:**

```tcl
set ns [new Simulator]
set tf [open out.tr w]
$ns trace-all $tf
set n0 [$ns node]
set n1 [$ns node]
$ns duplex-link $n0 $n1 1Mb 10ms DropTail
set tcp [new Agent/TCP]
set sink [new Agent/TCPSink]
$ns attach-agent $n0 $tcp
$ns attach-agent $n1 $sink
$ns connect $tcp $sink
set ftp [new Application/FTP]
$ftp attach-agent $tcp
$ns at 0.5 "$ftp start"
$ns at 9.5 "$ftp stop"
$ns at 10.0 "finish"
proc finish {} {
    global ns tf
    $ns flush-trace
    close $tf
    exit 0
}
$ns run
```

**Line-by-Line Explanation:**

| Line | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `set ns [new Simulator]` | Creates the scheduler object |
| `set tf [open out.tr w]` | Opens trace file for packet logging |
| `$ns trace-all $tf` | Attaches trace to all links |
| `set n0 [$ns node]` | Creates node $n_0$ |
| `set n1 [$ns node]` | Creates node $n_1$ |
| `$ns duplex-link $n0 $n1 1Mb 10ms DropTail` | 1 Mbps bandwidth, 10 ms delay, DropTail queue |
| `set tcp [new Agent/TCP]` | Creates a TCP agent (sender) |
| `set sink [new Agent/TCPSink]` | Creates a TCP receiver |
| `$ns attach-agent $n0 $tcp` | Attaches TCP to $n_0$ |
| `$ns attach-agent $n1 $sink` | Attaches sink to $n_1$ |
| `$ns connect $tcp $sink` | Establishes connection (binds addresses) |
| `set ftp [new Application/FTP]` | Creates FTP traffic generator |
| `$ftp attach-agent $tcp` | Attaches FTP to the TCP agent |
| `$ns at 0.5 "$ftp start"` | Starts FTP at $t = 0.5$ s |
| `$ns at 9.5 "$ftp stop"` | Stops FTP at $t = 9.5$ s |
| `$ns at 10.0 "finish"` | Terminates simulation at $t = 10.0$ s |
| `$ns run` | Launches the event scheduler |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Adapted]**

**(a)** What are the **different types of network simulators**? Compare **NS-2, NS-3, and Cisco Packet Tracer** on the basis of language, license, scalability, and use case. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Network simulators are classified as:
- **Simulators** (e.g., NS-2, NS-3) — Use mathematical models.
- **Emulators** (e.g., GNS3) — Run real device firmware.
- **Hybrid tools** (e.g., Packet Tracer) — Combine both approaches.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | NS-2 | NS-3 | Cisco Packet Tracer |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Language** | C++ + OTcl | C++ + Python | Proprietary (C++ backend) |
| **License** | Open-source (GPL) | Open-source (GPL) | Free for education |
| **Scalability** | Medium (~1000 nodes) | High (~10,000+ nodes) | Low (~200 devices) |
| **GUI** | External (NAM) | Limited (NetAnim) | Built-in drag-and-drop |
| **Primary Use** | Wired/wireless research | Modern 5G/IoT research | Teaching, CCNA labs |
| **Trace Format** | `.tr` (text) | `.pcap` + ASCII | Internal |
| **Learning Curve** | Steep | Steep | Beginner-friendly |

---

**(b)** Explain the **Packet Tracer Simulation Mode**. How does it help in understanding **OSI layer encapsulation** during a packet transfer between two PCs? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Packet Tracer Simulation Mode:**
- A **non-real-time** mode where packets are sent **one at a time** (step-by-step).
- Allows the user to **inspect every Protocol Data Unit (PDU)** as it traverses the network.
- Provides **layer-wise visibility** of headers and trailers — an invaluable teaching aid.

**How it Helps Understand OSI Encapsulation:**

When PC1 sends a packet to PC2, the Simulation Mode reveals the following:

| OSI Layer | Action Visible in Packet Tracer | Encapsulation Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **7. Application** | FTP/HTTP request generated | Data |
| **6. Presentation** | Data formatted/encrypted | Data |
| **5. Session** | Session established | Data |
| **4. Transport** | TCP/UDP header added | Segment |
| **3. Network** | IP header added (src/dst IP) | Packet / Datagram |
| **2. Data Link** | MAC header + trailer added | Frame |
| **1. Physical** | Bits transmitted as signals | Bits |

**Step-by-Step PDU Movement in Packet Tracer:**
1. User creates a simple ping from PC1 to PC2.
2. Switches Simulation Mode → ON.
3. The PDU envelope appears at PC1 (Layer 7 → Layer 1 encapsulation visible by clicking the envelope).
4. PDU travels through the switch → reaches PC2.
5. At PC2, **de-encapsulation** occurs (Layer 1 → Layer 7).
6. The user can **click the envelope at each step** to see the headers added/stripped.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Lab Record Note:** Always include a screenshot of Simulation Mode with an opened PDU envelope for full marks in the lab record.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Students often **omit the difference between Simulator and Emulator** in (a) — this costs 2 marks easily.
> - In OTcl script questions, **not annotating each line** loses half the marks. The KTU examiner expects **comment-style explanation** for every line of code.
> - For trace-file-based numerical questions, **forgetting the ×8 conversion (bytes to bits)** is a very common mistake — verify units carefully.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — High-Yield Points for KTU 2024 Scheme:**

- ✅ A **network simulator** is a software tool that models network behavior using **discrete-event simulation** without real hardware.
- ✅ **Packet Tracer** is the **default KTU lab simulator**; **NS-2/NS-3** are preferred for research-based experiments.
- ✅ NS-2 uses a **split-language architecture**: **C++** for protocol implementation, **OTcl** for scenario configuration.
- ✅ The **event scheduler** advances simulation time to the smallest-timestamp event:
$$t_{next} = \min(t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n)$$
- ✅ **Trace files (`.tr`)** in NS-2 record every packet event — `+` (enqueue), `-` (dequeue), `r` (receive), `d` (drop).
- ✅ **Throughput formula** — multiply bytes by 8 to get bits before dividing by time.
- ✅ **Packet Tracer Simulation Mode** shows **OSI layer-wise encapsulation** — a guaranteed KTU viva question.
- ✅ **Simulator vs Emulator** — simulator models mathematically, emulator runs real firmware (GNS3 is an emulator; NS-2 is a simulator; Packet Tracer is hybrid).
- ✅ Standard KTU lab simulator output includes: **Throughput (bps)**, **End-to-End Delay (s)**, **Packet Delivery Ratio (%)**, **Jitter (s)**, **Packet Loss Ratio (%)**.
- ✅ Always use **AWK/Python scripts** to extract statistics from NS-2 trace files; visualize using **XGRAPH** or **Gnuplot**.
- ✅ The 7-step simulation workflow: **Objective → Topology → Links → Protocols → Traffic → Schedule → Run & Analyze**.
- ✅ For the **lab record**, document: Objective, Topology Diagram, Code/Script, Output Screenshots, Result Table, and Inference.
